era_context: The Hellenistic world following Alexander the Great's death (323 BCE). The Seleucid Empire controls the Near East and Central Asia; Ptolemaic Egypt dominates the eastern Mediterranean; Macedonian kingdoms fragment and compete. In India, the Mauryan Empire under Ashoka is consolidating power. China is in the Warring States period, approaching unification under Qin. Rome is expanding in Italy but not yet a Mediterranean superpower.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek/Roman contact zones

events.title: Euclid's Elements compiled in Alexandria
events.description: Euclid of Alexandria systematizes Greek mathematics into the Elements, a comprehensive treatise on geometry, number theory, and proportion. This work becomes the foundational mathematical text for over 2,000 years and represents the apex of Hellenistic mathematical rigor. The Elements codify axiomatic method and logical proof, establishing standards for mathematical reasoning that persist into the modern era.

sources.name: Euclid, Elements (surviving manuscripts and later references by Proclus, Pappus)
Proclus, Commentary on the First Book of Euclid's Elements

events.certainty_note: Euclid's floruit is traditionally dated to ~300 BCE based on Proclus's account that he lived under Ptolemy I. No contemporary documentation of the Elements' composition survives; dating rests on historiographic consensus and internal evidence.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya consolidates Mauryan Empire
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya, founder of the Mauryan Empire, continues consolidation of power across the Indian subcontinent. By 300 BCE, the empire extends from the Indus Valley to the Deccan, establishing centralized administration, a standing army, and trade networks. This period marks the emergence of the first pan-Indian state and the beginning of Ashoka's reign (who will succeed him).

sources.name: Arthashastra (attributed to Kautilya; compiled later but reflects Mauryan administration)
Strabo, Geography (Greek account of Mauryan Empire)
Ashoka's edicts (inscribed on stone pillars and rocks)

events.certainty_note: Chandragupta's reign is dated c. 322–298 BCE by most scholars, placing 300 BCE within his rule. Dating relies on synchronisms with Greek sources (Megasthenes' embassy) and later Indian chronicles. Exact chronology remains debated.

events.title: Seleucus I consolidates Seleucid control of the Near East
events.description: Seleucus I Nicator, one of Alexander's generals, solidifies control over the eastern portions of Alexander's empire, establishing the Seleucid dynasty. By 300 BCE, he has defeated rivals (including Antigonus at Ipsus in 301 BCE) and secured dominion over Syria, Mesopotamia, and parts of Anatolia and Central Asia. He founds Antioch as a major administrative and cultural center.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (founder of Seleucid dynasty)
Antigonus I Monophthalmus (rival, defeated at Ipsus)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Ipsus (301 BCE) and Seleucus's consolidation are well-attested in multiple Greek historical sources, though written after the fact. The broad outline is secure.

events.title: Zeno of Citium founds the Stoic school in Athens
events.description: Zeno of Citium establishes Stoicism, a philosophical school emphasizing virtue, reason, and acceptance of fate. He teaches in the Stoa Poikile (Painted Porch) in Athens, giving the school its name. Stoicism becomes one of the most influential philosophical movements in the Hellenistic and Roman worlds, shaping ethics, politics, and personal conduct for centuries.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers

events.certainty_note: Zeno's floruit is traditionally dated to c. 300 BCE, but sources are late (Diogenes Laërtius wrote in the 3rd century CE). The founding of the school is placed around this time by historiographic consensus, though exact dating is uncertain.

events.title: Qin state advances toward unification of China
events.description: The Qin state, under King Zheng (later Qin Shi Huang), continues military expansion and administrative reforms during the late Warring States period. By 300 BCE, Qin is the dominant military power in China, employing legalist philosophy and advanced military tactics. Within a decade, Qin will unify all of China, ending the Warring States period.
key_figures: King Zheng of Qin (later Qin Shi Huang)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Bamboo annals and oracle bone inscriptions

events.certainty_note: Qin's military dominance by 300 BCE is well-attested in the Shiji, though written centuries later. The exact sequence of campaigns and dates within the Warring States period remain debated by scholars.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of Euclid to 300 BCE is not universally accepted; some scholars argue for a slightly later date (c. 290–280 BCE) based on internal evidence and the lack of contemporary documentation. The founding of Stoicism by Zeno is similarly traditional and lacks contemporary corroboration. Chandragupta's exact reign dates (c. 322–298 BCE) are debated, with some scholars proposing alternative chronologies. The significance of Zeno's school in 300 BCE may be overstated; Stoicism's major influence came later, particularly in the Roman period.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 300 BCE is uneven. Greek and Hellenistic history is well-attested through later historiographers (Plutarch, Diodorus, Appian) who drew on earlier sources, though contemporary documentation is sparse. Indian history relies heavily on later compilations (Arthashastra, Ashoka's edicts) and Greek accounts (Strabo, Megasthenes). Chinese history is documented in the Shiji, compiled centuries later but drawing on earlier records. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Southeast Asia have virtually no written sources for this year. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and Near Eastern events; Asian and African developments are underrepresented relative to their actual historical importance.

graph_edges.note: Euclid's Elements precedes and influences Archimedes' mathematical work
Chandragupta's consolidation of Mauryan Empire leads to Ashoka's reign
Seleucus's consolidation follows from his victory at Ipsus in 301 BCE
Qin's dominance in 300 BCE leads to unification in 221 BCE

era_context: The Hellenistic world in the immediate aftermath of Alexander's death (323 BCE). The Diadochi (Alexander's successors) are competing for control of his fragmented empire. The Battle of Ipsus (301 BCE) is a pivotal moment in this struggle, determining the shape of the Hellenistic kingdoms for the next century. Simultaneously, the Mauryan Empire is consolidating in India, and China remains in the Warring States period.

events.title: Battle of Ipsus: Seleucus and Lysimachus defeat Antigonus
events.description: The Battle of Ipsus is fought between the Diadochi (successors of Alexander the Great) competing for control of his empire. Seleucus I and Lysimachus, allied against Antigonus I Monophthalmus and his son Demetrius, defeat their rivals decisively. Antigonus is killed in the battle. The victory reshapes the Hellenistic world: Seleucus gains control of Syria, Mesopotamia, and the eastern territories; Lysimachus secures Anatolia and Thrace; Ptolemy retains Egypt; and Demetrius retains Macedonia and Greece. This battle effectively ends the possibility of reunifying Alexander's empire and establishes the three major Hellenistic kingdoms that will dominate the Mediterranean and Near East for the next two centuries.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Ipsus is one of the best-attested events of the Hellenistic period, described in multiple Greek historical sources. The date (301 BCE) is secure, and the broad outline of the battle and its consequences are well-established.
cross_references: Related to -323-001 (Alexander's death and division of empire)

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya's reign continues; Mauryan expansion
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues his consolidation of the Indian subcontinent. By 301 BCE, the Mauryan Empire has expanded significantly, incorporating much of northern and central India. The empire's administrative structure, military organization, and trade networks are becoming increasingly sophisticated. Chandragupta's reign represents the first major attempt to create a unified Indian state.

sources.name: Megasthenes, Indica (Greek ambassador's account, preserved in later sources)

events.certainty_note: Chandragupta's reign is dated c. 322–298 BCE. The year 301 BCE falls within this period, but no specific events are documented for this exact year. The entry reflects the broader context of Mauryan expansion during this period.

events.title: Demetrius I Poliorcetes retains control of Macedonia and Greece
events.description: Following the Battle of Ipsus, Demetrius I Poliorcetes (the Besieger), son of the defeated Antigonus, retains control of Macedonia and much of Greece. Though his father is killed at Ipsus, Demetrius's military reputation and control of key strongholds allow him to maintain his position. He becomes one of the major Hellenistic rulers and will continue to play a significant role in Mediterranean politics for decades.
events.certainty_note: Demetrius's retention of Macedonia and Greece after Ipsus is well-attested in Greek historical sources.

events.title: Ptolemy I consolidates Ptolemaic Egypt
events.description: Ptolemy I Soter, one of Alexander's generals, continues to consolidate his control over Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean. By 301 BCE, Ptolemy has secured Egypt as his domain and is expanding Ptolemaic influence in the Aegean and Levantine regions. He establishes Alexandria as a major cultural and administrative center, attracting scholars and artists from across the Greek world.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (founder of Ptolemaic dynasty)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's consolidation of Egypt is well-attested, though specific events for 301 BCE are not documented. The entry reflects the broader context of Ptolemaic expansion during this period.

events.title: Qin state continues military expansion in China
events.description: The Qin state, under King Zheng (later Qin Shi Huang), continues its military campaigns to unify China. By 301 BCE, Qin is the dominant military power in the Warring States period, employing advanced tactics and legalist administrative principles. The state is systematically conquering neighboring states and consolidating control over northern and central China.
key_figures: King Zheng of Qin (later Qin Shi Huang)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Qin's military dominance during this period is attested in the Shiji, though specific campaigns for 301 BCE are not clearly documented. The entry reflects the broader context of Qin expansion during the late Warring States period.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Ipsus is sometimes portrayed as a decisive end to the Diadochi wars, but in fact, conflicts continued for decades afterward. Demetrius I remained a major power and continued to challenge the settlement established at Ipsus. The notion that Ipsus 'settled' the Hellenistic world is an oversimplification. Additionally, the exact location of the Battle of Ipsus has been debated by scholars; while Phrygia is the traditional location, some have questioned this identification.
historiographic_note: The year 301 BCE is well-documented for Hellenistic history due to the significance of the Battle of Ipsus, which is described in multiple Greek historical sources (though written centuries later). However, these sources are not contemporary; they were compiled by Plutarch, Diodorus, and Appian in the Roman period. Indian and Chinese history for this year are less well-documented in contemporary sources. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and Near Eastern events; the historical significance of events in India and China may be underrepresented in the available sources.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Ipsus leads to Seleucus's consolidation of the eastern territories
Demetrius retains Macedonia and Greece despite his father's defeat at Ipsus
Ptolemy consolidates Egypt while Ipsus reshapes the Hellenistic world

era_context: The Hellenistic world in the immediate lead-up to the Battle of Ipsus (301 BCE). The Diadochi are maneuvering for advantage, with Antigonus I Monophthalmus and his son Demetrius I Poliorcetes challenging the other successors of Alexander. In India, Chandragupta Maurya continues consolidating the Mauryan Empire. China remains in the Warring States period, with Qin ascending to dominance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Specific events for 302 BCE in most regions

events.title: Antigonus I and Demetrius I mobilize for conflict with Seleucus and Lysimachus
events.description: Antigonus I Monophthalmus and his son Demetrius I Poliorcetes mobilize their forces in preparation for a major confrontation with Seleucus I and Lysimachus. The tensions that will culminate in the Battle of Ipsus (301 BCE) are escalating. Antigonus, aging but still a formidable military commander, seeks to prevent the consolidation of power by his rivals and to maintain his dominion over Anatolia and the eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: No specific events are documented for 302 BCE. This entry reflects the broader context of escalating tensions in the lead-up to Ipsus. The exact sequence and timing of military preparations are not clearly attested in surviving sources.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya's reign continues; administrative consolidation
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues his reign and the consolidation of the Mauryan Empire. By 302 BCE, the empire has expanded significantly across the Indian subcontinent. Administrative structures, military organization, and trade networks are being developed to support the growing empire.
events.certainty_note: Chandragupta's reign is dated c. 322–298 BCE. No specific events are documented for 302 BCE. This entry reflects the broader context of Mauryan consolidation during this period.

events.title: Qin state continues military campaigns in China
events.description: The Qin state, under King Zheng, continues its military expansion during the Warring States period. Qin is systematically conquering neighboring states and consolidating control over northern and central China. The state's military superiority and legalist administrative system are enabling rapid territorial expansion.
key_figures: King Zheng of Qin (later Qin Shi Huang)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: No specific events are documented for 302 BCE in the Shiji. This entry reflects the broader context of Qin expansion during the late Warring States period.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, it should be noted that 302 BCE is poorly documented in surviving sources. The events listed are inferred from the broader historical context rather than from specific contemporary or near-contemporary documentation.
historiographic_note: The year 302 BCE is poorly documented in surviving sources. For Hellenistic history, we have only later compilations (Plutarch, Diodorus) that provide a general narrative of the Diadochi conflicts but do not document specific events for this year. For Indian and Chinese history, contemporary documentation is sparse or absent. The historical record for 302 BCE is thin, and any events listed are reconstructed from broader historical contexts rather than from specific sources. This year exemplifies the challenge of writing year-by-year history for periods with limited documentation.

graph_edges.note: Antigonus and Demetrius's mobilization precedes the Battle of Ipsus

era_context: The Hellenistic world during the Diadochi period, with the major successors of Alexander the Great competing for control of his fragmented empire. Antigonus I Monophthalmus and Demetrius I Poliorcetes are challenging Seleucus I and Lysimachus. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is consolidating the Mauryan Empire. China remains in the Warring States period, with Qin ascending to dominance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most regions lack specific documentation for 303 BCE
disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified. No events are documented for this year.
historiographic_note: The year 303 BCE is essentially undocumented in surviving sources. While the broader Hellenistic, Indian, and Chinese historical contexts are known, no specific events are attested for this particular year. The historical record for 303 BCE is negligible. This year exemplifies the challenge of writing year-by-year history for periods with limited documentation. The absence of documented events does not mean nothing happened; it reflects the limitations of surviving sources.

era_context: The Hellenistic world during the Diadochi period. Antigonus I Monophthalmus and Demetrius I Poliorcetes are major powers, competing with Seleucus I, Lysimachus, and Ptolemy I for control of Alexander's fragmented empire. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is consolidating the Mauryan Empire. China remains in the Warring States period, with Qin ascending to dominance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most regions lack specific documentation for 304 BCE

events.title: Demetrius I Poliorcetes proclaimed king by his father Antigonus
events.description: Antigonus I Monophthalmus proclaims his son Demetrius I Poliorcetes as king, elevating him to royal status. This act is significant as it represents a formal claim to dynastic succession and a challenge to the other Diadochi. Demetrius becomes one of the major Hellenistic rulers and will play a significant role in Mediterranean politics for decades.
events.certainty_note: The date of Demetrius's proclamation as king is traditionally placed around 304 BCE, but this dating is not firmly attested in contemporary sources. Later historians (Plutarch, Diodorus) provide the account, but the exact year is uncertain. Some scholars place this event slightly earlier or later.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Demetrius's proclamation as king is uncertain. While 304 BCE is the traditional date, some scholars have questioned this dating or placed the event in a different year. The significance of this event in the broader context of the Diadochi conflicts is also debated; it may have been more of a symbolic gesture than a major political turning point.
historiographic_note: The year 304 BCE is poorly documented in surviving sources. The only event listed (Demetrius's proclamation as king) is attested in later historical compilations but lacks contemporary documentation. The exact date is uncertain, and the event's significance is debated. For Indian and Chinese history, no specific events are documented for this year. The historical record for 304 BCE is minimal, and any events listed are based on traditional dating from later sources rather than contemporary documentation.

graph_edges.note: Demetrius's proclamation as king precedes the Battle of Ipsus, where his father is defeated

era_context: The Diadochi Wars are in their final phase. Antigonus I and Demetrius I are still powerful, but the coalition against them is solidifying. Ptolemy I, Seleucus I, and Lysimachus are coordinating their efforts to prevent Antigonus from dominating the Mediterranean world. The decisive Battle of Ipsus will occur in 301 BCE. In Greece, the city-states are experiencing the effects of these conflicts. The Mauryan Empire is consolidating its control over India. The Qin state is rising in China. This is a period of intense military and political activity across the known world.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from interior India and China

events.title: Coalition of Ptolemy I, Seleucus I, and Lysimachus against Antigonus I
events.region: Eastern Mediterranean, Mesopotamia, Thrace, Asia Minor
events.description: Ptolemy I, Seleucus I, and Lysimachus formally coordinate their efforts against Antigonus I and Demetrius I. This coalition is motivated by the recognition that Antigonus's dominance threatens the independence of all three. The coalition is not a formal alliance with written treaties, but rather a coordination of military and political efforts. Ptolemy's role is primarily defensive, protecting Egypt from Demetrius's attacks. Seleucus and Lysimachus are preparing for a major military campaign against Antigonus. The coalition sets the stage for the decisive conflict that will occur in 301 BCE.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (founder of Seleucid Empire)
Lysimachus (ruler of Thrace and Asia Minor)
events.certainty_note: The coalition against Antigonus is attested in later sources, and the year 305 BCE is the traditional date for its formal coordination. However, the exact year is not independently confirmed, and the coalition was likely forming gradually over several years.
cross_references: Part of broader Diadochi Wars (-323 to -301)

events.title: Demetrius I besieges Rhodes
events.description: Demetrius I Poliorcetes besieges the island of Rhodes, which has refused to submit to Antigonus's authority. The siege is one of the most famous military operations of the ancient world, lasting for about a year (305–304 BCE). Demetrius employs advanced siege technology and tactics, but the Rhodians resist stubbornly. The siege is ultimately unsuccessful, as Demetrius is forced to lift it due to the need to respond to the coalition forming against him. The siege demonstrates Demetrius's military skill but also the limits of his power.
key_figures: Demetrius I Poliorcetes (Macedonian general, commander of siege)
events.certainty_note: The Siege of Rhodes is well-attested in later sources and is traditionally dated to 305–304 BCE. This is one of the better-documented events of this period.

events.title: Seleucus I establishes the Seleucid Empire and consolidates eastern territories
events.description: Seleucus I formally establishes the Seleucid Empire, consolidating his control over Mesopotamia and the eastern satrapies. He is organizing the empire into provinces, establishing Greek settlements, and building the administrative and military infrastructure that will characterize the Seleucid state. The empire stretches from Mesopotamia to Central Asia and includes parts of Persia and the Indus Valley. Seleucus is also establishing diplomatic relations with the Mauryan Empire in India. The Seleucid Empire is one of the largest and most powerful states in the world at this time.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (founder and emperor of Seleucid Empire)
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's consolidation of the Seleucid Empire occurred over several years in the period 312–305 BCE. The year 305 BCE is traditionally dated for the formal establishment of the empire, but the exact year is not independently confirmed.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya's empire reaches its territorial maximum
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya's empire continues to expand and consolidate. By this period, the Mauryan Empire controls much of the Indian subcontinent, from the Gangetic plain to the Deccan and beyond. The empire is characterized by a sophisticated administrative system, a powerful military, and extensive trade networks. Chandragupta is also known to have had contact with the Hellenistic world, and there are reports of diplomatic exchanges with Seleucus I. The Mauryan Empire is one of the largest and most powerful states in the world at this time, rivaling the Seleucid Empire in size and power.

sources.name: Strabo, Geography (Book 15, citing earlier Greek sources)

events.certainty_note: Chandragupta's reign is traditionally dated 322–298 BCE, but Indian chronology is uncertain. The year 305 BCE falls within his reign, but specific events are not dated to this year in surviving sources. This entry reflects the general context of Mauryan expansion during his rule.
cross_references: Contemporary with Hellenistic conflicts in the West

events.title: Qin state continues rise to dominance in China
events.description: The Qin state continues its military and administrative reforms, gradually gaining dominance over the other Warring States. The state is implementing Legalist philosophy and building the military and bureaucratic apparatus that will eventually unify China. This period sees continued warfare among the Warring States, with Qin gradually gaining advantage through superior organization and military tactics. The state is not yet dominant, but the trajectory is clear.
key_figures: Qin rulers of the period (specific ruler for 305 BCE uncertain)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled c. 109 BCE

events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology of the Warring States period (475–221 BCE) is uncertain, with discrepancies between traditional dating and modern scholarship. The year 305 BCE is assigned by proleptic Gregorian conversion, but the specific events of this year in Qin are not independently documented.
cross_references: Part of broader Warring States period (-475 to -221)

disconfirming_evidence: The coalition against Antigonus is attested in later sources, but the exact year 305 BCE is not independently confirmed. Some scholars have suggested that the coalition was forming gradually over several years rather than being formally established in a single year. The Siege of Rhodes is well-attested and is traditionally dated to 305–304 BCE, but some sources place it slightly earlier or later. The significance of the siege is sometimes overstated; while it was a major military operation, it did not prevent the coalition from eventually defeating Antigonus. Seleucus's consolidation of the Seleucid Empire is attested in later sources, but the exact year 305 BCE is not independently confirmed. For Chandragupta and Qin, the dating is even more uncertain, with traditional chronologies differing from modern reconstructions.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 305 BCE is dependent on later Greek sources (Diodorus, Plutarch, Appian) written centuries after the events. The Siege of Rhodes is one of the better-documented events of this period, but even its dating is not entirely certain. The sources are biased toward political and military history and toward the perspectives of the Macedonian elite. Archaeological evidence from this period is sparse and difficult to date precisely. For India and China, the sources are even more problematic, relying on traditional king-lists and texts compiled centuries later. The experiences of ordinary people, local populations, and non-Greek peoples are largely absent from the record.

graph_edges.note: The coalition against Antigonus led to the Battle of Ipsus
The Siege of Rhodes weakened Demetrius and contributed to his eventual defeat at Ipsus
Seleucus's consolidation of the Seleucid Empire enabled his victory at Ipsus

era_context: The Diadochi Wars are in their final phase before the decisive Battle of Ipsus (301 BCE). Antigonus I and Demetrius I are at the height of their power, but the coalition against them is solidifying. Ptolemy I, Seleucus I, and Lysimachus are coordinating their efforts. In Greece, the city-states are experiencing the effects of these conflicts. The Mauryan Empire is consolidating its control over India. The Qin state is rising in China. This is a period of intense military and political activity across the known world.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from interior India and China

events.title: Battle of Salamis in Cyprus: Demetrius I defeats Ptolemy's fleet
events.description: Demetrius I Poliorcetes defeats the Egyptian fleet commanded by Ptolemy I's admiral in a naval battle off Salamis in Cyprus. This is a major victory for Demetrius and Antigonus, demonstrating their naval superiority and their ability to threaten Ptolemy's control of the eastern Mediterranean. The victory enhances Demetrius's reputation as a military commander and temporarily strengthens Antigonus's position. However, the victory does not translate into lasting strategic advantage, as Ptolemy retains control of Egypt and the coalition against Antigonus continues to strengthen.
key_figures: Demetrius I Poliorcetes (Macedonian general, commander of Antigonus's forces)
Ptolemy I Soter (Egyptian king, defeated in this battle)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Salamis in Cyprus is well-attested in later sources and is traditionally dated to 306 BCE. This is one of the few events of this period with relatively secure dating.
cross_references: Part of broader Diadochi Wars (-323 to -301)
Leads to -301-001 (Battle of Ipsus, where Demetrius is defeated)

events.title: Antigonus I proclaimed king by his supporters
events.description: Following the victory at Salamis, Antigonus I is proclaimed king by his supporters. This is a formal recognition of his power and his ambitions to establish a dynasty. The proclamation is a response to the earlier proclamations of Demetrius, Ptolemy, and other Diadochi as kings. Antigonus is now in his eighties but remains the dominant military and political figure among the Diadochi. However, the proclamation also crystallizes the opposition against him, as the other Diadochi see his kingship as a threat to their own independence.
events.certainty_note: Antigonus's proclamation as king is attested in later sources and is traditionally dated to 306 BCE, following the Battle of Salamis. However, the exact year is not independently confirmed.

events.title: Seleucus I and Lysimachus prepare for conflict with Antigonus
events.description: Seleucus I and Lysimachus are preparing their forces for a major conflict with Antigonus. They are coordinating their military strategies and gathering resources for the campaign that will culminate in the Battle of Ipsus in 301 BCE. Ptolemy I is also preparing his forces, though his role will be primarily defensive. The preparations are extensive, involving the mobilization of troops, the organization of supply lines, and the coordination of military movements across vast distances.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (Macedonian general, founder of Seleucid Empire)
Lysimachus (Macedonian general, ruler of Thrace and Asia Minor)
events.certainty_note: The preparations for the conflict with Antigonus are attested in later sources, but the specific year 306 BCE is not independently confirmed. This entry reflects the general context of the period leading up to the Battle of Ipsus.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya possibly encounters Seleucus I through diplomatic contact
events.description: There are reports in later Greek sources (particularly Strabo) of diplomatic contact between Chandragupta Maurya and Seleucus I. The exact nature and timing of this contact is uncertain, but it represents one of the earliest known interactions between the Hellenistic world and the Indian subcontinent. The contact may have involved the exchange of ambassadors and the establishment of trade relations. This interaction is significant for the history of cross-cultural exchange, though the details are sparse and often legendary.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (Macedonian general, founder of Seleucid Empire)

sources.name: Strabo, Geography (Book 15, citing earlier Greek sources)

events.certainty_note: The contact between Chandragupta and Seleucus is attested in later Greek sources, but the exact year is uncertain. The year 306 BCE is assigned by convention, but some sources place this contact earlier or later. The details of the contact are often legendary or exaggerated.
cross_references: Part of broader history of Hellenistic-Indian contact

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Salamis in Cyprus is well-attested and is traditionally dated to 306 BCE, but some modern scholars have questioned whether the battle occurred in this year or in 307 BCE. The significance of the battle is sometimes overstated; while it was a major naval victory for Demetrius, it did not prevent the coalition against Antigonus from forming or from eventually defeating him. The proclamation of Antigonus as king is attested in later sources, but the exact year is uncertain. The contact between Chandragupta and Seleucus is reported in later Greek sources, but the details are often legendary or exaggerated, and the exact year is highly uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 306 BCE is dependent on later Greek sources (Diodorus, Plutarch, Appian) written centuries after the events. The Battle of Salamis is one of the better-attested events of this period, but even its dating is not entirely certain. The sources are biased toward political and military history and toward the perspectives of the Macedonian elite. Archaeological evidence from this period is sparse and difficult to date precisely. The contact between Chandragupta and Seleucus is reported in later Greek sources, but these sources are often unreliable regarding Indian affairs. The experiences of ordinary people, local populations, and non-Greek peoples are largely absent from the record.

graph_edges.note: The victory at Salamis prompted Antigonus's proclamation as king
The victory at Salamis temporarily strengthened Antigonus but ultimately led to his defeat at Ipsus
Seleucus and Lysimachus's preparations led to the Battle of Ipsus

era_context: The Diadochi Wars are approaching their climax. Antigonus I and Demetrius I remain powerful, but the coalition against them is solidifying. Ptolemy I, Seleucus I, and Lysimachus are increasingly coordinating their efforts to prevent Antigonus from dominating the Mediterranean world. In Greece, the city-states are experiencing the effects of these conflicts. The Mauryan Empire continues to expand in India. The Qin state is rising in China. This is a period of transition and consolidation across the known world.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from interior India and China

events.title: Demetrius I proclaimed king by his father Antigonus I
events.description: Antigonus I formally proclaims his son Demetrius I as king, elevating him from general to monarch. This is a significant political move, as it represents the first formal claim to kingship among the Diadochi since Alexander's death. The proclamation is both a recognition of Demetrius's military achievements and a statement of Antigonus's ambitions to establish a dynasty. However, the move also provokes the other Diadochi, who see it as a threat to the balance of power. This event marks a turning point in the Diadochi Wars, as it crystallizes the opposition against Antigonus.
events.certainty_note: The proclamation of Demetrius as king is attested in later sources, and the year 307 BCE is the traditional date given in modern scholarship. However, the exact year is not independently confirmed in contemporary sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Diadochi Wars (-323 to -301)

events.title: Ptolemy I proclaimed king by his supporters in Egypt
events.description: Following Antigonus's proclamation of Demetrius as king, Ptolemy I is proclaimed king by his supporters in Egypt. This is a reciprocal move, establishing Ptolemy as a legitimate monarch rather than merely a satrap. The proclamation solidifies Ptolemy's control over Egypt and establishes the Ptolemaic dynasty. It also represents a formal break with the fiction of a unified Macedonian empire under a regent, as the Diadochi are now openly establishing themselves as independent kings.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (proclaimed king of Egypt)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's proclamation as king is attested in later sources, and the year 307 BCE is the traditional date. However, some sources suggest the proclamation may have occurred slightly earlier or later.

events.title: Seleucus I and Lysimachus formalize alliance against Antigonus
events.description: Seleucus I and Lysimachus formalize their alliance against Antigonus I. This alliance is a crucial development in the Diadochi Wars, as it brings together two of the most powerful Diadochi against Antigonus. The alliance is motivated by the recognition that Antigonus's dominance threatens the independence of both Seleucus and Lysimachus. Ptolemy I is also part of this coalition, though his role is primarily naval and defensive. The alliance sets the stage for the decisive conflict that will occur in 301 BCE.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (Macedonian general, founder of Seleucid Empire)
Lysimachus (Macedonian general, ruler of Thrace and Asia Minor)
events.certainty_note: The alliance between Seleucus and Lysimachus is attested in later sources, but the exact year is uncertain. The year 307 BCE is assigned by convention within the broader chronology of the Diadochi Wars.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya's empire reaches its height
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya's empire continues to expand and consolidate. By this period, the Mauryan Empire controls much of the Indian subcontinent, from the Gangetic plain to the Deccan. The empire is characterized by a sophisticated administrative system, a powerful military, and extensive trade networks. Chandragupta is also known to have had contact with the Hellenistic world, and there are reports of diplomatic exchanges with Seleucus I. The Mauryan Empire is one of the largest and most powerful states in the world at this time.

sources.name: Strabo, Geography (Book 15, citing earlier Greek sources)

events.certainty_note: Chandragupta's reign is traditionally dated 322–298 BCE, but Indian chronology is uncertain. The year 307 BCE falls within his reign, but specific events are not dated to this year in surviving sources.
cross_references: Contemporary with Hellenistic conflicts in the West

disconfirming_evidence: The year 307 BCE is traditionally dated for the proclamations of Demetrius and Ptolemy as kings, but modern scholarship has questioned whether these proclamations occurred simultaneously or in different years. Some sources suggest the proclamations may have been separated by a year or more. Additionally, the significance of these proclamations is sometimes overstated; they were formal recognitions of power that had already been exercised for years. The alliance between Seleucus and Lysimachus is attested in later sources, but the exact timing is uncertain. For the Mauryan Empire, the dating is even more uncertain, with traditional chronologies differing from modern reconstructions.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 307 BCE is dependent on later Greek sources (Diodorus, Plutarch, Appian) written centuries after the events. These sources are biased toward political and military history and toward the perspectives of the Macedonian elite. The chronology of this period is uncertain, and individual years lack precise documentation. Archaeological evidence from this period is sparse and difficult to date precisely. The experiences of ordinary people, local populations, and non-Greek peoples are largely absent from the record. For India and China, the sources are even more problematic, relying on traditional king-lists and texts compiled centuries later.

graph_edges.note: Demetrius's proclamation as king prompted Ptolemy's reciprocal proclamation
Demetrius's proclamation as king prompted the alliance against Antigonus
The Seleucus-Lysimachus alliance led to the Battle of Ipsus

era_context: Continuation of the Diadochi Wars. Antigonus I and Demetrius I remain the dominant military power in the Eastern Mediterranean, but the coalition against them is solidifying. Ptolemy I controls Egypt and is expanding his influence in the Aegean and Levant. Seleucus I is consolidating the eastern satrapies. Lysimachus controls Thrace and parts of Asia Minor. In Greece, the city-states are caught between these powers. The Mauryan Empire continues to expand in India. The Qin state is rising in China.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from interior India and China

events.title: Demetrius I Poliorcetes besieges or campaigns in the Aegean and eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Demetrius I, known as 'Poliorcetes' (the Besieger) for his siege warfare expertise, is conducting military campaigns to consolidate Antigonus's control over the Aegean and threaten Ptolemy's holdings. He is building a powerful navy and conducting sieges of fortified cities. His military reputation is growing, and he is becoming one of the most formidable commanders of the age. These campaigns are part of the broader struggle for control of the Mediterranean world following Alexander's death.
key_figures: Demetrius I Poliorcetes (Macedonian general, son of Antigonus I)
events.certainty_note: Demetrius's campaigns in this period are attested in later sources, but the specific year 308 BCE is not independently confirmed. The chronology is reconstructed from the narrative arc of the Diadochi Wars.
cross_references: Part of broader Diadochi Wars (-323 to -301)

events.title: Ptolemy I Soter consolidates control of Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Ptolemy I, having secured Egypt as his satrapy after Alexander's death, is consolidating his control and expanding his influence in the eastern Mediterranean. He is building a powerful navy to counter Antigonus and Demetrius, and he is establishing Egypt as a major Hellenistic power. Ptolemy is also beginning to gather cultural and intellectual resources, laying the groundwork for Alexandria's later prominence as a center of learning. His strategy is to maintain Egypt's independence and wealth while preventing any single rival from dominating the Mediterranean.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (Macedonian general, founder of Ptolemaic Egypt)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's consolidation of Egypt is well-attested in the literary tradition, but specific events in 308 BCE are not independently dated.

events.title: Seleucus I continues eastward expansion and consolidation
events.description: Seleucus I is expanding his control eastward, establishing the foundations of the Seleucid Empire. He is organizing the eastern satrapies, establishing Greek settlements, and building the administrative and military infrastructure that will characterize the Seleucid state. This expansion is gradual and faces resistance from local powers, but Seleucus's superior military organization and resources are enabling him to consolidate control. He is also beginning to establish diplomatic relations with Indian powers, including the Mauryan Empire.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (Macedonian general, founder of Seleucid Empire)
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's eastward expansion occurred over several years in the period 312–305 BCE. The year 308 BCE is assigned by convention, but specific events are not independently documented.

events.title: Lysimachus consolidates control of Thrace and western Asia Minor
events.description: Lysimachus, one of Alexander's generals, is consolidating his control over Thrace and western Asia Minor. He is building a powerful kingdom that rivals Antigonus, Ptolemy, and Seleucus in resources and military strength. Lysimachus is also beginning to position himself as a potential ally against Antigonus, recognizing that Antigonus's dominance threatens the balance of power among the Diadochi.
key_figures: Lysimachus (Macedonian general, ruler of Thrace and Asia Minor)
events.certainty_note: Lysimachus's consolidation of his kingdom is attested in later sources, but specific events in 308 BCE are not independently dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The year 308 BCE is not independently documented in surviving sources. The events listed are reconstructed from the broader narrative of the Diadochi Wars, which is itself subject to chronological uncertainty. Modern scholarship has identified inconsistencies in the dating of events in this period, with some scholars placing key developments a year or two earlier or later. The significance of individual years in the Diadochi Wars is often overstated in historical narratives; the conflicts were continuous and fluid rather than neatly divided by year.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 308 BCE is entirely dependent on later Greek sources (Diodorus, Plutarch, Appian) written centuries after the events. These sources are biased toward political and military history and toward the perspectives of the Macedonian elite. The experiences of ordinary people, local populations, and non-Greek peoples are largely absent from the record. The chronology of this period is uncertain, and individual years lack precise documentation. Archaeological evidence from this period is sparse and difficult to date precisely.

graph_edges.note: Demetrius's campaigns and Ptolemy's consolidation were simultaneous and opposed
Seleucus's consolidation enabled his alliance against Antigonus
Lysimachus's consolidation enabled his alliance against Antigonus

era_context: The Hellenistic world in the aftermath of Alexander the Great's death (323 BCE). The Diadochi Wars continue as Alexander's generals compete for control of his fragmented empire. In the Eastern Mediterranean, Antigonus I Monophthalmus and his son Demetrius I Poliorcetes are consolidating power in Macedonia and the Aegean. Egypt is under Ptolemy I Soter, who has secured the richest satrapy. The Seleucid Empire is being formed under Seleucus I Nicator in Mesopotamia and the East. In Greece proper, city-states navigate the new political landscape. In India, the Mauryan Empire under Chandragupta is expanding. In China, the Qin state is rising toward unification.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from interior India beyond Mauryan heartland

events.title: Battle of Ipsus preparations and Antigonus I's dominance in the Aegean
events.description: Antigonus I Monophthalmus, now in his eighties, commands the most powerful military force among the Diadochi. He and his son Demetrius I control Macedonia, much of Greece, and the Aegean fleet. This year marks the height of Antigonus's power before the coalition against him solidifies. Demetrius is conducting campaigns to consolidate Aegean control and threaten Egypt. The tension between Antigonus and the other Diadochi (Ptolemy, Seleucus, Lysimachus) is escalating toward the decisive Battle of Ipsus in 301 BCE.
key_figures: Antigonus I Monophthalmus (Macedonian general, regent of Alexander's empire)
Demetrius I Poliorcetes (son of Antigonus, military commander)
events.certainty_note: Events of 309 BCE are not individually dated in surviving sources with precision. This reconstruction is based on the chronological framework of the Diadochi Wars as reconstructed by modern scholarship, particularly the narrative arc leading to Ipsus (301 BCE). The year is assigned by convention within the broader conflict.

events.title: Seleucus I consolidates control of Babylon and the eastern satrapies
events.description: Seleucus I Nicator, having secured Babylon after the Battle of the Hydaspes (309 BCE, though some sources place this slightly earlier), is consolidating his control over Mesopotamia and extending his authority eastward. He is establishing the administrative and military foundations of what will become the Seleucid Empire. This period sees him organizing satrapies, recruiting Greek settlers, and beginning the process of Hellenization in the East. His control of the wealthy Mesopotamian heartland gives him resources to challenge Antigonus.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (Macedonian general, founder of Seleucid Empire)
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's consolidation of Babylon occurred in the period 312–309 BCE. The exact year 309 BCE is not independently confirmed for specific administrative actions, but this represents the general trajectory of his power consolidation during this period.
cross_references: Part of broader Diadochi Wars (-323 to -301)

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya's empire expands in the Indian subcontinent
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya, founder of the Mauryan Empire, continues to expand his dominion across northern and central India. By this period, he has defeated the Nanda dynasty and is consolidating control over the Gangetic plain and surrounding regions. The Mauryan state is becoming the dominant power in India, with a sophisticated administrative system, military organization, and economic infrastructure. This expansion is contemporary with the Hellenistic conflicts in the West, though the two worlds remain largely separate.
key_figures: Chandragupta Maurya (founder and emperor of Mauryan Empire)

sources.name: Arthashastra (attributed to Kautilya/Chanakya, compiled later but describing Mauryan administration)
Strabo, Geography (Book 15, citing earlier Greek sources)
Buddhist and Jain texts (Pali Canon, Jain scriptures)

events.certainty_note: Chandragupta's reign is traditionally dated 322–298 BCE, but Indian chronology of this period is uncertain. The year 309 BCE falls within his reign, but specific events are not dated to this year in surviving sources. This entry reflects the general context of Mauryan expansion during his rule.
cross_references: Contemporary with Hellenistic conflicts in the West
Part of broader Indian history (-322 to -298)

events.title: Qin state consolidates power in China during the Warring States period
events.description: The Qin state, located in the northwest of China, continues its military and administrative reforms under its rulers. By the early 4th century BCE, Qin has implemented the Legalist philosophy and is building the military and bureaucratic apparatus that will eventually unify China. This period sees continued warfare among the Warring States, with Qin gradually gaining advantage through superior organization and military tactics. The state is not yet dominant, but the trajectory is clear.
key_figures: Qin rulers of the period (specific ruler for 309 BCE uncertain; likely during reign of a mid-4th century BCE Qin lord)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled c. 109 BCE

events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology of the Warring States period (475–221 BCE) is uncertain, with discrepancies between traditional dating and modern scholarship. The year 309 BCE is assigned by proleptic Gregorian conversion, but the specific events of this year in Qin are not independently documented. This entry reflects the general context of Qin's rise during this era.
cross_references: Part of broader Warring States period (-475 to -221)

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of the Diadochi Wars is reconstructed from later sources (Diodorus, Plutarch, Appian) written centuries after the events. Modern scholarship has identified inconsistencies and gaps in these accounts. The precise dating of events in 309 BCE is particularly uncertain; some scholars place key battles and political developments a year or two earlier or later. Additionally, the significance attributed to Antigonus's power in this year must be tempered by the fact that his coalition was already forming against him—his dominance was more apparent than real. For Chandragupta and Qin, the dating is even more uncertain, with traditional Indian and Chinese chronologies differing from modern reconstructions based on astronomical data and archaeological evidence.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 309 BCE is heavily dependent on Greek sources written by later historians, creating a Western-centric bias. The Diadochi Wars are well-attested in the literary tradition, but individual years lack precise documentation. For India and China, the sources are even more problematic: Indian chronology relies on traditional king-lists and Buddhist texts compiled centuries later, while Chinese chronology depends on the Shiji, which was compiled over 200 years after the events it describes. Archaeological evidence from this period is sparse and difficult to date precisely. The surviving sources are biased toward political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are poorly documented. Entire regions (sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, Mesoamerica) are absent from the historical record for this year.

graph_edges.note: Antigonus's dominance in 309 BCE provoked the coalition that defeated him at Ipsus in 301 BCE
Seleucus's consolidation of Babylon enabled his alliance against Antigonus
Antigonus and Seleucus were rivals during this period

era_context: The Diadochi Wars continue with the peace agreement of 311 BCE holding, though tensions remain. Seleucus I is consolidating his control over the eastern satrapies. Ptolemy I maintains control of Egypt and the Levantine coast. Cassander controls Macedon and Greece. Antigonus I and Demetrius Poliorcetes remain militarily powerful in Asia Minor and the eastern Mediterranean. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin ascendant. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is consolidating the Mauryan Empire.

events.title: Demetrius Poliorcetes defeats Ptolemaic forces in Cyprus
events.description: Demetrius Poliorcetes, continuing his campaigns in the eastern Mediterranean, defeats Ptolemaic forces in Cyprus. This victory consolidates Antigonid control of the island and demonstrates Demetrius's military superiority in naval warfare. The victory is part of Demetrius's broader campaign to expand Antigonid influence in the eastern Mediterranean at the expense of Ptolemy I.
events.certainty_note: Demetrius's campaigns in Cyprus are attested in Hellenistic sources, though specific events in 310 BCE are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Cassander consolidates control of Macedon and Greece
events.description: Cassander continues to consolidate his control over Macedon and the Greek city-states. He faces ongoing challenges from Antigonus and Demetrius, but maintains his position as the dominant power in the Macedonian homeland. Cassander's consolidation of power in Macedon is crucial for the eventual coalition victory against Antigonus.
events.certainty_note: Cassander's consolidation of power in Macedon is attested in Hellenistic sources, though specific events in 310 BCE are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Seleucus I establishes the Seleucid administrative system
events.description: Seleucus I continues to establish and refine the administrative system that will govern the Seleucid Empire. He establishes a network of cities, military garrisons, and administrative centers to control his vast territory. This administrative system is based on Macedonian models but adapted to the conditions of the eastern satrapies. The establishment of this system is crucial for the long-term stability and success of the Seleucid Empire.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's establishment of administrative systems is attested in Hellenistic sources, though specific events in 310 BCE are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Qin state continues expansion in the Warring States period
events.description: The Qin state continues its military expansion during the Warring States period. The Qin's legalist administrative system and military innovations continue to give it an advantage over rival states. This period is part of the long process that will culminate in Qin unification of China in 221 BCE.
key_figures: Qin leadership (specific ruler uncertain for this year)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The Qin's expansion during the Warring States period is well-documented in Chinese historiography, but specific events in 310 BCE are not clearly attested.
cross_references: Part of Warring States period (-475 to -221)

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya's reign continues in India
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues to consolidate and expand the Mauryan Empire. The empire is becoming increasingly centralized and powerful, extending from the Indus Valley to the Deccan Plateau. Chandragupta's reign represents a major turning point in Indian history, establishing a model of centralized imperial rule that will influence Indian politics for centuries.

sources.name: Mauryan inscriptions and later Buddhist texts

events.certainty_note: The chronology of Chandragupta's reign is uncertain. Indian sources do not provide precise dating for specific years.
cross_references: Part of Mauryan Empire period (-322 to -185)

disconfirming_evidence: The specific events attributed to 310 BCE in the Diadochi Wars are not always clearly attested in the sources. Some events that are dated to 310 BCE in some sources are dated to 311 BCE or 309 BCE in others. The significance of Demetrius's victories in Cyprus is debated; while they demonstrate his military skill, they did not prevent the eventual coalition victory against Antigonus. The chronology of Chandragupta's reign remains uncertain, and some scholars place specific events in his reign at different dates.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 310 BCE is less detailed than for some other years in the Diadochi Wars period. The sources are still centuries removed from the events and may reflect biases toward military narratives. Information about India and China remains sparse in Western sources and relies on later compilations. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean world and the Diadochi Wars; developments in other regions are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Demetrius's victories in Cyprus were part of the broader Antigonid strategy that eventually led to confrontation with the coalition at Ipsus
Seleucus's administrative consolidation continued during the period leading up to the Battle of Ipsus

era_context: The Diadochi Wars continue with the coalition against Antigonus I consolidating. Seleucus I is establishing his control over the eastern satrapies. Ptolemy I maintains control of Egypt and the Levantine coast. Cassander controls Macedon and Greece. Lysimachus controls Thrace. Antigonus I remains militarily powerful but increasingly isolated. In China, the Warring States period continues. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is consolidating the Mauryan Empire.

events.title: Peace agreement among Diadochi
events.description: A peace agreement is reached among the Diadochi, temporarily halting the wars. The agreement recognizes the territorial divisions that have emerged: Ptolemy controls Egypt and the Levantine coast, Seleucus controls the eastern satrapies, Cassander controls Macedon and Greece, and Lysimachus controls Thrace. Antigonus I and his son Demetrius are recognized as controlling Asia Minor and parts of the eastern Mediterranean. This peace is temporary and will be broken within a few years, but it represents an important moment of stabilization in the Diadochi Wars.
events.certainty_note: A peace agreement among the Diadochi is attested in Hellenistic sources, though the precise date and terms are somewhat uncertain. Some sources place this agreement in 311 BCE, others in 310 BCE.

events.title: Seleucus I consolidates control of the eastern satrapies
events.description: Following his invasion of Babylon in 312 BCE, Seleucus I continues to consolidate his control over the eastern satrapies. He establishes administrative systems and military garrisons to secure his territory. This consolidation is crucial for the establishment of the Seleucid Empire, which will become one of the largest Hellenistic kingdoms. Seleucus's success in the east allows him to maintain his position as one of the most powerful Diadochi.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's consolidation of the eastern satrapies is attested in Hellenistic sources, though specific events in 311 BCE are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Demetrius Poliorcetes besieges Salamis in Cyprus
events.description: Demetrius Poliorcetes undertakes a siege of the city of Salamis in Cyprus, which is held by Ptolemaic forces. The siege is part of Demetrius's broader campaign to expand Antigonid control in the eastern Mediterranean. The siege is eventually successful, and Demetrius captures the city, consolidating Antigonid control of Cyprus.
events.certainty_note: The siege of Salamis is attested in Hellenistic sources, though the precise date is somewhat uncertain. Some sources place it in 311 BCE, others in 310 BCE.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya's reign continues in India
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues to consolidate and expand the Mauryan Empire. He is establishing a centralized administrative system and military organization. The Mauryan Empire is becoming one of the largest empires in the ancient world, extending from the Indus Valley to the Deccan Plateau.

sources.name: Mauryan inscriptions and later Buddhist texts

events.certainty_note: The chronology of Chandragupta's reign is uncertain. Indian sources do not provide precise dating for specific years.
cross_references: Part of Mauryan Empire period (-322 to -185)

disconfirming_evidence: The peace agreement among the Diadochi in 311 BCE is disputed in terms of its exact date and terms. Some sources place it in 310 BCE. Additionally, the significance of this peace agreement is debated; some historians argue that it was more of a temporary truce than a genuine settlement, and that the underlying tensions among the Diadochi remained unresolved. The siege of Salamis is sometimes dated to 310 BCE rather than 311 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 311 BCE remains dependent on Hellenistic sources that are centuries removed from the events. The peace agreement among the Diadochi is attested in multiple sources, but the precise terms and significance are somewhat uncertain. Information about India and China remains sparse in Western sources and relies on later compilations.

graph_edges.note: The peace agreement of 311 BCE was temporary and eventually broke down, leading to renewed conflict and the Battle of Ipsus in 301 BCE
Seleucus's consolidation of the eastern satrapies continued during the period leading up to the Battle of Ipsus

era_context: A pivotal year in the Diadochi Wars. Antigonus I remains the dominant military power, but a coalition of other Diadochi is beginning to form against him. Seleucus I, with Egyptian support from Ptolemy I, invades Babylon and begins his rise to power. This year marks a turning point in the struggle for Alexander's legacy. In China, the Warring States period continues. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is consolidating the Mauryan Empire.

events.title: Seleucus I invades Babylon with Egyptian support
events.description: Seleucus I Nicator, with military support from Ptolemy I of Egypt, invades Babylon and defeats the Antigonid garrison. This invasion marks a major turning point in the Diadochi Wars. Seleucus's successful conquest of Babylon allows him to consolidate control over the eastern satrapies and establish the foundation of the Seleucid Empire. The invasion is coordinated with other Diadochi who are beginning to form a coalition against Antigonus's dominance. This event is traditionally dated to 312 BCE and marks the beginning of the Seleucid era in later chronologies.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (Egyptian satrap, supporter of Seleucus)
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's invasion of Babylon in 312 BCE is well-attested in multiple Hellenistic sources and is accepted by modern scholarship with high confidence. The date 312 BCE is used as the epoch for the Seleucid era in later chronologies.

events.title: Coalition of Diadochi forms against Antigonus
events.description: Following Seleucus's invasion of Babylon, a coalition of Diadochi begins to form against Antigonus I, who has become too dominant militarily. Ptolemy I, Seleucus I, Cassander, and Lysimachus coordinate their efforts to limit Antigonus's power. This coalition will eventually lead to the Battle of Ipsus in 301 BCE, where Antigonus will be defeated and killed. The formation of this coalition marks a shift in the Diadochi Wars from a period of Antigonid dominance to a more balanced struggle for power.
events.certainty_note: The formation of a coalition against Antigonus is attested in Hellenistic sources, though the precise timing and coordination of the coalition in 312 BCE is not entirely clear from the sources.

events.title: Demetrius Poliorcetes continues campaigns in the Levant
events.description: Demetrius Poliorcetes, son of Antigonus I, continues military campaigns in the Levant to consolidate Antigonid control. Despite the formation of a coalition against his father, Demetrius remains militarily successful in this period. His campaigns demonstrate the military superiority of the Antigonid forces, though they cannot ultimately prevent the eventual coalition victory at Ipsus.
events.certainty_note: Demetrius's campaigns in the Levant during this period are attested in Hellenistic sources, though specific events in 312 BCE are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Qin state continues military expansion in China
events.description: The Qin state continues its military expansion during the Warring States period. The Qin's legalist administrative system and military innovations continue to give it an advantage over rival states. This period is part of the long process that will culminate in Qin unification of China in 221 BCE.
key_figures: Qin leadership (specific ruler uncertain for this year)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The Qin's expansion during the Warring States period is well-documented in Chinese historiography, but specific events in 312 BCE are not clearly attested.
cross_references: Part of Warring States period (-475 to -221)

disconfirming_evidence: While Seleucus's invasion of Babylon in 312 BCE is well-attested, some modern scholars question whether this single event was as decisive in determining the outcome of the Diadochi Wars as traditional historiography suggests. The coalition against Antigonus did not form immediately after Seleucus's invasion; it took several years for the coalition to solidify and coordinate effectively. Additionally, Antigonus remained militarily dominant for several more years after 312 BCE, suggesting that Seleucus's invasion, while significant, did not immediately shift the balance of power.
historiographic_note: The year 312 BCE is one of the best-documented years in the Diadochi Wars period, with multiple sources attesting to Seleucus's invasion of Babylon. However, these sources are still centuries removed from the events and may reflect biases toward military narratives. The date 312 BCE was later adopted as the epoch for the Seleucid era, which may have influenced how later historians dated events in this period. Information about China and India remains sparse in Western sources and relies on later compilations of Chinese and Indian texts.

graph_edges.note: Seleucus's invasion of Babylon in 312 BCE led directly to the formation of a coalition against Antigonus, which culminated in the Battle of Ipsus in 301 BCE
The coalition of Diadochi formed in 312 BCE defeated Antigonus at Ipsus in 301 BCE

era_context: Continuation of the Diadochi Wars in the Hellenistic world. Antigonus I remains militarily dominant in the eastern Mediterranean following his victory at Gaza. Seleucus is consolidating power in Babylon and the eastern satrapies. Ptolemy I controls Egypt and the Levantine coast. Cassander maintains control of Macedon and Greece. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin expansion. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is consolidating the Mauryan Empire.

events.title: Antigonus I campaigns in Asia Minor
events.description: Following his victory at Gaza, Antigonus I Monophthalmus conducts campaigns in Asia Minor to consolidate control over the region and suppress rival Diadochi. These campaigns are part of his broader strategy to dominate the eastern Mediterranean and Near East. His military success in this period makes him the most powerful of the Diadochi, though his ultimate ambition to reunify Alexander's empire will ultimately fail.
events.certainty_note: Antigonus's campaigns in Asia Minor during this period are attested in Hellenistic sources, though specific events in 313 BCE are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Demetrius Poliorcetes besieges Tyre
events.description: Demetrius Poliorcetes, son of Antigonus I, undertakes a major siege of the city of Tyre, which is held by Ptolemaic forces. The siege is one of the most famous military operations of the Diadochi Wars, demonstrating Demetrius's military genius and earning him the epithet 'Poliorcetes' (the Besieger). The siege lasts several months and involves innovative siege machinery and tactics. Tyre eventually falls to Demetrius, consolidating Antigonid control of the Levantine coast.
key_figures: Demetrius Poliorcetes (Macedonian general, son of Antigonus I)
events.certainty_note: The siege of Tyre is well-attested in Hellenistic sources. Some sources date it to 313 BCE, others to 312 BCE. The date 313 BCE is accepted by many modern scholars, though there is some uncertainty.

events.title: Ptolemy I consolidates control of Egypt and the Levantine coast
events.description: Despite setbacks in the Diadochi Wars, Ptolemy I Soter consolidates his control over Egypt and maintains a strong presence in the Levantine coast. He establishes the Ptolemaic dynasty, which will rule Egypt for three centuries. His strategy of maintaining a strong navy and controlling key coastal cities allows him to resist Antigonus's dominance and eventually emerge as one of the most successful Diadochi.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (Macedonian general, founder of Ptolemaic Egypt)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's consolidation of power in Egypt is well-attested in Hellenistic sources, though specific events in 313 BCE are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya consolidates the Mauryan Empire in India
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya, founder of the Mauryan Empire, continues to consolidate his control over the Indian subcontinent. He has recently defeated the Nanda dynasty and is establishing a centralized administrative system. The Mauryan Empire will become one of the largest empires in ancient history, extending from the Indus Valley to the Deccan Plateau. Chandragupta's reign marks a major turning point in Indian history, establishing a model of centralized imperial rule.

sources.name: Mauryan inscriptions and later Buddhist texts

events.certainty_note: The chronology of Chandragupta's reign is uncertain. Indian sources do not provide precise dating, and the relationship between Indian and Greek chronologies is debated. Chandragupta's reign is traditionally dated to c. 322-298 BCE, placing 313 BCE within his consolidation period.
cross_references: Part of Mauryan Empire period (-322 to -185)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the siege of Tyre is disputed among modern scholars. Some sources place it in 312 BCE rather than 313 BCE. Additionally, the significance of Demetrius's victory at Tyre in determining the outcome of the Diadochi Wars is debated; some historians argue that control of coastal cities, while militarily impressive, did not translate into lasting political dominance. The chronology of Chandragupta's reign is highly uncertain, and some scholars place his consolidation of power earlier or later than 313 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 313 BCE remains dominated by Hellenistic sources, particularly Diodorus Siculus and Plutarch. These sources are centuries removed from the events and may reflect biases toward military narratives and the achievements of famous generals. Information about India comes from later compilations and Buddhist texts, which are not contemporary with the events. Chinese sources provide information about the Warring States period but are also compiled centuries after the events. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean world and the Diadochi Wars; developments in other regions are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: The siege of Tyre and Seleucus's invasion of Babylon occurred around the same time, representing a turning point in the Diadochi Wars
Antigonus's campaigns in Asia Minor eventually led to his confrontation with the coalition of other Diadochi at Ipsus

era_context: The Hellenistic world in the aftermath of Alexander the Great's death (323 BCE). The Diadochi Wars continue as Alexander's generals compete for control of his fragmented empire. In the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East, Seleucus, Antigonus, Lysimachus, Cassander, and Ptolemy are consolidating regional power. The Greek city-states remain politically fragmented. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin ascendant. In India, the Mauryan Empire is consolidating under Chandragupta.

events.title: Battle of Gaza
events.description: Antigonus I Monophthalmus and his son Demetrius Poliorcetes defeat Ptolemy I's forces under Seleucus and Demetrius of Phaleron in a major naval and land engagement. This victory consolidates Antigonus's control over the Levantine coast and demonstrates the military superiority of the Antigonid faction in the ongoing Diadochi Wars. The battle is a turning point in the struggle for Alexander's legacy, though Antigonus's ultimate dominance would prove temporary.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica (Library of History), Book 19

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in Hellenistic historiography, though our sources (Diodorus, Plutarch) are from the 1st century CE, centuries after the event. The date 314 BCE is accepted by modern scholarship with high confidence, though some sources place it in 313 BCE.
cross_references: Part of broader Diadochi Wars (-323 to -301)

events.title: Seleucus I consolidates control of Babylon
events.description: Following his defeat at Gaza, Seleucus I Nicator retreats to Babylon, where he consolidates his power base. He begins the systematic organization of the eastern satrapies that will form the core of the Seleucid Empire. This period marks the beginning of Seleucus's rise from a defeated general to one of the most powerful Diadochi, eventually controlling the largest territorial inheritance from Alexander's empire.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (Macedonian general, future founder of Seleucid Empire)
events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Seleucus's power in Babylon is attested in multiple sources, though the precise chronology of events in 314 BCE is somewhat uncertain. Modern scholarship places this period as the foundation of Seleucid power.

events.title: Cassander strengthens control of Macedon and Greece
events.description: Cassander, son of Antipater, consolidates his position in Macedon and extends his influence over the Greek city-states. He faces opposition from Antigonus and Demetrius but maintains control of the Macedonian homeland. This period sees the continued suppression of Greek autonomy and the establishment of Cassander's dynasty in Macedon, which will rule until the rise of Philip V.
events.certainty_note: Cassander's consolidation of power in this period is well-attested in Hellenistic sources, though specific events in 314 BCE are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Qin state continues expansion in China
events.region: China (Qin territory, modern Shaanxi and Sichuan)
events.description: During the Warring States period, the Qin state under its leadership continues military campaigns to expand territory and consolidate power. The Qin's legalist administrative system and military innovations give it an advantage over rival states. This period is part of the long process that will culminate in Qin unification of China in 221 BCE.
key_figures: Qin leadership (specific ruler uncertain for this year)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Warring States period chronicles and bamboo texts

events.certainty_note: The Qin's expansion during the Warring States period is well-documented in Chinese historiography, but specific events in 314 BCE are not clearly attested. This entry reflects the broader context of Qin ascendancy.
cross_references: Part of Warring States period (-475 to -221)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Gaza's date is sometimes placed in 313 BCE rather than 314 BCE in different scholarly sources, reflecting uncertainty in Hellenistic chronology. Some modern historians question the precise significance attributed to this battle in determining the ultimate outcome of the Diadochi Wars, noting that Antigonus's victory here did not prevent his eventual defeat at Ipsus in 301 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 314 BCE is dominated by Hellenistic sources, particularly Diodorus Siculus and Plutarch, both writing centuries after the events. These sources are themselves drawing on earlier Hellenistic historians (now lost), creating a chain of transmission that introduces potential distortions. The narrative is heavily focused on the Diadochi Wars and the Mediterranean world; information about China, India, and other regions is sparse or absent from Western sources. Chinese sources (Shiji) provide information about Qin expansion but are also compiled centuries after the events. The surviving record is biased toward military and political events involving major figures; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Antigonus's victory at Gaza prompted Seleucus to seek Egyptian support, leading to his invasion of Babylon in 312 BCE
Seleucus's consolidation in Babylon eventually led to his dominance in the eastern territories, confirmed at the Battle of Ipsus
Cassander's consolidation of Macedon occurred during the same period as the broader Diadochi Wars

era_context: The Diadochi Wars are entering a new phase. Antigonus dominates the eastern Mediterranean but faces growing opposition from a coalition of rivals. Seleucus is in Egypt with Ptolemy, planning his return. Cassander controls Macedonia and Greece. Lysimachus controls Thrace. The balance of power is shifting as the rivals begin to coordinate against Antigonus. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is consolidating his empire. This is a period of political maneuvering and the formation of alliances that will lead to the major battles of the next few years.

events.title: Coalition forms against Antigonus; Seleucus, Ptolemy, Cassander, and Lysimachus coordinate
events.description: Recognizing the threat posed by Antigonus's dominance, Seleucus (now in Egypt with Ptolemy), Ptolemy I, Cassander, and Lysimachus began to coordinate their efforts against him. This coalition was motivated by the fear that Antigonus would eventually dominate all of Alexander's former empire. The coordination of these rivals marked a crucial turning point in the Diadochi Wars. The coalition would eventually lead to the Battle of Ipsus in 301 BCE, where Antigonus would be defeated and killed.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (in Egypt, planning return)
events.certainty_note: The formation of a coalition against Antigonus is attested in ancient sources, though the exact date and the precise nature of the coordination are somewhat uncertain. The year 315 BCE is conventional for marking the beginning of this coalition.

events.title: Antigonus campaigns in the Levant and Phoenicia
events.description: Antigonus, seeking to consolidate his control and counter the growing coalition against him, conducted military campaigns in the Levant and Phoenicia. These campaigns were aimed at securing his control of the wealthy coastal regions and preventing Ptolemy from expanding his influence.
