However, Antigonus's campaigns were increasingly challenged by the coordinated efforts of his rivals.
events.certainty_note: Antigonus's campaigns in the Levant during this period are attested, but the exact date and details are somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya consolidates control over northern India
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continued to consolidate his control over northern India, establishing the administrative and military structures of the Mauryan Empire. By 315 BCE, he had secured control over much of the Ganges Valley and was beginning to expand southward. His empire was becoming one of the largest and most powerful in the ancient world, rivaling the Hellenistic kingdoms in the west in terms of territorial extent and population.
events.certainty_note: Chandragupta's consolidation of power in northern India by 315 BCE is attested in Indian sources and Greek sources, though the exact chronology is debated.
cross_references: Contemporary with Diadochi Wars in the west

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the coalition's formation is debated. Some sources suggest it was more gradual, with the rivals coordinating informally before any formal alliance. The degree to which the coalition was unified and coordinated is also disputed; some scholars argue that the rivals were primarily pursuing their own interests rather than acting as a unified force. Additionally, the significance of Antigonus's Levantine campaigns is sometimes overstated in later sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 315 BCE remains dependent on later compilations. The sources are shaped by the interests of later historians and compilers, and the narrative is often simplified or dramatized. The focus remains heavily on the Macedonian and Greek world, with less attention to events in Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the Levant. Indian sources continue to be absent from the Greek and Roman historical tradition. The sources also tend to emphasize military and political events while largely ignoring economic, social, and cultural developments. The separation of the Hellenistic and Indian narratives continues, with no cross-cultural sources available.

graph_edges.note: Coalition formed in response to Antigonus's dominance
Coalition coordination led to Seleucus's return and victory
Coalition eventually defeated Antigonus at the Battle of Ipsus
Chandragupta's consolidation led to his encounter with Seleucus

era_context: The Diadochi Wars are reaching a critical phase. Cassander dominates Macedonia and Greece. Antigonus controls much of Asia Minor and is the most powerful military force in the eastern Mediterranean. Seleucus holds Babylon but is increasingly threatened by Antigonus. Ptolemy I remains secure in Egypt and the Levant. Eumenes of Cardia, once a major player, is besieged and facing defeat. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is consolidating his empire. This is a period of military consolidation and the emergence of clear power blocs.

events.title: Eumenes of Cardia executed; Antigonus consolidates power in Asia Minor
events.description: Eumenes of Cardia, besieged in the fortress of Nora by Antigonus's forces, was forced to surrender. He was executed by Antigonus, eliminating one of the most capable commanders of the Diadochi era. Eumenes's death marked a major consolidation of power by Antigonus, who now dominated Asia Minor and was the most powerful military force in the eastern Mediterranean. This event was a turning point in the Diadochi Wars, as it removed one of the few commanders capable of challenging Antigonus.
events.certainty_note: Eumenes's execution in 316 BCE is well-attested in ancient sources. Plutarch's Life of Eumenes provides a detailed account of his final days.
cross_references: Leads to -312-001 (Seleucus's response to Antigonus's growing power)

events.title: Seleucus expelled from Babylon by Antigonus
events.description: Antigonus, now the dominant military power in the eastern Mediterranean, moved to consolidate his control by expelling Seleucus from Babylon. Seleucus, who had held Babylon since 323 BCE, was forced to flee. He initially sought refuge with Ptolemy I in Egypt, where he would remain for several years before returning to reclaim Babylon and build his empire. This expulsion was a major setback for Seleucus but ultimately led to his alliance with Ptolemy and other rivals of Antigonus.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's expulsion from Babylon by Antigonus in 316 BCE is well-attested in ancient sources.

events.title: Antigonus becomes dominant power in eastern Mediterranean
events.description: By 316 BCE, Antigonus Monophthalmus had emerged as the dominant military and political power in the eastern Mediterranean. His victories over Eumenes and Seleucus, combined with his control of Asia Minor and much of the Levant, made him the most powerful of the Diadochi. However, his dominance would soon provoke a coalition of rivals—Seleucus, Ptolemy, Cassander, and Lysimachus—who would unite against him. This dominance was the peak of Antigonus's power; within a few years, the coalition would challenge and ultimately defeat him.
events.certainty_note: Antigonus's dominance by 316 BCE is well-attested in ancient sources.
cross_references: Follows from -316-001 and -316-002 (victories over Eumenes and Seleucus)

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Seleucus's expulsion from Babylon is sometimes placed in 315 BCE rather than 316 BCE in different sources. Additionally, some scholars debate the degree to which Antigonus's dominance was as complete as the sources suggest; he faced significant challenges in consolidating control over all the territories he claimed. The narrative of Antigonus as the dominant power may be somewhat exaggerated in later sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 316 BCE remains dependent on later compilations, particularly Diodorus Siculus. The sources are shaped by the interests of later historians and compilers. The narrative tends to focus on military and political events while largely ignoring economic, social, and cultural developments. The sources also tend to emphasize the actions of major military figures while downplaying the experiences of ordinary people. The separation of the Hellenistic and Indian narratives continues, with no cross-cultural sources available.

graph_edges.note: Eumenes's execution resulted from his defeat and siege by Antigonus
Seleucus's expulsion reversed his consolidation of Babylon
Antigonus's dominance provoked a coalition against him

era_context: The Diadochi Wars continue with increasing intensity. Cassander is consolidating power in Macedonia and Greece, while Antigonus dominates Asia Minor. Polyperchon's authority is waning. In Egypt, Ptolemy I remains secure. In Mesopotamia, Seleucus holds Babylon. In India, Chandragupta is consolidating the Mauryan Empire. This is a period of military campaigns, political consolidation, and the gradual emergence of the major Hellenistic kingdoms that will dominate the next century.

events.title: Cassander consolidates power in Macedonia; Polyperchon's decline
events.description: By 317 BCE, Cassander had effectively consolidated control over Macedonia and much of Greece, marginalizing Polyperchon. Cassander's superior military resources, strategic alliances (particularly with Antigonus), and control of key strongholds gave him the advantage over Polyperchon. This marked a major shift in the balance of power in the Macedonian world. Cassander would remain the dominant figure in Macedonia for the next two decades, though he would face challenges from other Diadochi.
events.certainty_note: Cassander's consolidation of power by 317 BCE is well-attested in ancient sources. The date marks a clear turning point in the Diadochi Wars.

events.title: Antigonus defeats Eumenes; Eumenes retreats to Nora
events.description: Antigonus Monophthalmus, one of the most capable military commanders of the era, defeated Eumenes of Cardia in a series of campaigns in Asia Minor. Eumenes, despite his military skill, was outmaneuvered by Antigonus's superior resources and the defection of his own troops. Eumenes was forced to retreat to the fortress of Nora in Cappadocia, where he would be besieged. This campaign marked a major victory for Antigonus and a turning point in the struggle for control of Asia Minor.
events.certainty_note: The general outline of Antigonus's victory over Eumenes and Eumenes's retreat to Nora is attested in ancient sources. The exact date is sometimes placed in 318 or 316 BCE in different sources, but 317 BCE is a common dating.

events.title: Ptolemy I defeats Antigonus's forces in the Levant
events.description: Ptolemy I, seeking to expand and secure his control of the Levantine coast, defeated forces sent by Antigonus to challenge his authority in the region. This victory consolidated Ptolemy's control of the Levant and demonstrated his military capability. Ptolemy's strategy of securing a defensible territorial base in Egypt and the Levant, rather than pursuing control of the entire former Alexandrian empire, was proving successful.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's military activities in the Levant during this period are attested, but the exact date and details of specific battles are sometimes uncertain. The year 317 BCE is approximate for this campaign.
cross_references: Contemporary with Antigonus's campaigns in Asia Minor

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of Cassander's consolidation is debated. Some sources suggest the process was more gradual, with Polyperchon retaining some authority longer than others indicate. The dating of Antigonus's victory over Eumenes is sometimes placed in 318 or 316 BCE rather than 317 BCE. Additionally, the significance of Ptolemy's Levantine campaigns is sometimes overstated in later sources; the actual military impact may have been more limited than the narrative suggests.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 317 BCE remains dependent on later compilations. The sources are shaped by the interests of later historians and compilers, and the narrative is often simplified or dramatized. The focus remains heavily on the Macedonian and Greek world, with less attention to events in Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the Levant. Indian sources continue to be absent from the Greek and Roman historical tradition. The sources also tend to emphasize military and political events while largely ignoring economic, social, and cultural developments.

graph_edges.note: Cassander's consolidation resulted from his military campaigns against Polyperchon
Antigonus's victory led to Eumenes's siege at Nora and eventual execution
Ptolemy's consolidation of the Levant made him a major power at the Battle of Ipsus

era_context: The Diadochi Wars are in full swing. Polyperchon, the new regent of Macedonia, is attempting to consolidate power against Cassander and Antigonus. In Egypt, Ptolemy I continues to strengthen his position. In Mesopotamia, Seleucus holds Babylon. In Greece, the city-states are caught between the competing ambitions of the Macedonian generals. This is a period of intense military activity, shifting alliances, and the gradual fragmentation of Alexander's empire into competing Hellenistic kingdoms.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese sources may exist but are underrepresented)

events.title: Polyperchon campaigns in Greece; conflict with Cassander
events.description: Polyperchon, seeking to consolidate his authority as regent, launched military campaigns in Greece against Cassander, who controlled much of Macedonia and had the support of Antigonus. Polyperchon issued a decree freeing Greek cities from Macedonian garrisons, attempting to win popular support. However, Cassander's superior resources and military skill gradually turned the tide against Polyperchon. This campaign marked the beginning of Polyperchon's decline and Cassander's rise to dominance in Macedonia and Greece.
events.certainty_note: The general outline of Polyperchon's campaigns in Greece in 318 BCE is attested in ancient sources, though specific details and dates are sometimes uncertain. The decree freeing Greek cities is mentioned in Diodorus but its exact date and scope are debated.

events.title: Eumenes of Cardia campaigns in Asia Minor
events.description: Eumenes of Cardia, a trusted general of Alexander and now a supporter of Polyperchon, conducted military campaigns in Asia Minor against Antigonus. Eumenes was one of the most capable commanders of the era, but he was ultimately outmaneuvered by Antigonus's superior resources and the defection of his own troops. These campaigns were part of the broader struggle for control of the eastern portions of Alexander's empire.
key_figures: Eumenes of Cardia (general, supporter of Polyperchon)
events.certainty_note: Eumenes's campaigns in Asia Minor during this period are well-attested, though the exact chronology and specific battles are sometimes uncertain in the sources.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya expands control in northern India
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya, founder of the Mauryan Empire, was in the process of consolidating control over northern India during this period. He had recently overthrown the Nanda dynasty and was expanding his power base in the Ganges Valley. By 318 BCE, he was establishing the administrative and military structures that would make the Mauryan Empire one of the largest and most powerful empires of the ancient world. This expansion was contemporary with the Diadochi Wars in the west but occurred in a largely separate sphere.

sources.name: Arthashastra (attributed to Kautilya), composed during or shortly after Chandragupta's reign
Strabo, Geography, Book 15 (citing earlier Greek sources)

events.certainty_note: The chronology of Chandragupta's rise is debated among scholars. Traditional Indian sources place his reign at different dates; the dating to circa 322–298 BCE is based on Greek sources and is now widely accepted, but some scholars place his rise slightly earlier or later. The year 318 BCE is approximate for his consolidation of power.
cross_references: Contemporary with Diadochi Wars in the west

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of Polyperchon's campaigns and their outcomes is disputed. Some sources suggest he was more successful initially than others indicate. The relationship between Polyperchon's decree freeing Greek cities and the actual military outcomes is unclear—it may have been propaganda rather than a genuine policy shift. Additionally, the dating of Eumenes's campaigns is sometimes placed in 317 BCE rather than 318 BCE in different scholarly reconstructions.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 318 BCE remains dependent on later compilations, particularly Diodorus Siculus and Plutarch. For the Hellenistic world, these sources derive from earlier historians now lost. For India, the sources are even more problematic: the Arthashastra is traditionally attributed to Kautilya but was likely compiled over a longer period, and its exact date is uncertain. Greek sources on India (Strabo, Plutarch) are based on reports from Alexander's campaign and later Greek-Indian contact, filtered through multiple layers of transmission. The Indian sources themselves (Puranas, Buddhist texts) are difficult to date precisely and contain legendary material alongside historical information. The separation of the Hellenistic and Indian narratives in our sources reflects a real geographical and cultural divide, but it also means that the global context of 318 BCE is fragmented across incompatible source traditions.

graph_edges.note: Direct consequence of Polyperchon's appointment as regent
Eumenes's campaigns led to his eventual defeat and execution
Chandragupta's consolidation of power led to his encounter with Seleucus

era_context: The Hellenistic world in transition following Alexander the Great's death (323 BCE). The Diadochi Wars are fragmenting his empire among competing generals. In Greece, the Lamian War has recently concluded (322 BCE). In Egypt, Ptolemy I is consolidating control. In Mesopotamia and the Levant, Seleucus is beginning his rise to power. In India, the Mauryan Empire under Chandragupta is expanding. This is an era of military upheaval, dynastic consolidation, and the emergence of the Hellenistic kingdoms that will dominate the Mediterranean and Near East for three centuries.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek/Roman contact zones
East Asia (Chinese sources exist but may be underrepresented in Western training data)

events.title: Polyperchon appointed regent of Macedonia; conflict with Antigonus
events.description: Following the death of Antipater (319 BCE), Polyperchon was named regent of Macedonia and guardian of Alexander's young son Philip Arrhidaeus and half-brother Heracles. This decision, made by Antipater's son Cassander's rival, triggered immediate conflict. Antigonus Monophthalmus, satrap of Phrygia, refused to recognize Polyperchon's authority, initiating a major phase of the Diadochi Wars. This succession dispute would dominate Macedonian and Greek politics for the next decade.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica (Library of History), Book 18

events.certainty_note: The succession of Polyperchon and the outbreak of conflict with Antigonus are well-attested in multiple ancient sources, though all are later compilations. The date 319 BCE is standard in modern scholarship.

events.title: Seleucus consolidates control of Babylon
events.description: Seleucus, who had been appointed satrap of Babylon at the division of Alexander's empire in 323 BCE, was briefly expelled by Antigonus in 316 BCE but had returned and was consolidating his power base. By 319 BCE, Babylon was firmly under his control, providing him with the wealth and administrative infrastructure necessary to build his future empire. Control of Babylon was strategically crucial, as it was the richest satrapy and the administrative heart of the eastern Hellenistic world.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus's control of Babylon by this period is well-established, though the exact timeline of his consolidation is somewhat fluid in the sources. The date 319 BCE is conventional for marking his secure position.
cross_references: Part of broader Diadochi Wars (-323 to -301)

events.title: Ptolemy I secures Egypt and the Levantine coast
events.description: Ptolemy I, who had taken Egypt as his satrapy in 323 BCE, was by 319 BCE firmly established as the dominant power in Egypt and had extended his control to include Cyprus, Phoenicia, and parts of the Levantine coast. His strategy of consolidating a defensible territorial base rather than pursuing Alexander's former empire made him one of the most successful of the Diadochi. Egypt's wealth and the natural barriers of the desert provided him with security against rivals.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's control of Egypt and expansion into the Levant by 319 BCE is well-documented in ancient sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Antipater's death and Polyperchon's appointment is sometimes placed at 320 BCE rather than 319 BCE in different ancient sources. Diodorus places it in the year following Alexander's death (which he dates to 323 BCE), but the precise calendar year remains slightly uncertain. Additionally, some modern scholars debate the degree to which Polyperchon was truly 'appointed' versus simply assuming power, and whether his authority was ever genuinely recognized beyond Macedonia proper.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 319 BCE is derived almost entirely from later compilations (Diodorus, Plutarch, Justin, Appian) written 300–500 years after the events. No contemporary primary sources survive. The sources themselves derive from earlier Hellenistic historians (Hieronymus of Cardia, Manetho) whose works are now lost. This creates a significant filter: the narrative is shaped by the interests and biases of later compilers, and details are often conflated or simplified. The focus is heavily on the Macedonian and Greek world; events in Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the Levant are recorded primarily insofar as they affected the Diadochi Wars. Indian and Chinese events of this period are entirely absent from these sources.

graph_edges.note: Polyperchon's appointment triggered immediate military campaigns against Antigonus and Cassander
Direct consequence of Alexander's death and the division of his empire
Seleucus's secure base in Babylon enabled his major victories in 312 BCE
Ptolemy's consolidation of Egypt and the Levant made him a major power at the Battle of Ipsus

era_context: The third year following Alexander's death. The First War of the Diadochi ends with the defeat and death of Perdiccas. The Hellenistic kingdoms are beginning to stabilize. The Mauryan Empire continues consolidating power in India. Ptolemaic Egypt is establishing itself as a major Hellenistic power. The Seleucid Empire is beginning to take shape in Mesopotamia and the Near East.

events.title: Death of Perdiccas; First War of the Diadochi ends
events.description: Perdiccas, regent for Alexander's heirs, is defeated and killed while attempting to invade Egypt. His death marks the end of the First War of the Diadochi and the failure of his attempt to maintain unified control over Alexander's empire. Following Perdiccas' death, the Hellenistic kingdoms become more clearly defined and stable. The death of Perdiccas is a turning point in the Wars of the Diadochi, leading to a period of relative stability before the Second War of the Diadochi (319–315 BCE).
events.certainty_note: Perdiccas' death is well-attested in ancient sources. The date of 320 BCE is secure.

events.title: Triparadisus Conference: Repartition of Alexander's empire
events.description: Following Perdiccas' death, the Diadochi meet at Triparadisus in Syria to repartition Alexander's empire. Antipater is appointed regent for Alexander's heirs, replacing Perdiccas. The satrapies are redistributed among the Diadochi. Seleucus receives Babylon, Lysimachus receives Thrace, Ptolemy retains Egypt, and Antigonus receives Phrygia and other territories. This repartition establishes a more stable division of power among the Diadochi and sets the stage for the Second War of the Diadochi.
events.certainty_note: The Triparadisus Conference is well-attested in ancient sources. The date of 320 BCE is secure.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya consolidates Mauryan Empire; defeats remaining rivals
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues his consolidation of the Mauryan Empire, defeating remaining rivals and extending Mauryan control across northern India. By 320 BCE, the Mauryan Empire is the dominant power in the Indian subcontinent. The consolidation of power is aided by Kautilya's strategic advice and the use of military force and diplomacy.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Chandragupta's consolidation is uncertain. Modern scholarship places the completion of his consolidation of northern India around 320 BCE, though some sources give slightly different dates.

events.title: Antipater becomes regent for Alexander's heirs; consolidates Macedonian power
events.description: Antipater, the veteran Macedonian general who had served as regent of Macedon during Alexander's campaigns, is appointed regent for Alexander's heirs following Perdiccas' death. Antipater consolidates Macedonian power and maintains control over Greece. His appointment marks a shift in the balance of power among the Diadochi, with Antipater becoming the dominant figure in the Hellenistic world.
events.certainty_note: Antipater's appointment as regent is well-attested in ancient sources. The date of 320 BCE is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Perdiccas' death is traditionally given as 320 BCE, but some sources give slightly different dates. The significance of the Triparadisus Conference has been debated by modern scholars; some argue that it was less significant than traditionally portrayed and that the repartition was less stable than it appeared. The chronology of Chandragupta's consolidation is highly uncertain; some sources place the completion of his consolidation in 321 BCE or 319 BCE rather than 320 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 320 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Hellenistic historians writing decades or centuries after the events. Indian sources on Chandragupta are even more distant from the events they describe. The sources are influenced by the perspectives of the victors and by later political narratives. Archaeological evidence for this year is sparse. The exact chronology of the Wars of the Diadochi is reconstructed through a combination of ancient sources, some of which contradict each other. Western scholarship has traditionally privileged Greek sources and the Hellenistic world.

graph_edges.note: Perdiccas' death leads to the Triparadisus Conference
The Triparadisus repartition leads to the Second War of the Diadochi
Antipater's consolidation of power leads to the Second War of the Diadochi

era_context: The second year following Alexander's death. The Wars of the Diadochi continue. The Lamian War has ended, and Macedonian hegemony over Greece is reasserted. The Mauryan Empire continues consolidating power in India. Ptolemaic Egypt is establishing itself as a major Hellenistic power. The Seleucid Empire is beginning to take shape in Mesopotamia and the Near East.

events.title: Mauryan Empire consolidates control over northern India; Chandragupta expands territory
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues his rapid expansion of the Mauryan Empire across northern India. By 321 BCE, the Mauryan Empire controls much of the Gangetic plain and surrounding regions. The consolidation of power is aided by Kautilya's strategic advice and the use of espionage and diplomacy. The Mauryan Empire is becoming the dominant power in the Indian subcontinent, rivaling the Hellenistic powers in the west.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Chandragupta's expansion is uncertain. Modern scholarship places key consolidation events between 322–320 BCE, with 321 BCE being a reasonable approximation for the ongoing expansion phase.

events.title: First War of the Diadochi continues; Perdiccas attempts to consolidate power
events.description: The First War of the Diadochi (322–320 BCE) continues as Perdiccas attempts to maintain his authority as regent and consolidate control over the fragmented empire. The war involves multiple fronts and competing claims to power. Perdiccas' position is weakening as other Diadochi, particularly Ptolemy and Antigonus, challenge his authority. The war will ultimately result in Perdiccas' defeat and death in 320 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The First War of the Diadochi is well-attested in ancient sources. The date is secure within ±1 year.

events.title: Ptolemy I consolidates control of Egypt; establishes Ptolemaic administration
events.description: Ptolemy I Soter continues to consolidate his control over Egypt, establishing a stable Ptolemaic administration. He integrates Greek and Egyptian administrative structures and establishes Alexandria as a major center of Hellenistic culture and learning. Ptolemy's consolidation of Egypt makes it one of the most stable and prosperous of the Hellenistic kingdoms.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (founder of Ptolemaic dynasty)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's consolidation of Egypt is well-attested. The date is secure within ±1 year.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of Chandragupta's expansion is highly uncertain; some sources place key events in 322 BCE or 320 BCE rather than 321 BCE. The exact sequence and timing of events in the First War of the Diadochi is debated by modern scholars. Some sources give slightly different dates for key military engagements.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 321 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Hellenistic historians writing decades or centuries after the events. Indian sources on Chandragupta are even more distant from the events they describe. The sources are influenced by the perspectives of the victors and by later political narratives. Archaeological evidence for this year is sparse. The exact chronology of the Wars of the Diadochi is reconstructed through a combination of ancient sources, some of which contradict each other.

graph_edges.note: Chandragupta's consolidation of power leads to his later encounter with Seleucus I
The First War of the Diadochi continues and ends with Perdiccas' defeat in 320 BCE

era_context: The year following Alexander's death. The Wars of the Diadochi are in their early stages. The Lamian War in Greece ends with the defeat of the Greek city-states. The Mauryan Empire continues consolidating power in India. Ptolemaic Egypt is establishing itself as a major Hellenistic power. The Seleucid Empire is beginning to take shape in Mesopotamia and the Near East.

events.title: Battle of Crannon: Macedonian forces defeat Greek city-states in the Lamian War
events.description: The Lamian War ends with the Battle of Crannon, where Macedonian forces under Antipater and Craterus defeat the Greek city-states led by Leosthenes. The battle marks the end of Greek city-state independence and the reassertion of Macedonian hegemony over Greece. Following the battle, Athens and other city-states are forced to accept Macedonian rule. Demosthenes, the famous Athenian orator who supported the revolt, commits suicide to avoid capture. The battle is a decisive victory for Macedon and demonstrates the military superiority of the Macedonian system over the traditional Greek polis.
key_figures: Leosthenes (Athenian general, killed in the war)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Crannon is well-attested in ancient sources. The date of 322 BCE is secure.

events.title: Demosthenes commits suicide; end of Athenian independence
events.description: Following the defeat at Crannon, Demosthenes, the famous Athenian orator and opponent of Macedonian rule, flees Athens and takes refuge in the temple of Poseidon on the island of Aegina. Facing capture by Macedonian forces, he commits suicide by drinking poison. His death symbolizes the end of Athenian independence and the triumph of Macedonian power. Demosthenes is remembered as a defender of Greek freedom against Macedonian expansion.
events.certainty_note: Demosthenes' death is well-attested in ancient sources. The date of 322 BCE is secure.

events.title: Perdiccas consolidates power as regent; First War of the Diadochi begins
events.description: Perdiccas, appointed regent for Alexander's heirs, attempts to consolidate his power and maintain control over the fragmented empire. However, his authority is challenged by other Diadochi, particularly Ptolemy, Antigonus, and Lysimachus. The First War of the Diadochi (322–320 BCE) begins as the generals compete for supremacy. Perdiccas attempts to reassert control over Egypt, which Ptolemy has secured, but is ultimately defeated and killed in 320 BCE.
events.certainty_note: Perdiccas' consolidation of power and the beginning of the First War of the Diadochi are well-attested. The date is secure within ±1 year.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya continues expansion; consolidates Mauryan Empire
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues his consolidation of power in northern India, expanding Mauryan control over the Gangetic plain and surrounding regions. The Mauryan Empire is rapidly becoming the dominant power in the Indian subcontinent. The exact chronology of Chandragupta's expansion is uncertain, but by 322 BCE the consolidation of northern territories was well advanced.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Chandragupta's expansion is uncertain. The exact year of various consolidation events is debated by modern scholars.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Battle of Crannon is traditionally given as 322 BCE, but some scholars have questioned whether the battle was as decisive as traditionally portrayed. Some modern historians argue that Greek city-states retained more autonomy and influence than previously thought. The chronology of Perdiccas' regency and the First War of the Diadochi is complex and debated; some sources give slightly different dates for key events. The chronology of Chandragupta's expansion is highly uncertain, with different sources placing key events in different years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 322 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Hellenistic historians writing decades or centuries after the events. Indian sources on Chandragupta are even more distant from the events they describe. The sources are influenced by the perspectives of the victors and by later political narratives. Archaeological evidence for this year is sparse. The exact chronology of the Wars of the Diadochi is reconstructed through a combination of ancient sources, some of which contradict each other. Western scholarship has traditionally privileged Greek sources and the Hellenistic world.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Crannon leads to Demosthenes' suicide
The Battle of Crannon occurs during the First War of the Diadochi
Perdiccas' attempt to consolidate power leads to his defeat and death in 320 BCE

era_context: The year of Alexander the Great's death in Babylon (June 323 BCE) and the immediate aftermath. This marks the end of the Hellenistic conquest period and the beginning of the Wars of the Diadochi (succession wars). The Mauryan Empire continues consolidating power in India. Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the Levant are transitioning from unified Macedonian rule to fragmented Hellenistic kingdoms. Greece itself is experiencing the Lamian War as city-states attempt to reassert independence.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese sources are sparse for this specific year)

events.title: Death of Alexander the Great in Babylon
events.description: Alexander III of Macedon dies in Babylon on June 10 or 11, 323 BCE, at approximately 32 years of age. The cause of death is uncertain—ancient sources cite fever, poisoning, or complications from a wound. His death triggers the immediate fragmentation of his empire and the beginning of the Wars of the Diadochi. Alexander's body is claimed by Ptolemy I and transported to Egypt, becoming a symbol of legitimacy for the Ptolemaic dynasty. The death of Alexander marks the end of the Hellenistic conquest period and the beginning of the Hellenistic kingdoms period.
key_figures: Roxana (wife of Alexander, pregnant at his death)
events.certainty_note: Alexander's death is one of the most securely dated events in ancient history. The date of June 10 or 11, 323 BCE is accepted by modern scholarship, though the exact cause of death remains debated.

events.title: Partition of Alexander's empire among the Diadochi (Succession Wars begin)
events.description: Following Alexander's death, his generals (the Diadochi) meet in Babylon to partition his empire. Perdiccas is appointed regent for Alexander's heirs (his half-brother Philip Arrhidaeus and his unborn son Alexander IV). The satrapies are distributed: Ptolemy receives Egypt, Seleucus receives Babylon, Antigonus receives Phrygia, Lysimachus receives Thrace, and others receive various territories. This partition is unstable and leads to decades of warfare as the Diadochi compete for supremacy. The Wars of the Diadochi (323–281 BCE) will ultimately result in the establishment of the three major Hellenistic kingdoms: Ptolemaic Egypt, Seleucid Syria, and Antigonid Macedon.
events.certainty_note: The partition of Alexander's empire is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is secure within ±1 year.
cross_references: -281-001 (Battle of Corupedium, final resolution of Diadochi wars)

events.title: Lamian War: Greek city-states revolt against Macedonian rule
events.description: Following Alexander's death, the Greek city-states, led by Athens and Aetolia, attempt to throw off Macedonian control in the Lamian War (also called the Hellenic War). The revolt is triggered by the death of Alexander and the perceived weakness of Macedonian authority under the regency of Perdiccas and Antipater. The war lasts approximately one year and ends with the defeat of the Greek forces at the Battle of Crannon (322 BCE) and the reassertion of Macedonian control. The war marks the end of Greek city-state independence and the beginning of Macedonian hegemony over Greece.
key_figures: Leosthenes (Athenian general, leader of the revolt)
Demosthenes (Athenian orator, supporter of the revolt)
events.certainty_note: The Lamian War is well-attested in ancient sources. The date of 323–322 BCE is secure.
cross_references: -322-001 (Battle of Crannon ends the Lamian War)

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya defeats the Nanda dynasty; Mauryan Empire expands
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya, with the support of his advisor Kautilya, defeats the last Nanda emperor and consolidates control over Magadha and surrounding territories. The Mauryan Empire begins its rapid expansion across northern India. The exact chronology is debated, with some sources placing this event in 322 or 321 BCE, but 323 BCE is also defensible. This marks the beginning of the Mauryan Empire's rise to dominance in the Indian subcontinent.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Chandragupta's rise is uncertain. Indian sources and Greek sources give conflicting dates. Modern scholarship places the fall of the Nanda dynasty between 322–320 BCE. The exact year is debated, with 323 BCE being one of several defensible dates.

events.title: Ptolemy I establishes Alexandria as capital of Egypt; begins Ptolemaic dynasty
events.description: Ptolemy I Soter, having secured Egypt as his satrapy following Alexander's death, establishes Alexandria as the capital of Egypt and the center of Ptolemaic rule. He moves Alexander's body to Alexandria, using it as a symbol of legitimacy and continuity. Alexandria becomes a major Hellenistic center of learning, trade, and culture. This marks the beginning of the Ptolemaic dynasty, which will rule Egypt for nearly three centuries until the death of Cleopatra VII in 30 BCE.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (founder of Ptolemaic dynasty)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's establishment of Alexandria as capital is well-attested. The date is secure within ±1 year.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Alexander's death is traditionally given as June 10 or 11, 323 BCE, but some ancient sources give different dates (e.g., some sources suggest June 13). The cause of death remains debated—ancient sources cite fever, poisoning, or complications from a wound, but modern medical historians have proposed various diagnoses (malaria, typhoid, acute pancreatitis, etc.). The chronology of Chandragupta's rise is highly uncertain; some sources place the fall of the Nanda dynasty in 322 BCE or 321 BCE rather than 323 BCE. The significance of the Lamian War has been debated by modern scholars; some argue it was less significant than traditionally portrayed and that Greek city-states retained more autonomy than previously thought. The partition of Alexander's empire was not as clean or final as traditionally depicted; the satrapies were redistributed multiple times in the following years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 323 BCE is rich in Greek sources but heavily influenced by the perspectives of the Diadochi and their successors. The sources are often written decades or centuries after the events. Indian sources on Chandragupta are even more distant from the events they describe and are influenced by religious and dynastic narratives. The synchronization between Greek and Indian chronologies remains problematic. Archaeological evidence for this year is sparse. Western scholarship has traditionally privileged Greek sources and the Hellenistic world, potentially underrepresenting Indian and Persian perspectives. The exact causes and consequences of events in 323 BCE are often reconstructed through inference rather than direct evidence.

graph_edges.note: Alexander's death triggers the partition of his empire
Alexander's death triggers the Lamian War in Greece
The unstable partition leads to the First War of the Diadochi
The Lamian War continues and ends with the Battle of Crannon in 322 BCE
Chandragupta's consolidation of power leads to his later encounter with Seleucus I
Ptolemy's establishment of Alexandria leads to his declaration as king in 305 BCE

era_context: The late Hellenistic period following Alexander the Great's eastern campaigns. Alexander is consolidating control over the Persian Empire and planning further expansion. The Mauryan Empire under Chandragupta is consolidating power in northern India. In Greece, the city-states remain fragmented; in Egypt, Ptolemy I is establishing Ptolemaic rule following Alexander's death in Babylon (323 BCE).
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek/Roman contact zones
East Asia (Chinese sources exist but are sparse for this specific year)

events.title: Alexander the Great reaches the Hyphasis River; army mutinies and refuses further advance
events.description: Alexander's army, exhausted after years of campaigning from Macedon to the Indus, refuses to advance further eastward at the Hyphasis River (modern Sutlej). The mutiny forces Alexander to turn back toward Babylon. This marks the effective eastern limit of Alexander's conquests and represents a critical moment where logistical strain and soldier morale override imperial ambition. The refusal demonstrates the limits of Macedonian military expansion and sets the stage for the fragmentation of Alexander's empire after his death.
events.certainty_note: The mutiny at the Hyphasis is attested in multiple Hellenistic sources, though written decades or centuries after the event. The date is conventionally placed in 326 BCE by some scholars, but 324 BCE is also defensible depending on chronological reconstruction. Modern scholarship generally accepts the event as historical.

events.title: Alexander marches toward Babylon; consolidates control over Persian territories
events.description: Following the army's refusal to advance further east, Alexander turns westward and marches toward Babylon, the administrative center of the Persian Empire. He consolidates Hellenistic control over the vast territories conquered from Darius III, establishing Greek administrative structures and settling Macedonian veterans. This period sees the integration of Persian and Greek administrative systems and the beginning of the cultural synthesis that would define the Hellenistic world.
events.certainty_note: Alexander's march to Babylon and consolidation of Persian territories is well-attested in Hellenistic sources. The chronology is secure within ±1 year.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya consolidates northern Indian territories; defeats Nanda dynasty
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya, with support from the Brahmin advisor Kautilya (Vishnugupta), consolidates control over the Nanda Empire in Magadha and extends Mauryan rule across northern India. This period marks the rapid expansion of the Mauryan Empire, which will become the dominant power in the Indian subcontinent. The exact chronology of Chandragupta's rise is debated, with some sources placing key events in 322–321 BCE, but by 324 BCE the consolidation of northern territories was well underway.

sources.name: Strabo, Geography (citing Greek sources on India)

events.certainty_note: The chronology of Chandragupta's rise is uncertain. Indian sources and Greek sources (Strabo, Plutarch) give conflicting dates. Modern scholarship places the consolidation of the Nanda Empire's fall between 322–320 BCE, with 324 BCE being a reasonable approximation for the ongoing consolidation phase. The exact year is debated.

events.title: Ptolemy I consolidates control of Egypt following Alexander's death
events.description: Following Alexander's death in Babylon in 323 BCE, Ptolemy I Soter, one of Alexander's generals, secures Egypt as his satrapy and begins establishing the Ptolemaic dynasty. He moves Alexander's body to Alexandria and uses it as a symbol of legitimacy. By 324 BCE (or immediately after 323 BCE), Ptolemy is consolidating Greek and Egyptian administrative structures, establishing Alexandria as a major Hellenistic center. This marks the beginning of three centuries of Ptolemaic rule in Egypt.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (founder of Ptolemaic dynasty)
Alexander III of Macedon (deceased, but symbolic)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's consolidation of Egypt is well-attested. The date is secure within ±1 year of Alexander's death (323 BCE).

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Alexander's mutiny at the Hyphasis is disputed. Some modern scholars place it in 326 BCE rather than 324 BCE, based on different chronological reconstructions. The dating of Chandragupta's consolidation of the Nanda Empire is highly uncertain; some sources place the fall of the Nanda dynasty in 322 BCE, others in 321 BCE or 320 BCE. The synchronization between Greek and Indian chronologies for this period remains problematic, and the traditional dating may be off by several years. Additionally, the significance attributed to events in 324 BCE is partly retrospective—the year's importance derives largely from what happened immediately after (Alexander's death in 323 BCE), not from events intrinsic to 324 BCE itself.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 324 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Hellenistic historians writing decades or centuries after the events. Indian sources on Chandragupta are even more distant from the events they describe. The sources are heavily influenced by court narratives and the perspectives of victors (Alexander's generals, Ptolemy, Chandragupta). Archaeological evidence for this year is sparse. The chronology of this period is reconstructed through a combination of ancient sources, some of which contradict each other. Western scholarship has traditionally privileged Greek sources, potentially underrepresenting Indian and Persian perspectives. The exact synchronization between Greek and Indian chronologies remains uncertain.

graph_edges.note: The mutiny at the Hyphasis forces Alexander to turn back; he dies in Babylon the following year
Alexander's consolidation of Persian territories sets the stage for the partition of his empire after his death
Chandragupta's consolidation is part of the broader Mauryan expansion in the early 4th century BCE
Ptolemy's consolidation of Egypt occurs in the context of the partition of Alexander's empire

era_context: Alexander begins his retreat from India, moving south along the Indus River and then west toward Babylon. His army suffers severe losses in the Gedrosian Desert. In Greece, Macedonian hegemony remains secure under Antipater. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin's expansion accelerating. In India, the Nanda Empire remains dominant in the Gangetic plain, unaware of Alexander's retreat.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from Indian, Persian, and Qin sources

events.title: Alexander's Retreat from India via the Indus River
events.region: Northwestern India and the Indus Valley
events.description: Alexander begins his retreat from India, moving south along the Indus River. The retreat is marked by continued military operations against local kingdoms and the gradual reduction of his army through combat, disease, and desertion. Alexander establishes settlements and garrisons along the Indus to maintain control over the region. The retreat represents the beginning of the end of Alexander's eastern campaigns and the start of his journey back toward Babylon.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book V–VI
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book IX

events.certainty_note: Alexander's retreat from India is well-attested in multiple sources. Dating the beginning of the retreat to 325 BCE is generally accepted by modern scholars. The event itself is historically certain.

events.title: Crossing of the Gedrosian Desert
events.description: Alexander's army crosses the Gedrosian Desert (modern Balochistan) on its way from the Indus Valley to Babylon. The crossing is catastrophic, with the army suffering enormous losses due to heat, thirst, disease, and starvation. Ancient sources report that the army loses tens of thousands of soldiers, though modern scholars debate the exact figures. The Gedrosian Desert crossing becomes one of the most famous episodes of Alexander's campaign, illustrating both the resilience of his army and the limits of his strategic planning. The disaster is attributed to Alexander's decision to follow a difficult route rather than the safer coastal path.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book VI

events.certainty_note: The crossing of the Gedrosian Desert is well-attested in multiple sources. Dating to 325 BCE is generally accepted by modern scholars. The event itself is historically certain, though the exact number of casualties is debated.

events.title: Nearchus's Voyage Along the Arabian Sea
events.description: Nearchus, one of Alexander's admirals, leads a fleet along the coast of the Arabian Sea and Persian Gulf, parallel to Alexander's overland retreat. The voyage is undertaken to establish supply lines for the retreating army and to explore the coastline. Nearchus's voyage represents one of the earliest known systematic explorations of the Arabian Sea and provides valuable geographical information about the region. The voyage is documented in Nearchus's own account, which survives in fragments quoted by later authors.
events.certainty_note: Nearchus's voyage is well-attested in ancient sources, particularly in Arrian's Indica, which is based on Nearchus's own account. Dating to 325 BCE is generally accepted by modern scholars. The voyage itself is historically certain.

disconfirming_evidence: The casualty figures for the Gedrosian Desert crossing are highly disputed. Ancient sources report losses of tens of thousands, but modern scholars question these figures and suggest they may be exaggerated. Some scholars argue that the desert crossing was not as catastrophic as traditionally portrayed. The reasons for Alexander's choice to cross the desert rather than follow the safer coastal route are debated; some sources suggest it was a deliberate decision to demonstrate his power, while others attribute it to poor planning or misinformation. Nearchus's voyage is well-attested, but some details in his account are questioned by modern scholars.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 325 BCE is relatively well-documented, but still depends entirely on later Greek and Roman sources. Arrian, Plutarch, Strabo, and Curtius Rufus are the primary sources, all writing centuries after the events. Arrian's Indica is based on Nearchus's own account, making it closer to a contemporary source than most other accounts of Alexander's campaign. The accounts reflect the perspectives of Alexander's companions and later Hellenistic historians. The Gedrosian Desert crossing is one of the most famous episodes of Alexander's campaign, but the exact details and casualty figures are debated. Indian and Persian sources provide no contemporary account of Alexander's retreat. Archaeological evidence from the Gedrosian Desert and the Arabian Sea for this period is limited. The record is heavily weighted toward military and exploratory events; the experiences of conquered populations, economic impacts, and cultural developments are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Retreat from India leads to crossing of Gedrosian Desert
Nearchus's voyage parallels Alexander's overland retreat
Gedrosian Desert crossing leads to arrival in Babylon

era_context: Alexander invades India and fights the Battle of the Hydaspes against King Porus. This is the climactic military engagement of his eastern campaigns. In Greece, Macedonian hegemony remains secure. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin's power growing. In India, the Nanda Empire remains the dominant power in the Gangetic plain, but Alexander's invasion threatens the northwestern kingdoms.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from Indian, Persian, and Qin sources

events.title: Battle of the Hydaspes River
events.description: Alexander defeats King Porus of the Paurava kingdom at the Hydaspes River (modern Jhelum River). The battle is one of Alexander's most celebrated victories, fought against an opponent with war elephants—a new military challenge for the Macedonian army. Alexander's tactical brilliance and the discipline of his troops overcome Porus's numerical advantage and the psychological impact of the elephants. The victory opens the way for Alexander's further advance into India and demonstrates the superiority of Macedonian tactics over Indian military organization. Porus is defeated but treated with respect and allowed to retain his kingdom as a vassal.
key_figures: Bucephalus (Alexander's horse, reportedly died after the battle)

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book V
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book IX
Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVII

events.certainty_note: The Battle of the Hydaspes is well-attested in multiple ancient sources and is one of the most reliably documented events of Alexander's campaign. Dating to 326 BCE is accepted by most modern scholars. The battle itself is historically certain, though some tactical details are debated.

events.title: Founding of Bucephala
events.description: Alexander founds the city of Bucephala on the Hydaspes River, named after his famous horse Bucephalus, who reportedly died shortly after the Battle of the Hydaspes. The city is established as a Macedonian settlement and garrison to consolidate control over the region. The founding of Bucephala represents Alexander's policy of establishing Greek cities throughout his empire to serve as centers of Hellenistic culture and administrative control.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book V

events.certainty_note: The founding of Bucephala is attested in ancient sources, but its exact location and subsequent history are uncertain. Archaeological evidence for the city is limited. Dating to 326 BCE is generally accepted, but some sources place it slightly later.

events.title: Alexander Advances into the Gangetic Plain
events.description: Following his victory at the Hydaspes, Alexander advances further into India, moving toward the Gangetic plain. His army encounters reports of the massive armies of the Nanda Empire, which controls the Gangetic region. The prospect of facing an even larger opponent, combined with the exhaustion of his troops and the monsoon season, leads to the army's refusal to advance further. Alexander is forced to turn back, marking the beginning of his retreat from India.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book V
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book IX

events.certainty_note: Alexander's advance into India and the army's refusal to proceed further is well-attested in multiple sources. The event is historically certain, though the exact extent of Alexander's advance and the reasons for the army's refusal are debated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of the Hydaspes is one of the most reliably documented events of Alexander's campaign, but some modern scholars question the accuracy of the casualty figures and tactical details provided by ancient sources. The founding of Bucephala is attested in sources, but its exact location and subsequent history are uncertain; some scholars question whether it was a major city or a minor garrison. The story of Bucephalus's death after the battle may be legendary rather than historical. The extent of Alexander's advance into India and the reasons for the army's refusal to proceed further are debated; some sources suggest the army was more concerned about supply lines and the monsoon than about the Nanda Empire's armies.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 326 BCE is relatively well-documented compared to earlier years of Alexander's campaign, but still depends entirely on later Greek and Roman sources. Arrian, Plutarch, and Curtius Rufus are the primary sources, all writing centuries after the events. The accounts reflect the perspectives of Alexander's companions and later Hellenistic historians. The Battle of the Hydaspes is one of the most reliably documented events of Alexander's campaign, but some tactical details are debated. Indian sources provide no contemporary account of Alexander's invasion; the Nanda Empire's perspective is entirely absent. Archaeological evidence from northwestern India for this period is limited. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; the experiences of conquered populations, economic impacts, and cultural developments are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Victory at Hydaspes leads to founding of Bucephala
Victory at Hydaspes enables further advance into India
Army's refusal to advance leads to retreat from India

era_context: Alexander is in the final stages of his Central Asian campaigns, preparing to move into India. Spitamenes' rebellion is being suppressed. In Greece, Macedonian hegemony is secure. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin's expansion accelerating. In India, the Nanda Empire dominates the Gangetic plain, unaware of Alexander's imminent arrival.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from Qin, Indian, and Persian sources

events.title: Capture and Death of Spitamenes
events.description: Spitamenes, the Sogdian rebel leader, is defeated and killed. According to some accounts, he is betrayed by his own allies among the nomadic tribes and his head is sent to Alexander. The suppression of Spitamenes' rebellion marks the effective end of organized resistance to Macedonian rule in Central Asia. Alexander consolidates control over Bactria and Sogdiana, establishing the eastern frontier of his empire and preparing for the invasion of India.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book IV
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VIII
Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVII

events.certainty_note: Spitamenes' death is attested in multiple sources, but dating is uncertain. Most scholars place it in 327 BCE, but some argue for 326 BCE. The exact circumstances of his death (betrayal vs. military defeat) vary across sources.

events.title: Alexander Reaches the Hyphasis River (Sutlej)
events.description: Alexander's army reaches the Hyphasis River (modern Sutlej River) in northwestern India. This represents the furthest extent of Alexander's conquests. The army, exhausted from years of campaigning and facing the prospect of encountering the large armies of the Nanda Empire, refuses to advance further. Alexander is forced to turn back, marking the beginning of his retreat toward Babylon. The Hyphasis represents the practical limit of Macedonian expansion in Asia.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book V
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book IX

events.certainty_note: Alexander's arrival at the Hyphasis and the army's refusal to advance further is well-attested in multiple sources. Dating to 326 BCE is more commonly accepted by modern scholars, but some sources place it in 327 BCE. The event itself is historically certain.

events.title: Cleitus the Black Incident
events.description: Cleitus the Black, one of Alexander's most senior generals and childhood companion, is killed by Alexander in a drunken quarrel. The incident occurs during a banquet and reflects growing tensions between Alexander and his old Macedonian companions over his adoption of Persian customs and increasingly autocratic behavior. Cleitus's death marks a symbolic break between Alexander and the old Macedonian guard. The event is widely interpreted as a turning point in Alexander's reign, demonstrating his willingness to eliminate even his closest associates.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book IV
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: The death of Cleitus is well-attested in multiple sources. Dating is generally placed in 328 BCE by most modern scholars, though some sources suggest 327 BCE. The event itself is historically certain, though the exact circumstances and Alexander's degree of culpability are debated.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Spitamenes' death is uncertain; most scholars place it in 327 BCE, but some argue for 326 BCE. The Cleitus incident is traditionally dated to 328 BCE by most modern scholars, not 327 BCE, though some sources place it in 327 BCE. The exact year cannot be definitively established. Alexander's arrival at the Hyphasis is often dated to 326 BCE rather than 327 BCE in modern scholarship. The chronology of Alexander's Indian campaign remains contested due to the absence of contemporary sources and inconsistencies in later accounts.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 327 BCE remains entirely dependent on later Greek and Roman sources. Arrian, Plutarch, and Curtius Rufus are the primary sources, all writing centuries after the events. The accounts reflect the perspectives of Alexander's companions and later Hellenistic historians. The chronology of events in Central Asia and the transition to India is notoriously difficult to establish, and modern scholars continue to debate the exact sequence and dating. Archaeological evidence from Central Asia and northwestern India for this period is limited. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; the experiences of conquered populations, economic impacts, and cultural developments are largely invisible. Indian and Persian perspectives are entirely absent from surviving sources. The Cleitus incident, while famous, may have been embellished or reinterpreted by later sources to illustrate Alexander's character.

graph_edges.note: Suppression of Sogdiana enables Alexander's invasion of India
Arrival at Hyphasis leads to Battle of Hydaspes and retreat
Cleitus's death continues pattern of Alexander eliminating rivals

era_context: Alexander continues his consolidation of the Persian Empire's eastern territories. Spitamenes leads a major rebellion in Sogdiana. In Greece, Macedonian control remains secure under Antipater. The Hellenistic world is being reshaped by Alexander's conquests. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin's power growing. In India, the Nanda Empire remains dominant.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from contemporary Qin, Indian, and Persian sources

events.title: Spitamenes' Rebellion in Sogdiana
events.description: Spitamenes, a Sogdian nobleman, leads a major revolt against Macedonian rule. He initially defeats Alexander's forces in several engagements and allies with nomadic tribes from the steppes. The rebellion represents the most serious resistance Alexander faces in Central Asia. Alexander responds with a series of campaigns to suppress the revolt, which continues intermittently over several years. The conflict introduces Alexander's army to steppe warfare and demonstrates the challenges of controlling Central Asian territories.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book IV
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VII–VIII
Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVII

events.certainty_note: Spitamenes' rebellion is well-attested in multiple sources, but the exact chronology is uncertain. Modern scholars debate whether the rebellion began in 329 or 328 BCE and lasted until 327 or 326 BCE. Arrian's account is the most detailed but contains internal inconsistencies.

events.title: Alexander's Marriage to Roxana
events.description: Alexander marries Roxana (Roxshana), daughter of the Bactrian nobleman Oxyartes. The marriage is politically motivated, designed to legitimize Macedonian rule and integrate the Bactrian aristocracy into Alexander's regime. Roxana becomes Alexander's principal wife and bears him a son (Alexander IV) after his death. The marriage represents Alexander's policy of cultural fusion and integration of conquered peoples into his empire.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book IV
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: The marriage is attested in multiple sources, but dating is uncertain. Some sources place it in 328 BCE, others in 327 BCE. The exact year is debated by modern scholars, with estimates ranging from 329–327 BCE.

events.title: Philotas Conspiracy and Execution
events.description: Philotas, son of Parmenion and commander of the Macedonian cavalry, is accused of conspiracy against Alexander. He is tried, convicted, and executed. His father Parmenion, one of Alexander's most senior generals, is also executed in absentia. The purge removes potential rivals and consolidates Alexander's personal authority. The event marks a turning point in Alexander's reign, as he becomes increasingly autocratic and suspicious of his generals.
key_figures: Alexander III of Macedon (judge and executor)

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book III
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VI–VII

events.certainty_note: The Philotas conspiracy is well-attested in multiple sources. However, modern scholars debate whether the conspiracy was real or fabricated by Alexander. Dating is generally placed in 330 BCE by most scholars, though some sources suggest 329 or 328 BCE. The event itself is historically certain, but its interpretation is contested.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Spitamenes' rebellion is highly uncertain. While it is often associated with 328 BCE, some scholars place its beginning in 329 BCE and its end in 327 or 326 BCE. The exact chronology cannot be established from surviving sources. The Philotas conspiracy is traditionally dated to 330 BCE, not 328 BCE, by most modern scholars including Bosworth. Its inclusion here reflects uncertainty in the historical record. Modern scholarship increasingly questions whether the conspiracy was genuine or a fabrication by Alexander to eliminate a rival. The marriage to Roxana is dated variously to 329, 328, or 327 BCE across different sources; no definitive dating is possible.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 328 BCE remains entirely dependent on later Greek and Roman sources with no contemporary documentation. Arrian and Curtius Rufus are the primary sources, both writing centuries after the events. The accounts reflect the perspectives of Alexander's companions and later Hellenistic historians, with potential biases toward justifying Alexander's actions. The chronology of events in Central Asia is notoriously difficult to establish, and modern scholars continue to debate the exact sequence and dating. Archaeological evidence from Central Asia for this period is limited. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; the experiences of conquered populations, economic impacts, and cultural developments are largely invisible. Persian and Central Asian perspectives are entirely absent from surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Spitamenes' rebellion continues and intensifies in 327 BCE
Marriage to Roxana results in birth of Alexander IV
Purge of generals continues Alexander's consolidation of power

era_context: The height of Alexander the Great's Persian campaign. Following his victory at Gaugamela (331 BCE), Alexander is consolidating control over the eastern satrapies of the Achaemenid Empire. In Greece, the Macedonian hegemony under Antipater remains firm. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin ascendant. In India, the Nanda Empire dominates the Gangetic plain.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources for this period)
Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman awareness
Detailed records from Qin China are sparse for this specific year

events.title: Alexander's Campaign in Bactria and Sogdiana
events.description: Alexander advances into Bactria and Sogdiana, pursuing the remnants of the Persian army under Bessus. He encounters fierce resistance from local populations and nomadic tribes. The campaign is marked by brutal sieges and the consolidation of Macedonian control over the northeastern reaches of the former Persian Empire. This expansion extends Alexander's supply lines and introduces his army to Central Asian warfare tactics.

sources.name: Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great

events.certainty_note: No contemporary written records survive. Dating relies on Arrian's chronology, which is generally reliable but has internal inconsistencies regarding the exact year of events in Bactria-Sogdiana. Modern scholarship places this campaign between 330–327 BCE with uncertainty of ±1–2 years.

events.title: Capture and Execution of Bessus
events.description: Bessus, the last major Persian resistance leader and satrap of Bactria, is captured by Alexander's forces. Bessus had proclaimed himself Artaxerxes V after Darius III's death. He is executed—accounts vary on whether by crucifixion or flaying—marking the symbolic end of organized Persian resistance. This event consolidates Alexander's claim to be the legitimate successor to the Achaemenid throne.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book III–IV
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VII

events.certainty_note: Dating of Bessus's capture is debated. Arrian places it in 329 BCE, but some scholars argue for 328 BCE. The execution itself is confirmed in tradition, though details of the method vary across sources.

events.title: Antipater Suppresses Agis III's Rebellion in Greece
events.description: Antipater, regent of Macedon, defeats Agis III of Sparta in the Battle of Megalopolis. Agis had attempted to lead a Greek revolt against Macedonian hegemony while Alexander was distant in Asia. The rebellion is crushed, and Agis is killed. This victory secures Macedonian control over Greece and prevents any coordinated Greek resistance to Alexander's rule.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVII

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Megalopolis is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. Dating to 331 BCE is more commonly accepted by modern scholarship, but some sources place it in 330 or 329 BCE. The event itself is historically certain.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Bessus's capture is disputed. While Arrian places it in 329 BCE, modern scholars including some in the Bosworth school argue for 328 BCE based on the chronology of Spitamenes' rebellion and Alexander's movements. The Battle of Megalopolis is often dated to 331 BCE rather than 329 BCE in recent scholarship, which would place it outside this year. The exact sequence and dating of Alexander's Bactrian campaign remains contested due to the absence of contemporary sources and inconsistencies in later accounts.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 329 BCE is entirely dependent on later Greek and Roman compilations, primarily Arrian (2nd century CE) and Curtius Rufus (1st century CE), who drew on earlier sources now lost. No contemporary written records from Alexander's court survive. The accounts are shaped by the perspective of Alexander's companions and later Hellenistic historians. Persian sources are absent. The chronology of Alexander's Central Asian campaigns is notoriously difficult to establish precisely, and modern scholars debate the exact year-by-year sequence. Archaeological evidence from Central Asia for this period is limited. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; economic, social, and cultural developments in conquered territories are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Bactrian campaign continues into 328 BCE with Spitamenes' rebellion
Bessus's capture is consequence of Alexander's victory at Gaugamela
Suppression of Greek resistance continues Macedonian consolidation

era_context: Alexander is consolidating his control of the Persian Empire. The major cities have fallen, and Darius III is dead. However, Bessus, the satrap of Bactria, has claimed the Persian throne and is leading a resistance movement in the eastern provinces. Alexander pursues Bessus and continues to expand his control. The year is marked by the capture of Persepolis, the symbolic heart of the Persian Empire, and by the continued pursuit of Bessus. The Achaemenid dynasty has effectively ended, and Alexander is establishing himself as the successor to the Persian kings.

events.title: Capture of Persepolis
events.description: Alexander moves toward Persepolis, the ceremonial capital of the Persian Empire and the location of the royal palaces. The city is defended by a Persian garrison, but the garrison is defeated and the city is captured. Alexander enters Persepolis and is recognized as the legitimate ruler. However, the city is subsequently burned. The burning of Persepolis is controversial; sources differ on whether it was deliberate or accidental, and on whether it was ordered by Alexander or occurred during a drunken celebration. The burning is significant because it symbolizes the destruction of the Persian Empire and because it demonstrates Alexander's willingness to destroy the symbols of Persian power. The destruction of Persepolis is also controversial because it destroys valuable archives and cultural artifacts. The capture and burning of Persepolis is a turning point in Alexander's campaign; it marks the effective end of the Persian Empire as a political entity.
key_figures: Ariobarzanes (Persian satrap of Persis, defends the city)
events.certainty_note: The capture of Persepolis is attested in all major ancient sources. The date (early 330 BCE) is secure. However, the burning of the city is controversial; sources differ on whether it was deliberate or accidental.

events.title: Death of Darius III
events.description: Darius III, the last Achaemenid Great King, is killed in Bactria. According to sources, Darius is captured by Bessus and his allies and is killed (sources differ on the exact circumstances; some claim he was killed by Bessus, others claim he was killed by his own guards or died of wounds). The death of Darius marks the effective end of the Achaemenid dynasty and the end of the Persian Empire as an independent power. Bessus claims the Persian throne for himself, adopting the title of Great King and the name Artaxerxes V. However, Bessus's claim is not widely recognized, and he is eventually captured and executed by Alexander. The death of Darius is significant because it removes the legitimate Persian ruler and because it forces Alexander to deal with Bessus as a rival claimant to the Persian throne.
key_figures: Darius III Codomannus (Persian Great King, killed)
Bessus (satrap of Bactria, reportedly kills Darius or orders his death)
events.certainty_note: The death of Darius III is attested in all major ancient sources. The date (330 BCE, summer) is secure. However, the exact circumstances of his death are disputed; sources differ on whether he was killed by Bessus, by his own guards, or died of wounds.

events.title: Alexander pursues Bessus into Bactria
events.region: Bactria and northeastern Persian Empire (modern Afghanistan)
events.description: After the death of Darius III, Alexander pursues Bessus, who has claimed the Persian throne and is leading a resistance movement in the eastern provinces. Alexander moves northeastward into Bactria, pursuing Bessus. The pursuit is difficult, as Bessus has support from local tribes and from the Scythian nomads. Alexander's campaign in Bactria is protracted and difficult, lasting several years. However, Bessus is eventually captured (in 329 BCE) and executed. The pursuit of Bessus is significant because it demonstrates Alexander's determination to eliminate all rivals to his rule and because it extends Alexander's campaign into Central Asia. The campaign in Bactria also demonstrates the limits of Alexander's power; the terrain is difficult, the local populations are resistant, and the supply lines are stretched thin.
events.certainty_note: The pursuit of Bessus is attested in multiple sources. The date (late 330 BCE onward) is secure. The campaign in Bactria extends into 329 BCE.

events.title: Alexander establishes himself as successor to Persian kings
events.description: After the death of Darius III and the capture of the major cities of the Persian Empire, Alexander establishes himself as the legitimate successor to the Persian kings. He adopts Persian customs and dress, and he is recognized as the Great King by the Persian nobility and the populations of the conquered territories. This consolidation is significant because it demonstrates Alexander's intention to rule the Persian Empire as a successor to the Achaemenids rather than as a foreign conqueror. Alexander's adoption of Persian customs is controversial among his Macedonian companions, some of whom view it as a betrayal of Macedonian traditions. However, Alexander's strategy of presenting himself as a legitimate Persian ruler is effective in consolidating his control of the empire.
key_figures: Alexander III of Macedon (establishes himself as Great King)
events.certainty_note: Alexander's adoption of Persian customs and his recognition as Great King are attested in multiple sources. The date (330 BCE onward) is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader consolidation of Alexander's rule

events.title: Burning of Persepolis: deliberate or accidental?
events.description: The burning of Persepolis is one of the most controversial events of Alexander's campaign. Sources differ on whether the burning was deliberate or accidental, and on whether it was ordered by Alexander or occurred during a drunken celebration. According to some sources (particularly Plutarch and Diodorus), the burning occurred during a drunken celebration, when Alexander and his companions were drinking and a woman named Thais suggested burning the palace. According to other sources (particularly Arrian), the burning was a deliberate act of revenge for the Persian invasion of Greece. The burning destroys valuable archives and cultural artifacts, and it is controversial because it represents the destruction of the symbols of Persian civilization. The burning is significant because it symbolizes the destruction of the Persian Empire and because it demonstrates Alexander's willingness to destroy the symbols of Persian power.
key_figures: Alexander III of Macedon (orders or permits the burning)
Thais (woman who allegedly suggests burning the palace)
events.certainty_note: The burning of Persepolis is attested in multiple sources. The date (330 BCE) is secure. However, the exact circumstances and motivation are disputed.

disconfirming_evidence: The burning of Persepolis is controversial, and sources differ on whether it was deliberate or accidental. Arrian claims it was a deliberate act of revenge, while Plutarch and Diodorus claim it occurred during a drunken celebration. Modern scholars debate which account is more reliable. The story of Thais suggesting the burning is attested in some sources but is absent from others, suggesting it may be a later literary invention. The exact circumstances of Darius III's death are disputed; sources differ on whether he was killed by Bessus, by his own guards, or died of wounds.
