The claim that Alexander 'adopted Persian customs' is attested, but the extent and nature of this adoption are debated by modern scholars.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 330 BCE is rich but heavily dependent on sources written 300+ years after the events. The sources are generally favorable to Alexander and may exaggerate his achievements or minimize Persian capabilities. No contemporary Persian sources survive. The narrative is shaped by the triumphalist tradition of Alexander's campaign. The capture of Persepolis is well-attested and historically significant, but the burning of the city is controversial and the sources are inconsistent. The death of Darius III is well-attested, but the exact circumstances are disputed. The sources emphasize the dramatic personal confrontation between Alexander and Darius, which may be a later literary embellishment. The consolidation of Alexander's rule as successor to the Persian kings is attested, but the extent and nature of this consolidation are debated by modern scholars.

graph_edges.note: The capture of Persepolis led to the death of Darius III.
The death of Darius III prompted Alexander to pursue Bessus.
The capture of Persepolis enabled Alexander to establish himself as successor to Persian kings.
The burning of Persepolis was part of the capture of the city.
The pursuit of Bessus led to his eventual capture in 329 BCE.

era_context: Alexander is at the height of his power. He has conquered Egypt and the Levant and is preparing for a final confrontation with Darius III. Darius is assembling a massive army in Mesopotamia, drawing troops from across the Persian Empire. The year culminates in the Battle of Gaugamela, the decisive battle that will effectively end the Persian Empire as an independent power. This is the climactic year of Alexander's conquest of the Persian Empire.

events.title: Alexander leaves Egypt and marches toward Mesopotamia
events.description: After consolidating his control of Egypt, Alexander leaves Egypt and marches northward and eastward toward Mesopotamia. He crosses the Euphrates River and moves toward the Tigris River. Darius III is assembling a massive army in Mesopotamia, and Alexander is moving to confront him. The march is long and difficult, covering hundreds of miles through desert and mountain terrain. Alexander's army is well-supplied and disciplined, and the march is executed with remarkable efficiency. This campaign is significant because it represents the final phase of Alexander's conquest of the Persian Empire. The march demonstrates Alexander's logistical capabilities and his ability to move a large army across vast distances.
events.certainty_note: The march is attested in multiple sources. The date (spring/summer 331 BCE) is secure.

events.title: Battle of Gaugamela
events.description: Alexander encounters Darius III and a massive Persian army at Gaugamela in Mesopotamia on October 1, 331 BCE (according to the Macedonian calendar). The Persian army is estimated at 100,000–1,000,000 troops (sources vary wildly; modern estimates suggest 50,000–100,000). Alexander's army numbers approximately 47,000. The battle is fought on a plain where Darius has prepared the ground, removing obstacles and leveling the terrain to maximize the effectiveness of his cavalry and chariots. However, Alexander's superior tactics and the discipline of his troops overcome the Persian numerical advantage. Alexander's cavalry, led by Alexander himself, breaks the Persian center and forces Darius to flee the battlefield. The Persian army is routed. This victory is decisive and effectively ends the Persian Empire as an independent power. Darius flees eastward, and Alexander pursues him. The battle is the culmination of Alexander's campaign to conquer the Persian Empire. After Gaugamela, the major cities of the Persian Empire (Babylon, Susa, Persepolis) fall to Alexander with little resistance.
key_figures: Darius III Codomannus (Persian Great King, flees the battlefield)
Bessus (Persian satrap, later becomes a rival claimant to the Persian throne)
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in all major ancient sources. The date (October 1, 331 BCE, according to the Macedonian calendar) is secure. However, casualty figures and some tactical details are disputed. Modern scholarship accepts the battle as historical and strategically decisive.

events.title: Capture of Babylon
events.description: After the victory at Gaugamela, Alexander moves rapidly toward Babylon, the capital of the Persian Empire. The city is defended by a Persian garrison, but the garrison surrenders without a prolonged siege. Alexander enters Babylon and is recognized as the legitimate ruler. The city is not destroyed; Alexander treats it with respect and is recognized as the successor to the Persian kings. The capture of Babylon is significant because it represents the fall of the Persian capital and because it demonstrates that the Persian Empire has effectively collapsed. Alexander's respectful treatment of Babylon enhances his reputation and may have encouraged other Persian cities to surrender without resistance.
key_figures: Mazaeus (Persian satrap of Babylon, reportedly surrenders the city)
events.certainty_note: The capture of Babylon is attested in multiple sources. The date (late 331 BCE) is secure.

events.title: Capture of Susa
events.description: After Babylon, Alexander moves toward Susa, another major city of the Persian Empire and the location of the Persian royal treasury. The city is captured with little resistance. Alexander seizes the Persian royal treasury, which contains vast quantities of gold and silver. The capture of Susa is significant because it gives Alexander access to the wealth of the Persian Empire and because it demonstrates the complete collapse of Persian resistance. The treasure is so vast that it takes months to transport it. The capture of Susa also gives Alexander access to Persian administrative records and archives.
events.certainty_note: The capture of Susa is attested in multiple sources. The date (late 331 BCE or early 330 BCE) is secure.

events.title: Darius III flees eastward; Bessus becomes rival claimant
events.description: After the defeat at Gaugamela, Darius III flees eastward, attempting to escape Alexander's pursuit. He moves toward the eastern provinces of the Persian Empire, hoping to reorganize his forces. However, Bessus, the satrap of Bactria, and other Persian nobles begin to view Darius as a liability. Bessus and his allies eventually capture Darius and kill him (this occurs in 330 BCE, but the process begins in 331 BCE). Bessus then claims the Persian throne for himself, adopting the title of Great King. This event is significant because it marks the effective end of the Achaemenid dynasty and because it creates a rival claimant to the Persian throne, complicating Alexander's consolidation of power. The killing of Darius is a turning point; it removes the legitimate Persian ruler and forces Alexander to deal with Bessus as a rival claimant.
key_figures: Darius III Codomannus (Persian Great King, flees and is eventually killed)
Bessus (satrap of Bactria, becomes rival claimant to Persian throne)
events.certainty_note: The flight of Darius and the emergence of Bessus as a rival claimant are attested in sources. The exact date of Darius's death is disputed; sources place it in 330 BCE, but the process of his capture and death may have begun in 331 BCE.
cross_references: Leads to -330-001 (Capture of Persepolis and pursuit of Bessus)

disconfirming_evidence: The casualty figures for the Battle of Gaugamela are highly unreliable. Ancient sources claim Persian casualties of 100,000 or more, but modern scholars estimate the total Persian force at 50,000–100,000, making such casualty figures impossible. The claim that Darius was killed by Bessus in 330 BCE is attested, but the exact circumstances and date are disputed. Some sources suggest that Darius was killed by his own guards or by Bessus's men, while others suggest that he was killed in battle or died of wounds. The narrative of Alexander's rapid conquest of the Persian Empire after Gaugamela may be somewhat exaggerated; some Persian resistance continued, particularly in the eastern provinces.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 331 BCE is rich but heavily dependent on sources written 300+ years after the events. The sources are generally favorable to Alexander and may exaggerate his achievements or minimize Persian capabilities. No contemporary Persian sources survive. The narrative is shaped by the triumphalist tradition of Alexander's campaign. The Battle of Gaugamela is one of the most famous battles of antiquity, and its significance is well-established, but many details are unreliable or invented. The rapid fall of Babylon and Susa is well-attested, but the exact circumstances and the extent of Persian resistance are less reliably documented. The sources emphasize the dramatic personal confrontation between Alexander and Darius, which may be a later literary embellishment.

graph_edges.note: Alexander's march toward Mesopotamia led directly to the Battle of Gaugamela.
The victory at Gaugamela enabled Alexander to capture Babylon.
The capture of Babylon led to the capture of Susa.
The defeat at Gaugamela forced Darius to flee and prompted Bessus to claim the throne.
The capture of Susa set the stage for the capture of Persepolis.

era_context: Alexander continues his conquest of the Persian Empire following his victory at Issus. He moves south through the Levant, conquering Phoenician cities and Egypt. The Persian Empire is in retreat, though Darius III remains at large and is attempting to reorganize his forces. Egypt, under Persian control, is now within Alexander's reach. The year is marked by major sieges and the consolidation of Alexander's control over the eastern Mediterranean coast.

events.title: Siege of Tyre begins
events.description: Alexander moves south from Issus and begins the siege of Tyre, a major Phoenician city and naval power. Tyre is built on an island and is heavily fortified. The city is defended by a Phoenician garrison and is supported by the Persian navy. Alexander constructs a massive causeway (mole) from the mainland to the island to enable a direct assault. The siege is protracted and difficult; the Tyrians resist fiercely. Alexander's forces construct siege towers and battering rams. The siege lasts approximately seven months (from early 332 BCE to summer 332 BCE). This siege is significant because Tyre is a major naval power and because its conquest eliminates Persian naval bases in the eastern Mediterranean. The siege is also notable for its engineering achievement; the causeway is a remarkable feat of ancient engineering.
key_figures: Azemilcus (king of Tyre, leads the defense)
events.certainty_note: The siege is attested in multiple sources. The date (early 332 BCE to summer 332 BCE) is secure. The duration and engineering details are well-documented.

events.title: Fall of Tyre; massacre of defenders
events.description: After seven months of siege, Tyre falls to Alexander's forces in summer 332 BCE. The city is breached, and Alexander's troops enter the city. The defenders are killed or enslaved; sources report that 6,000–10,000 Tyrians are killed and 30,000 are enslaved. These figures are likely exaggerated, but the massacre is attested in multiple sources. The fall of Tyre is significant because it eliminates the last major Phoenician naval power and because it demonstrates Alexander's willingness to use extreme violence against cities that resist. The destruction of Tyre is also symbolically important, as it represents the end of Phoenician independence. The city is later rebuilt, but its power is broken.
key_figures: Azemilcus (king of Tyre, reportedly escapes by sea)
events.certainty_note: The fall of Tyre is attested in multiple sources. The date (summer 332 BCE) is secure. Casualty figures are unreliable.

events.title: Siege of Gaza
events.description: After the fall of Tyre, Alexander moves south and besieges Gaza, a fortified city in the southern Levant. Gaza is defended by a Persian garrison under the command of Batis. The city is heavily fortified and resists fiercely. Alexander constructs siege works and assault towers. The siege lasts approximately two months (from late summer to autumn 332 BCE). The city falls, and the garrison is killed. Batis is reportedly executed in a brutal manner (sources claim he is dragged around the city walls behind a chariot, echoing the treatment of Hector in Homer's Iliad). The fall of Gaza opens the road to Egypt. This siege is significant because it is the last major obstacle between Alexander and Egypt.
key_figures: Batis (Persian garrison commander, killed in brutal fashion)
events.certainty_note: The siege is attested in multiple sources. The date (late summer to autumn 332 BCE) is secure. The brutal execution of Batis is attested but may be a later literary invention or exaggeration.

events.title: Alexander enters Egypt; proclaimed Pharaoh
events.description: After the fall of Gaza, Alexander enters Egypt, which has been under Persian control since the conquest of Egypt by Cambyses II in 525 BCE. The Egyptians, who resent Persian rule, welcome Alexander as a liberator. Alexander is proclaimed Pharaoh and is recognized as the legitimate ruler of Egypt. He travels to Memphis and performs the traditional rituals of a Pharaoh. This event is significant because it adds Egypt to Alexander's dominions and because it gives him the legitimacy of a Pharaoh in the eyes of the Egyptian people. The conquest of Egypt is also strategically important because it secures the southern flank of Alexander's empire and provides access to Egypt's wealth and resources. Alexander's adoption of the Pharaonic title is a significant political move; it demonstrates his willingness to adopt local traditions and to present himself as a legitimate ruler rather than a foreign conqueror.
key_figures: Egyptian priests and nobles (recognize Alexander as Pharaoh)
events.certainty_note: Alexander's entry into Egypt and his recognition as Pharaoh are attested in multiple sources. The date (late 332 BCE) is secure. The details of the ceremonies and the Egyptian response are less reliably documented.

events.title: Alexander visits the Oracle of Amun at Siwa (or plans to)
events.description: After being proclaimed Pharaoh, Alexander reportedly plans to visit the Oracle of Amun at Siwa Oasis in western Egypt. The journey is difficult and dangerous, crossing the desert. According to sources, Alexander is guided by divine signs (ravens, according to some accounts) and reaches the oasis. The oracle reportedly confirms Alexander's divine parentage, declaring him to be the son of Amun-Ra. This event is significant because it enhances Alexander's legitimacy as a Pharaoh and because it contributes to the legend of Alexander's divine status. However, the exact date of this visit is disputed; some sources place it in 332 BCE, others in 331 BCE. The details of the oracle's pronouncement are not reliably documented; sources vary in their accounts.
key_figures: The Oracle of Amun (pronounces Alexander's divine parentage)
events.certainty_note: The visit to Siwa is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is disputed (332 or 331 BCE). The details of the oracle's pronouncement are not reliably documented. Modern scholars debate whether the visit occurred in 332 or 331 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The casualty figures for the fall of Tyre (6,000–10,000 killed, 30,000 enslaved) are likely exaggerated. The brutal execution of Batis (dragged behind a chariot) is attested in sources but may be a later literary invention designed to echo Homer's Iliad. The details of the oracle's pronouncement at Siwa are not reliably documented; sources vary in their accounts, and some scholars argue that the entire episode may be a later literary invention. The exact date of the Siwa visit is disputed; some sources place it in 332 BCE, others in 331 BCE. The claim that Alexander was guided by ravens is likely a later embellishment.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 332 BCE is rich but heavily dependent on sources written 300+ years after the events. The sources are generally favorable to Alexander and may exaggerate his achievements or minimize resistance. No contemporary Persian or Egyptian sources survive. The narrative is shaped by the triumphalist tradition of Alexander's campaign. The siege of Tyre is one of the most famous sieges of antiquity, and its significance is well-established, but many details are unreliable or invented. The visit to Siwa is particularly problematic; the sources are inconsistent about the date and the details of the oracle's pronouncement. The adoption of the Pharaonic title is well-attested and historically significant, but the exact ceremonies and the Egyptian response are less reliably documented.

graph_edges.note: The siege of Tyre led to its fall and the massacre of defenders.
The fall of Tyre enabled Alexander to move south to Gaza.
The fall of Gaza opened the road to Egypt.
Alexander's recognition as Pharaoh prompted his visit to the Oracle of Amun.
The conquest of the Levantine coast set the stage for the final confrontation with Darius.

era_context: Alexander's campaign in Asia Minor continues. The siege of Halicarnassus is completed, and Alexander moves inland and northward through Lycia and Pamphylia. Darius III is assembling a massive army to confront Alexander directly. The Persian Empire is still the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean and Near East, but Alexander's victories are establishing Macedonian military superiority. The year culminates in the decisive Battle of Issus, which will shift the balance of power fundamentally.

events.title: Siege of Halicarnassus concludes; Alexander advances inland
events.region: Halicarnassus and Lycia, southwestern Asia Minor
events.description: The siege of Halicarnassus, which began in 334 BCE, concludes in early 333 BCE. The inner citadel falls to Alexander's forces after months of assault. The Persian garrison under Orontobates evacuates by sea. Alexander does not pursue the Persian fleet; instead, he decides to eliminate Persian naval bases by conquering the coastal regions. He moves inland and northward through Lycia and Pamphylia, securing the southern coast of Asia Minor. This campaign is significant because it demonstrates Alexander's strategic decision to prioritize land conquest over naval warfare, effectively neutralizing Persian naval power by denying it bases. The conquest of Lycia and Pamphylia also secures supply lines and establishes Macedonian control over the southern Anatolian coast.
events.certainty_note: The conclusion of the siege and the subsequent campaign are attested in multiple sources. The date (early 333 BCE) is secure.

events.title: Battle of Issus
events.region: Issus, Cilicia (southeastern Asia Minor, near modern Turkey-Syria border)
events.description: Alexander encounters Darius III and a massive Persian army at Issus in Cilicia in November 333 BCE. The Persian army is estimated at 100,000–600,000 troops (sources vary wildly; modern estimates suggest 50,000–100,000). Alexander's army numbers approximately 35,000. The battle is fought in a narrow coastal plain where the Persian numerical advantage is negated. Alexander's cavalry, led by Alexander himself, breaks the Persian center and forces Darius to flee the battlefield. The Persian army is routed. This victory is decisive and psychologically devastating to the Persian Empire. Darius's flight is interpreted as a sign of weakness, and many Persian vassals and allies begin to question their loyalty. The battle is a turning point in the campaign; Alexander is now the dominant military power in the eastern Mediterranean. Darius escapes with his life but loses his family (wife, mother, and children are captured) and much of his army's equipment. The battle is fought on the same day that Darius's wife Stateira dies (according to some sources), though this may be a later literary invention.
key_figures: Darius III Codomannus (Persian Great King, flees the battlefield)
Stateira (wife of Darius III, captured; some sources claim she dies on the day of battle)
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in all major ancient sources. The date (November 333 BCE) is secure. However, casualty figures and some tactical details are disputed. Modern scholarship accepts the battle as historical and strategically decisive, though the exact numbers of troops and casualties are unreliable.

events.title: Capture of Darius III's family and camp at Issus
events.description: In the aftermath of the Battle of Issus, Alexander's forces capture Darius III's camp and family. Darius's wife Stateira, his mother Sisygambis, and his children are taken prisoner. This capture is significant because it gives Alexander leverage over Darius and because it demonstrates the completeness of the Persian defeat. The treatment of Darius's family becomes a matter of diplomatic importance; Alexander reportedly treats them with respect, which enhances his reputation and may have encouraged some Persian nobles to defect or negotiate. The capture of the royal family is also symbolically important, as it represents the humiliation of the Persian Great King.
events.certainty_note: The capture is attested in multiple sources. The date (November 333 BCE, immediately after the battle) is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader campaign to conquer Persian Empire

events.title: Darius III offers peace terms to Alexander
events.description: After the defeat at Issus, Darius III sends envoys to Alexander offering peace terms. According to sources, Darius offers to cede all Persian territory west of the Euphrates River and to pay a large ransom for the return of his family. Alexander rejects these terms, reportedly responding that he will take what he wants by conquest. This exchange is significant because it demonstrates Darius's attempt to negotiate from a position of weakness and Alexander's determination to conquer the entire Persian Empire rather than settle for territorial concessions. The rejection of Darius's offer marks a point of no return in the conflict; Alexander is now committed to the complete conquest of the Persian Empire.
key_figures: Alexander III of Macedon (recipient of peace offer)
events.certainty_note: The peace offer is attested in multiple sources, but the exact terms and Alexander's response are reported with variations. The event is likely historical, but the dialogue may be a later literary invention.
cross_references: Leads to continued conquest of Persian Empire

events.title: Memnon of Rhodes dies; Persian naval strategy collapses
events.description: Memnon of Rhodes, the Greek mercenary general commanding Persian naval forces, dies in 333 BCE (sources suggest late 333 or early 332 BCE). Memnon had been conducting a counter-campaign in the Aegean, attempting to disrupt Alexander's supply lines and encourage Greek city-states to revolt against Macedonian control. His death is a significant blow to Persian naval strategy. Without Memnon's leadership, the Persian navy loses its most capable commander, and the counter-campaign in the Aegean falters. This event is significant because it removes the one Persian commander who had successfully challenged Alexander's dominance and because it effectively concedes naval superiority to Alexander (or at least removes the threat of Persian naval dominance). The cause of Memnon's death is not clearly stated in sources; some suggest illness, others suggest wounds from battle.
key_figures: Memnon of Rhodes (Greek mercenary general, Persian naval commander)
events.certainty_note: Memnon's death is attested in sources, but the exact date and cause are uncertain. The date is placed in late 333 or early 332 BCE by modern scholars.

disconfirming_evidence: The casualty figures for the Battle of Issus are highly unreliable. Ancient sources claim Persian casualties of 100,000 or more, but modern scholars estimate the total Persian force at 50,000–100,000, making such casualty figures impossible. The claim that Darius's wife Stateira died on the day of the battle is disputed; some sources place her death years later. The narrative of Alexander's respectful treatment of Darius's family may be a later literary invention designed to enhance Alexander's reputation. The exact terms of Darius's peace offer are not reliably documented; sources vary in their accounts. The death of Memnon is attested, but the exact cause and date are uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 333 BCE is rich but heavily dependent on sources written 300+ years after the events. The sources are generally favorable to Alexander and may exaggerate his achievements or minimize Persian capabilities. No contemporary Persian sources survive. The narrative is shaped by the triumphalist tradition of Alexander's campaign. The Battle of Issus is one of the most famous battles of antiquity, and its significance is well-established, but many details are unreliable or invented. The sources emphasize the dramatic personal confrontation between Alexander and Darius, which may be a later literary embellishment.

graph_edges.note: The conclusion of the Halicarnassus siege and advance inland led directly to the Battle of Issus.
The Battle of Issus victory led to the capture of Darius's family.
The capture of Darius's family prompted his peace offer to Alexander.
The victory at Issus enabled Alexander to advance toward Egypt and the Levantine coast.
Memnon's death removed the most capable Persian naval commander.

era_context: The Hellenistic Age is beginning. Alexander III of Macedon has recently ascended to the throne (336 BCE) and is consolidating power in Greece and the Balkans. The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Darius III dominates Asia Minor, the Levant, Egypt, and Mesopotamia. Greek city-states remain fractious; Egyptian and Phoenician naval power is still significant. This is the threshold of one of history's most consequential military campaigns.

events.title: Alexander crosses the Hellespont and invades Asia Minor
events.region: Hellespont (Dardanelles Strait) / Phrygia, northwestern Asia Minor
events.description: Alexander III of Macedon, aged 22, leads an army of approximately 35,000 infantry and 5,000 cavalry across the Hellespont into Persian-controlled Asia Minor. This crossing marks the beginning of his campaign to conquer the Persian Empire. The crossing itself was unopposed; Persian forces under satraps Arsites, Spithridates, and Rheomithres were gathering but had not yet concentrated. Alexander's invasion was motivated by both personal ambition and the long-standing Greek desire to punish Persia for the invasions of 480–479 BCE. The crossing was a logistical and symbolic act of commitment—Alexander reportedly threw his spear onto Asian soil first, claiming it as a spear-won prize.
key_figures: Spithridates (Persian satrap of Lydia and Ionia)

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander (compiled 2nd century CE from earlier sources, particularly Ptolemy I and Aristobulus)
Plutarch, Life of Alexander (1st–2nd century CE)
Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica (1st century BCE)
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great (1st century CE, fragmentary)

events.certainty_note: The crossing is attested in multiple near-contemporary sources compiled by historians who had access to eyewitness accounts (Ptolemy, Aristobulus). The date 334 BCE is secure in the Macedonian and Greek chronological tradition. No contemporary inscriptions or papyri directly record the event, but the consistency of the narrative across independent sources and the strategic logic of the campaign make the event itself certain.

events.title: Battle of the Granicus River
events.region: Granicus River valley, Phrygia, northwestern Asia Minor
events.description: Approximately two months after crossing the Hellespont, Alexander encounters a Persian force under the command of satraps Arsites, Spithridates, and others at the Granicus River. The Persian force is estimated at 20,000–40,000 troops (sources vary widely). Alexander's army, numbering ~35,000, attacks across the river in a bold frontal assault. The Macedonian cavalry, led by Alexander himself, breaks the Persian line. The Persian force is routed; casualties are reported as 1,000–20,000 (sources are unreliable). This victory is Alexander's first major engagement against Persian forces and establishes his reputation as a military commander. It opens the road to Sardis and the interior of Asia Minor. The battle demonstrated the superiority of Macedonian phalanx tactics and cavalry coordination over Persian forces, though the Persians had not yet committed their full strength.
key_figures: Arsites (Persian satrap, reportedly killed in battle)
Spithridates (Persian satrap, reportedly killed in battle)
Memnon of Rhodes (Greek mercenary commander, reportedly advised against the battle)
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in all major ancient sources. The date (334 BCE, spring) is secure. However, casualty figures and tactical details vary significantly between sources. Modern scholarship accepts the battle as historical and strategically decisive, though exact numbers and some tactical claims (e.g., Alexander's near-death) are debated.

events.title: Siege and fall of Miletus
events.description: After the victory at Granicus, Alexander moves south along the Ionian coast, besieging the Greek city of Miletus, which had been garrisoned by Persian forces and Greek mercenaries. The city is defended by a Persian garrison under the command of Hegesistratus. Alexander's forces construct siege works and assault the city walls. The city falls after a brief siege (sources suggest days to weeks). The garrison is killed or enslaved. This siege is significant because it demonstrates Alexander's ability to conduct siege warfare and because it secures the Ionian coast for his advance. However, the Persian fleet remains a threat, and Alexander decides not to build a navy to match it, instead relying on land campaigns to eliminate Persian naval bases.
events.certainty_note: The siege is attested in multiple sources. The date (334 BCE, summer) is secure. Details of the siege are sparse in surviving accounts.
cross_references: Part of broader campaign to secure Ionian coast

events.title: Siege of Halicarnassus begins
events.description: Alexander moves to besiege Halicarnassus, a major fortified city and naval base in Caria, held by the Persian satrap Mausolus's widow Ada and a Persian garrison under Orontobates. The city is heavily fortified and defended by both land and sea forces. Alexander constructs siege works and assault towers. The siege is protracted and difficult; the city's defenders make multiple sorties. Eventually, the outer fortifications are breached, but the inner citadel holds out. The siege continues into 333 BCE. This siege is significant because Halicarnassus is a major Persian stronghold and because it demonstrates the difficulty of reducing fortified cities even for Alexander's army.
key_figures: Ada of Caria (widow of Mausolus, initially defending the city, later allied with Alexander)
events.certainty_note: The siege is attested in multiple sources. The date (334 BCE, autumn) is secure. The siege extended into 333 BCE.

events.title: Darius III mobilizes the Persian Empire's resources for war
events.description: In response to Alexander's invasion, Darius III Codomannus, the Achaemenid Great King, begins mobilizing the vast resources of the Persian Empire for a major counteroffensive. Darius had only recently secured his throne (336 BCE) and faced internal challenges, but Alexander's invasion forces him to prioritize external defense. Darius orders the assembly of a massive army, drawing troops from across the empire's provinces. He also sends the Greek mercenary general Memnon of Rhodes to command Persian naval forces and conduct a counter-campaign in the Aegean. This mobilization is significant because it represents the Persian Empire's commitment to defending itself against the Macedonian threat, though the empire's vast size and logistical challenges mean that concentrating forces takes time.
key_figures: Memnon of Rhodes (Greek mercenary general, Persian naval commander)
events.certainty_note: The mobilization is attested in sources, but the precise timing and extent are not documented in contemporary Persian records (which do not survive). The date is inferred from the narrative of events.

disconfirming_evidence: The casualty figures reported in ancient sources for the Battle of the Granicus are highly unreliable and vary wildly (from 1,000 to 20,000+ Persian casualties). Modern scholarship treats these numbers with skepticism. Additionally, some sources (particularly Curtius Rufus) contain dramatic embellishments and invented details (e.g., Alexander's near-drowning) that are not corroborated by earlier sources like Arrian. The claim that Alexander 'threw his spear' onto Asian soil is likely a later literary invention. The extent of Persian resistance and the actual strength of Persian forces in Asia Minor in 334 BCE are debated; some scholars argue that the satraps were not fully prepared for Alexander's invasion and that Darius's delayed response was partly due to underestimating the threat.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 334 BCE is rich but heavily dependent on sources written 300+ years after the events (Arrian, Plutarch, Diodorus, Curtius). These sources drew on earlier accounts by Ptolemy I and Aristobulus, who were participants in Alexander's campaign, but those earlier works survive only in quotation or paraphrase. No contemporary Persian sources survive; our understanding of the Persian perspective is filtered through Greek and later Hellenistic sources. The sources are generally favorable to Alexander and may exaggerate his achievements or minimize Persian capabilities. Archaeological evidence from Asia Minor provides some corroboration (destruction layers, inscriptions) but is limited. The narrative is shaped by the triumphalist tradition of Alexander's campaign and by the later Hellenistic historiographic tradition.

graph_edges.note: The crossing of the Hellespont directly led to the Battle of the Granicus within weeks.
Victory at Granicus enabled Alexander to advance south and besiege Miletus.
Fall of Miletus allowed Alexander to move further south to Halicarnassus.
Darius's mobilization resulted in the assembly of forces that would meet Alexander at Issus in 333 BCE.
Alexander's invasion triggered Darius's mobilization response.

era_context: The Hellenistic world in formation. Alexander III of Macedon (age 21) has recently consolidated power in Greece following his father Philip II's assassination and is preparing his Persian campaign. The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Darius III remains the dominant Near Eastern power. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin ascendant. Egypt is under Persian rule; India is fragmented into regional kingdoms.

events.title: Alexander's Campaign Against the Thracian Tribes and Danube Crossing
events.description: Alexander marched north from Macedon to secure his northern frontier against Thracian and Illyrian tribes. He crossed the Danube River (the first Macedonian king to do so) to demonstrate Macedonian power and prevent incursions. This campaign consolidated control over the Balkans and secured his rear before the Persian invasion. The crossing was largely unopposed; the symbolic and strategic significance lay in establishing Macedonian dominance over the region and preventing coordinated resistance from northern tribes during his absence.
key_figures: Alexander III of Macedon (King of Macedon)

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book I
Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVII

events.certainty_note: No contemporary accounts survive. Arrian (2nd century CE) drew on earlier Hellenistic sources, particularly Ptolemy and Aristobulus. The campaign is attested in multiple later sources with consistent broad outlines, but specific dates and tactical details vary.

events.title: Destruction of Thebes by Alexander
events.description: Upon learning that Thebes had revolted and was in contact with the Persian court, Alexander rapidly returned from Thrace and besieged the city. After a brief resistance, Thebes was stormed. The city was razed, its population enslaved or killed (estimates range from 6,000 to 30,000 deaths), and its territory distributed to neighboring cities. This act of exemplary destruction was intended to deter other Greek cities from rebellion and to demonstrate Alexander's resolve. The destruction shocked the Greek world and effectively ended organized Greek resistance to Macedonian hegemony.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book I.7-10

events.certainty_note: The destruction of Thebes is attested in multiple independent ancient sources with consistent core facts. The date (335 BCE) is secure. Casualty figures and some tactical details vary between sources, but the event itself is historically certain.

events.title: Corinthian League Reaffirms Macedonian Hegemony
events.description: Following the destruction of Thebes, the Greek city-states of the Corinthian League formally reaffirmed their allegiance to Alexander and elected him as hegemon (leader) for the planned campaign against Persia. This assembly legitimized Alexander's authority over Greece and secured Greek support (including troops and resources) for the Persian invasion. The League had been established by Philip II; Alexander's reaffirmation consolidated his inheritance of his father's hegemonic position.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book I.1

events.certainty_note: The reaffirmation of the Corinthian League's support is attested in multiple sources, but the precise date and formal procedures are not detailed in surviving accounts. The event is placed in 335 BCE by historiographic consensus, though some sources suggest it may have occurred in 336 BCE.

events.title: Qin State Consolidates Power in Warring States China
events.description: Under King Zheng (later Qin Shi Huang), the Qin state continued its aggressive expansion and administrative reforms. In 335 BCE, Qin defeated the State of Shu in a campaign that expanded Qin territory southwestward into the Sichuan basin. This conquest provided Qin with additional agricultural resources and manpower, strengthening its position relative to other Warring States. The Qin's legalist administrative system and military innovations were making it the dominant power in China.
key_figures: King Zheng of Qin (later Qin Shi Huang)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, Qin Shi Huang benji (Basic Annals)

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical chronology for this period is uncertain. The Shiji was compiled in the 1st century BCE, centuries after these events. The conquest of Shu is attested, but the precise date is debated among scholars. Some sources place it in 316 BCE rather than 335 BCE. The broader context of Qin expansion is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader Warring States consolidation

events.title: Darius III Consolidates Persian Rule
events.description: Darius III Codomannus, who had assumed the Persian throne in 336 BCE following internal dynastic turmoil, continued consolidating his authority over the vast Achaemenid Empire. In 335 BCE, he faced no major external threats and was unaware of the full extent of the Macedonian threat under Alexander. The Persian Empire remained the dominant power in the Near East, controlling Egypt, Mesopotamia, the Levant, Anatolia, and the Iranian plateau. However, internal instability and the empire's vast size would prove vulnerabilities when Alexander invaded.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book I (retrospective accounts of Persian state)

events.certainty_note: No contemporary Persian sources survive for this period. Information about Darius III and the Persian state comes entirely from Greek sources written after Alexander's conquest. These sources are biased toward the Macedonian perspective and may underestimate Persian stability or overstate internal divisions.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative emphasizes Alexander's rapid consolidation of power and the inevitability of his Persian conquest. However, modern scholarship notes: (1) The destruction of Thebes may have been more politically motivated than sources suggest—some scholars argue it was partly a response to Theban-Persian diplomatic contacts, but the extent of Persian involvement is debated. (2) The Corinthian League's 'election' of Alexander as hegemon was likely a formality; his power rested on military dominance, not genuine democratic consent. (3) Persian sources are entirely absent; the characterization of Darius III as weak or indecisive derives entirely from Greek sources written by Alexander's supporters. Modern scholarship increasingly recognizes that the Persian Empire was not in terminal decline and that Darius III was a capable administrator, though ultimately outmatched militarily.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 335 BCE is heavily weighted toward Greek and Macedonian sources, particularly Arrian's Anabasis (2nd century CE), which drew on Hellenistic-era accounts by Ptolemy and Aristobulus. These sources are generally reliable for major events but reflect Macedonian perspectives and may exaggerate Alexander's achievements or minimize Persian capabilities. Chinese sources (Shiji) are much later compilations and subject to chronological uncertainty. No contemporary Persian sources survive. The year is well-documented for the Mediterranean and Near East but poorly documented for India, sub-Saharan Africa, and northern Europe.

graph_edges.note: Securing the northern frontier enabled Alexander to proceed with the Persian invasion
The destruction of Thebes demonstrated Alexander's power and facilitated the Corinthian League's reaffirmation of support
The League's support provided troops and legitimacy for the Persian campaign
Qin's expansion in this period contributed to its eventual unification of China
Darius III's consolidation of Persian rule set the stage for his confrontation with Alexander

era_context: A pivotal year in Mediterranean and Near Eastern history. Philip II of Macedon is assassinated, and his young son Alexander III assumes power. The Achaemenid Persian Empire is in internal turmoil following the death of Artaxerxes II. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin and Chu as major powers. Egypt remains under Persian control. This year marks the beginning of the end of the Persian Empire's dominance.

events.title: Assassination of Philip II of Macedon; Alexander III Assumes Power
events.description: Philip II, king of Macedon and architect of Macedonian hegemony over Greece, was assassinated at a festival in Pella by Pausanias, a member of the royal guard. The motives remain debated—personal grievance, political conspiracy, or Persian instigation have all been proposed. Philip's death created a succession crisis, but his son Alexander III (age 20) quickly consolidated power with the support of the army and key generals. Alexander secured his position by eliminating potential rivals, including his half-brother Arrhidaeus and his cousin Amyntas. This event fundamentally altered the trajectory of Mediterranean history, as Alexander would soon launch the invasion of Persia that would reshape the known world.

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, XI.2
Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book I (brief mention)

events.certainty_note: The assassination of Philip II is attested in multiple independent ancient sources with consistent core facts. The date (336 BCE) is secure. However, the motives and any potential conspirators remain debated; ancient sources offer conflicting accounts, and modern scholars continue to discuss whether Persian involvement is plausible.

events.title: Alexander Consolidates Macedonian Hegemony Over Greece
events.description: In the months following his accession, Alexander moved swiftly to consolidate his control over Macedon and the Greek city-states that had been brought under Macedonian hegemony by his father Philip II. He secured the loyalty of the Macedonian army and nobility, then marched south to Greece to suppress potential revolts. Several Greek cities, including Athens and Thebes, considered rebellion, but Alexander's rapid response and show of force prevented open conflict in 336 BCE. He reaffirmed Macedonian control over the Corinthian League and secured the position of hegemon (leader) that his father had held.
key_figures: Antipater (regent in Macedon during Alexander's absence)

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book I.1

events.certainty_note: The broad outlines of Alexander's consolidation are attested in multiple sources, but specific dates and details of individual city submissions are not always clear. The process likely extended over several months in 336-335 BCE.

events.title: Darius III Assumes Persian Throne
events.description: Following the death of Artaxerxes II and a period of internal dynastic instability, Darius III Codomannus (a distant relative of the royal house) assumed the Persian throne. He was elevated to power by the eunuch Bagoas, who had wielded considerable influence over the previous kings. Darius III's early reign was marked by efforts to consolidate his authority and suppress rebellions in Egypt and other provinces. He was unaware of the full extent of the Macedonian threat under Alexander, as the Persian Empire had long dominated the eastern Mediterranean and Near East. His accession set the stage for the eventual confrontation with Alexander.

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, X.3

events.certainty_note: The accession of Darius III is attested in Greek sources, but no contemporary Persian sources survive. The date is placed in 336 BCE by historiographic consensus, though some sources suggest 338 BCE. The role of Bagoas and the circumstances of Darius's elevation are described in Greek sources, which may be biased or incomplete.

events.title: Artaxerxes II of Persia Dies; Succession Crisis Begins
events.description: Artaxerxes II, who had reigned for over 40 years, died in 336 BCE. His death triggered a succession crisis within the Persian royal house, as multiple claimants competed for the throne. The eunuch Bagoas, who had wielded considerable power during Artaxerxes II's reign, played a key role in determining the succession. This period of internal instability weakened Persian central authority and distracted the empire from external threats, particularly the rising power of Macedon under Alexander.

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, X.3

events.certainty_note: The death of Artaxerxes II and the succession crisis are attested in multiple Greek sources. The date (336 BCE) is secure. However, details of the internal Persian political maneuvering come entirely from Greek sources and may be incomplete or biased.

events.title: Qin State Continues Expansion Under King Zheng
events.description: In 336 BCE, the Qin state under King Zheng (later Qin Shi Huang) continued its aggressive expansion and administrative reforms. Qin defeated neighboring states and expanded its territory, consolidating its position as the dominant power in the Warring States period. The Qin's legalist administrative system, military innovations, and economic strength were making it increasingly dominant relative to other Chinese states.
key_figures: King Zheng of Qin (later Qin Shi Huang)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, Qin Shi Huang benji

events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology for this period is uncertain. The Shiji was compiled centuries after these events. The broader context of Qin expansion is secure, but specific events in 336 BCE are difficult to date precisely.
cross_references: Part of broader Warring States consolidation

disconfirming_evidence: Modern scholarship has challenged several traditional narratives about 336 BCE: (1) The assassination of Philip II is often portrayed as a simple act of personal revenge by Pausanias, but some scholars have suggested Persian involvement or a broader conspiracy. However, evidence for Persian involvement is circumstantial and debated. (2) Alexander's consolidation of power is sometimes presented as inevitable, but modern historians recognize that his position was initially precarious and that his success depended on the loyalty of key generals like Antipater and Parmenion. (3) The Persian succession crisis is often portrayed as evidence of Persian weakness, but modern scholarship recognizes that the Achaemenid Empire remained administratively sophisticated and militarily formidable, despite internal instability. (4) Some sources suggest Darius III's accession occurred in 338 BCE rather than 336 BCE, though 336 BCE is the more widely accepted date.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 336 BCE is heavily dependent on Greek sources, particularly Plutarch, Diodorus Siculus, and Justin, all writing centuries after the events. These sources are generally reliable for major political events but reflect Greek perspectives and may exaggerate or misinterpret Persian affairs. No contemporary Persian sources survive. Chinese sources (Shiji) are much later compilations with chronological uncertainties. The year is well-documented for the Mediterranean and Near East but poorly documented for other regions. The assassination of Philip II is one of the most debated events in ancient history, with scholars continuing to discuss motives and potential conspirators.

graph_edges.note: Philip's assassination forced Alexander to consolidate his power over Macedon and Greece
Alexander's consolidation of Greek hegemony enabled him to secure the northern frontier
Darius III's accession set the stage for his confrontation with Alexander
Artaxerxes II's death triggered the succession crisis that elevated Darius III
Philip's assassination and Alexander's rise initiated the chain of events leading to the Persian invasion

era_context: The late Classical period in Greece and the Mediterranean. Philip II of Macedon is at the height of his power, having consolidated Macedonian dominance over Greece through military conquest and diplomacy. The Achaemenid Persian Empire remains the dominant power in the Near East and eastern Mediterranean, though internal instability is beginning to manifest. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing consolidation of power among major states. Egypt is under Persian rule.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Specific events in Persia and China are less well-documented than Mediterranean events

events.title: Philip II Marries Cleopatra Eurydice; Succession Crisis Looms
events.description: Philip II, king of Macedon, married Cleopatra Eurydice, a Macedonian noblewoman of pure Macedonian descent. This marriage was politically significant because it raised questions about the legitimacy of Alexander's succession, as Alexander's mother Olympias was of Epirote (non-Macedonian) origin. The marriage created tension within the royal family and raised the possibility that Philip might produce a new heir of pure Macedonian blood who could supersede Alexander. This political crisis contributed to the instability that would follow Philip's assassination in 336 BCE. The marriage was a calculated political move by Philip to consolidate support among the Macedonian nobility.

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, IX.7

events.certainty_note: The marriage of Philip II to Cleopatra Eurydice is attested in multiple ancient sources. The date is placed in 337 BCE by historiographic consensus, though some sources suggest 336 BCE. The political significance of the marriage is clear from the sources.
cross_references: Related to succession disputes in Macedon

events.title: Corinthian League Formally Established Under Philip II
events.description: Philip II formalized the Corinthian League (also called the League of Corinth or Hellenic League) as a mechanism for controlling the Greek city-states and coordinating their military resources. The League was ostensibly a voluntary association of Greek states, but it was effectively under Macedonian hegemony. Philip was elected as hegemon (leader) and commander-in-chief of the League's forces. The League's primary purpose was to prepare for an invasion of Persia, which Philip had been planning. This institution would be inherited by Alexander and would provide the legal and organizational framework for his Persian campaign.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book I (context)

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Corinthian League is attested in ancient sources, but the precise date is debated. Some sources place it in 337 BCE, others in 336 BCE or even earlier. The broad outlines of the League's structure and purpose are clear, but specific details of its founding are not well-documented.

events.title: Philip II Prepares for Persian Invasion
events.description: In 337 BCE, Philip II was actively preparing for an invasion of the Persian Empire. He had assembled a large army, secured the support of the Greek city-states through the Corinthian League, and was gathering resources for the campaign. Philip sent an advance force under the command of Parmenion to establish a beachhead in Asia Minor. However, Philip's assassination in 336 BCE prevented him from leading the invasion himself. His son Alexander would inherit these preparations and would launch the invasion in 334 BCE.
key_figures: Antipater (general, left in charge of Macedon)

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book I (context)

events.certainty_note: Philip's preparations for the Persian invasion are attested in ancient sources, but specific details and dates are not always clear. The broad outlines are secure, but the timeline of preparations is somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Artaxerxes II Continues to Rule Persian Empire
events.description: In 337 BCE, Artaxerxes II continued to rule the vast Achaemenid Persian Empire. He was in the final years of his reign (he would die in 336 BCE). The Persian Empire remained the dominant power in the Near East and eastern Mediterranean, controlling Egypt, Mesopotamia, the Levant, Anatolia, and the Iranian plateau. However, the empire was experiencing internal instability, with rebellions in Egypt and other provinces, and the power of eunuchs like Bagoas was increasing. The Persian court was largely unaware of the full extent of the Macedonian threat under Philip II.

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, X.1-3

events.certainty_note: Information about Artaxerxes II's reign comes entirely from Greek sources, which are biased and may be incomplete. The date of his death (336 BCE) is secure, but details of his final years are sparse.

events.title: Qin State Expands Under King Zheng
events.description: In 337 BCE, the Qin state under King Zheng (later Qin Shi Huang) continued its expansion and consolidation of power. Qin defeated neighboring states and expanded its territory, strengthening its position as the dominant power in the Warring States period. The Qin's legalist administrative system and military innovations were making it increasingly dominant.
key_figures: King Zheng of Qin (later Qin Shi Huang)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, Qin Shi Huang benji

events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology for this period is uncertain. The Shiji was compiled centuries after these events. The broader context of Qin expansion is secure, but specific events in 337 BCE are difficult to date precisely.
cross_references: Part of broader Warring States consolidation

disconfirming_evidence: Modern scholarship has questioned some traditional interpretations of 337 BCE: (1) The marriage of Philip II to Cleopatra Eurydice is sometimes portrayed as a direct threat to Alexander's succession, but some scholars argue that Philip may have intended to maintain Alexander as his heir despite the new marriage. The political crisis may have been exaggerated by later sources. (2) The Corinthian League is sometimes presented as a purely Macedonian creation, but some scholars argue that it had genuine support from Greek city-states who saw it as a way to organize a collective defense against Persia. (3) Philip's preparations for the Persian invasion are sometimes portrayed as a personal ambition, but they may have reflected broader Greek sentiment about confronting Persian power.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 337 BCE is heavily dependent on Greek sources, particularly Plutarch, Diodorus Siculus, and Justin, all writing centuries after the events. These sources are generally reliable for major political events but may exaggerate or misinterpret details. No contemporary sources survive from Macedon or Persia. Chinese sources (Shiji) are much later compilations with chronological uncertainties. The year is moderately well-documented for the Mediterranean and Near East but poorly documented for other regions. The political situation in Macedon in 337 BCE is particularly important for understanding the succession crisis that would follow Philip's assassination, but sources are limited.

graph_edges.note: Philip's marriage to Cleopatra Eurydice created succession tensions that may have contributed to the circumstances of his assassination
The Corinthian League established by Philip was reaffirmed by Alexander and provided the framework for the Persian invasion
Philip's preparations for the Persian invasion were inherited and executed by Alexander
Artaxerxes II's continued rule ended with his death in 336 BCE, triggering a succession crisis

era_context: The late Classical period in Greece and the Mediterranean. Philip II of Macedon is consolidating his dominance over Greece through military victories and diplomacy. The Achaemenid Persian Empire remains the dominant power in the Near East, though internal instability is increasing. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin and Chu as major powers. Egypt is under Persian rule. This year marks a crucial moment in Philip's rise to hegemony over Greece.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Specific events in Persia and China are less well-documented

events.title: Battle of Chaeronea: Philip II Defeats Greek Coalition
events.description: Philip II of Macedon defeated a coalition of Greek city-states led by Athens and Thebes at the Battle of Chaeronea. This decisive victory effectively ended Greek independence and established Macedonian hegemony over Greece. The Athenian and Theban forces were crushed, and Philip's cavalry, commanded by his young son Alexander, played a crucial role in the victory. The battle demonstrated the superiority of Macedonian military organization and tactics over traditional Greek hoplite warfare. Following this victory, Philip was able to impose his will on the Greek city-states and establish the framework for the invasion of Persia.
key_figures: Alexander III of Macedon (cavalry commander, age 18)

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, IX.3-4

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Chaeronea is attested in multiple independent ancient sources with consistent core facts. The date (338 BCE) is secure. The battle is one of the most significant military engagements of the Classical period and is well-documented in ancient sources.

events.title: Philip II Establishes Macedonian Hegemony Over Greece
events.description: Following the Battle of Chaeronea, Philip II moved to consolidate his control over Greece. He imposed Macedonian garrisons in key cities, including Thebes, Corinth, and Athens. He also established the framework for the Corinthian League, which would formally organize the Greek city-states under Macedonian hegemony. Philip's victory at Chaeronea and his subsequent political consolidation marked the end of the Classical period of Greek independence and the beginning of the Hellenistic period, in which Greek culture would be spread throughout the Mediterranean and Near East by Macedonian military conquest.
events.certainty_note: The broad outlines of Philip's consolidation of control over Greece are attested in ancient sources, but specific details and dates of individual city submissions are not always clear. The process likely extended over several months in 338-337 BCE.

events.title: Artaxerxes II Faces Internal Instability in Persian Empire
events.description: In 338 BCE, Artaxerxes II continued to rule the Persian Empire, but the empire was experiencing increasing internal instability. Rebellions in Egypt and other provinces, combined with the growing power of eunuchs like Bagoas, weakened central authority. The Persian court was largely unaware of the full extent of the Macedonian threat under Philip II. The empire remained the dominant power in the Near East, but internal divisions would prove to be a significant vulnerability when Alexander invaded.

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, X.1-3

events.certainty_note: Information about Artaxerxes II's reign comes entirely from Greek sources, which are biased and may be incomplete. The internal instability of the Persian Empire is attested, but specific events in 338 BCE are not well-documented.

events.title: Qin State Continues Expansion in Warring States China
events.description: In 338 BCE, the Qin state under King Zheng (later Qin Shi Huang) continued its expansion and consolidation of power. Qin defeated neighboring states and expanded its territory, strengthening its position as the dominant power in the Warring States period. The Qin's legalist administrative system and military innovations were making it increasingly dominant relative to other Chinese states.
key_figures: King Zheng of Qin (later Qin Shi Huang)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, Qin Shi Huang benji

events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology for this period is uncertain. The Shiji was compiled centuries after these events. The broader context of Qin expansion is secure, but specific events in 338 BCE are difficult to date precisely.
cross_references: Part of broader Warring States consolidation

disconfirming_evidence: Modern scholarship has questioned some traditional interpretations of the Battle of Chaeronea and its aftermath: (1) The battle is sometimes portrayed as a decisive end to Greek independence, but some scholars argue that Greek city-states retained considerable autonomy within the Macedonian hegemonic system. (2) Alexander's role in the victory is sometimes exaggerated in ancient sources; modern historians debate the extent of his contribution to the battle. (3) The aftermath of Chaeronea is sometimes portrayed as a period of harsh Macedonian rule, but some scholars argue that Philip's treatment of the defeated cities was relatively lenient, designed to secure their cooperation rather than to punish them. (4) The date of the Battle of Chaeronea is sometimes given as 338 BCE, but some ancient sources suggest 339 BCE, though 338 BCE is the more widely accepted date.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 338 BCE is heavily dependent on Greek sources, particularly Plutarch, Diodorus Siculus, Justin, and Pausanias, all writing centuries after the events. These sources are generally reliable for major military and political events but may exaggerate or misinterpret details. No contemporary sources survive from Macedon or Persia. The Battle of Chaeronea is one of the most significant military engagements of the Classical period and is well-documented in ancient sources, though specific tactical details are sometimes disputed. Chinese sources (Shiji) are much later compilations with chronological uncertainties. The year is moderately well-documented for the Mediterranean and Near East but poorly documented for other regions.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Chaeronea enabled Philip to consolidate Macedonian hegemony over Greece
Philip's consolidation of control over Greece led to the establishment of the Corinthian League
The Battle of Chaeronea was part of the broader process that led to Philip's assassination and Alexander's rise
The Battle of Chaeronea established the Macedonian hegemony that would enable Alexander's Persian invasion

era_context: The late Classical period in Greece and the Mediterranean. Philip II of Macedon is consolidating his power and preparing for dominance over Greece. The Achaemenid Persian Empire remains the dominant power in the Near East, though internal instability is increasing. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing consolidation of power among major states. Egypt is under Persian rule. This year marks the period immediately before Philip's decisive victory at Chaeronea.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most regions outside the Mediterranean and Near East
Specific events in Persia and China are poorly documented

events.title: Philip II Campaigns in Thrace and Scythia
events.description: Philip II of Macedon conducted military campaigns in Thrace and against the Scythian tribes to the north. These campaigns were designed to secure Macedon's northern frontier and to expand Macedonian territory. Philip's campaigns in Thrace were successful, and he established Macedonian control over much of the region. These campaigns also served to strengthen Philip's military reputation and to consolidate his power base before his decisive confrontation with the Greek city-states at Chaeronea in 338 BCE.

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, IX.2

events.certainty_note: Philip's campaigns in Thrace and against the Scythians are attested in ancient sources, but specific dates and details are not always clear. The campaigns are generally placed in the period 339-338 BCE, but the exact chronology is uncertain.

events.title: Fourth Sacred War in Greece: Phocis vs. Thebes and Amphictyonic League
events.description: The Fourth Sacred War (also called the Phocian War) was a conflict between the city-state of Phocis and the Amphictyonic League (a religious and political association of Greek city-states). The war had begun in 356 BCE and continued through the 340s BCE. In 339 BCE, the conflict was ongoing, with Phocis under pressure from the Amphictyonic League and its allies, including Thebes. Philip II of Macedon was invited to intervene as a representative of the Amphictyonic League, which gave him a pretext to intervene in Greek affairs. The resolution of the Fourth Sacred War would lead directly to the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The Fourth Sacred War is attested in ancient sources, but the chronology is complex and disputed. The war lasted from 356 to 346 BCE, with various phases and interruptions. The events of 339 BCE are part of the broader conflict, but specific events in that year are not well-documented.
cross_references: Related to broader Greek political instability

events.title: Artaxerxes II Rules Persian Empire; Internal Instability Continues
events.description: In 339 BCE, Artaxerxes II continued to rule the Persian Empire, which remained the dominant power in the Near East and eastern Mediterranean. However, the empire was experiencing increasing internal instability, with rebellions in Egypt and other provinces, and the growing power of eunuchs like Bagoas. The Persian court was largely unaware of the full extent of the Macedonian threat under Philip II. Artaxerxes II was in the final years of his reign (he would die in 336 BCE).
events.certainty_note: Information about Artaxerxes II's reign comes entirely from Greek sources, which are biased and may be incomplete. The internal instability of the Persian Empire is attested, but specific events in 339 BCE are not well-documented.

events.title: Qin State Expands in Warring States China
events.description: In 339 BCE, the Qin state under King Zheng (later Qin Shi Huang) continued its expansion and consolidation of power. Qin defeated neighboring states and expanded its territory, strengthening its position as the dominant power in the Warring States period. The Qin's legalist administrative system and military innovations were making it increasingly dominant.
key_figures: King Zheng of Qin (later Qin Shi Huang)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, Qin Shi Huang benji

events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology for this period is uncertain. The Shiji was compiled centuries after these events. The broader context of Qin expansion is secure, but specific events in 339 BCE are difficult to date precisely.
cross_references: Part of broader Warring States consolidation

disconfirming_evidence: Modern scholarship has questioned some traditional interpretations of 339 BCE: (1) The Fourth Sacred War is sometimes portrayed as a simple religious conflict, but modern scholars recognize it as a complex political struggle involving multiple Greek city-states and their competing interests. (2) Philip II's intervention in the Fourth Sacred War is sometimes portrayed as a calculated move to gain influence in Greece, but some scholars argue that Philip was invited by the Amphictyonic League and that his intervention was initially welcomed by some Greek city-states. (3) The chronology of the Fourth Sacred War is complex and disputed; some sources suggest different dates for key events. (4) The extent of Persian internal instability in 339 BCE is debated; some scholars argue that the Persian Empire remained administratively sophisticated and militarily formidable despite internal divisions.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 339 BCE is sparse and heavily dependent on Greek sources, particularly Diodorus Siculus and Pausanias, both writing centuries after the events. These sources are generally reliable for major events but may lack specific details about individual years. No contemporary sources survive from Macedon or Persia. The Fourth Sacred War is complex and its chronology is disputed among modern scholars. Chinese sources (Shiji) are much later compilations with chronological uncertainties. The year is poorly documented overall, with most information coming from the broader context of the period rather than specific events in 339 BCE.

The Fourth Sacred War provided Philip with a pretext to intervene in Greece, leading to Chaeronea

era_context: 340 BCE was a year of escalating conflict and political tension across the Mediterranean and Asia. Philip II of Macedon was at the height of his power and was preparing for his final push to dominate Greece. Artaxerxes III was aging and facing challenges to Persian authority. In China, Qi was experiencing internal instability while other Warring States powers were consolidating their strength. This year represents a moment of transition before the major conflicts that would reshape the Mediterranean world in the following years.

events.title: Demosthenes delivers Third Philippic against Philip II
events.description: Demosthenes delivered the Third Philippic, his most famous speech against Philip II of Macedon. The speech was a passionate and eloquent call for Athenian resistance to Macedonian expansion and for Greek unity against the Macedonian threat. The Third Philippic became an iconic expression of Greek resistance to foreign domination and remains one of the most celebrated speeches in Western history. However, despite its rhetorical power, the speech failed to mobilize sufficient Greek opposition to Philip, and Athens remained divided and unable to effectively counter Macedonian expansion.

sources.name: Demosthenes, Third Philippic (primary text, speech delivered 340 BCE)

events.certainty_note: The Third Philippic is preserved as a primary text and is generally dated to 340 BCE. The date is well-established in ancient sources.

events.title: Philip II besieges Perinthus; conflict with Athens escalates
events.description: Philip II besieged the city of Perinthus (in Thrace), which was allied with Athens. The siege was a major military operation and a direct challenge to Athenian interests. The conflict over Perinthus escalated tensions between Philip and Athens and demonstrated Philip's willingness to directly confront Athenian power. The siege lasted several months and was ultimately unsuccessful, but it marked a major escalation in the conflict between Philip and the Greek city-states.
key_figures: Philip II of Macedon (King, r. -359 to -336)

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVI
Demosthenes, Third Philippic and other speeches

events.certainty_note: The siege of Perinthus is well-attested in ancient sources and is generally dated to 340 BCE. The basic facts of the siege are not disputed.
cross_references: Part of Philip's expansion into Greek affairs

events.title: Artaxerxes III dies; succession crisis in Persian Empire
events.description: Artaxerxes III Ochus died in 340 BCE (or possibly 338 BCE, sources differ), creating a succession crisis in the Persian Empire. The death of the aging Great King, who had spent his reign consolidating Persian power and suppressing rebellions, left the empire vulnerable to internal instability and external threats. The succession was contested, and the empire would experience a period of internal conflict and weakening before the arrival of Alexander the Great.
key_figures: Artaxerxes III Ochus (Persian Great King, r. -358 to -338 or -340)

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVI

events.certainty_note: The death of Artaxerxes III is attested in ancient sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Some sources place it in 338 BCE, others in 340 BCE. The dating is approximate within ±2 years.
cross_references: Precedes the succession of Arses (-338-001) or Darius III (-336-001)
Marks the beginning of the Persian Empire's decline

events.title: Qi state experiences military setback; decline accelerates
events.description: The state of Qi experienced a significant military setback, possibly against the state of Zhao or another rival power. This defeat marked the beginning of Qi's rapid decline from its position as one of the dominant Warring States powers. The military setback, combined with internal political instability, would lead to Qi's loss of power and influence in the following years.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The military setback of Qi is attested in Chinese sources, but the exact date and nature of the conflict are uncertain. The year 340 BCE is conventional; the setback may have occurred in 339 or 341 BCE.
cross_references: Part of Qi's decline from dominance
Precedes Qi's loss of power in the 320s

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Artaxerxes III's death is disputed; some sources place it in 338 BCE rather than 340 BCE. The exact nature and outcome of the siege of Perinthus are debated; some sources suggest it was more successful for Philip than others indicate. The characterization of Qi's military setback in 340 BCE is speculative; the sources do not provide a precise date or detailed account of the conflict. The significance of Demosthenes' Third Philippic is sometimes overstated; while it is a famous speech, it did not immediately mobilize Greek opposition to Philip.
historiographic_note: The year 340 BCE is moderately well-documented for the Mediterranean world through Greek sources, particularly Demosthenes' speeches and later historical accounts. However, these sources are biased toward Greek perspectives and often hostile to Persian interests. Egyptian sources are sparse. Chinese sources provide information about Warring States conflicts but are themselves compilations with editorial biases. The Indian subcontinent, Sub-Saharan Africa, and the Americas are absent from surviving records. The historical record reflects the preservation bias of literate, centralized states with written traditions.

The siege of Perinthus in 340 BCE escalated tensions that led to the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE
