era_context: Philip II continues his military expansion in the Balkans and begins to intervene in Greek affairs. The Persian Achaemenid Empire faces continued instability. The Greek city-states, particularly Athens and Thebes, remain powerful but increasingly threatened by Macedonian expansion. In China, the Warring States period continues.

events.title: Philip II intervenes in Thessalian affairs
events.description: Philip II begins to intervene in the internal conflicts of Thessaly, a region in central Greece. Thessaly is fragmented among competing aristocratic factions, and Philip exploits these divisions to extend Macedonian influence southward. This intervention marks the beginning of Philip's expansion into Greek affairs proper, moving beyond the Balkans. Thessaly's resources and strategic location make it valuable to Philip's ambitions.
events.certainty_note: Philip's intervention in Thessaly is attested in multiple sources, but the precise chronology is debated. Some sources place the beginning of Philip's Thessalian involvement in 357 or 356 BCE.

events.title: Third Sacred War begins in Greece
events.description: The Third Sacred War begins when Phocis seizes control of the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi and its treasury. This war will last approximately ten years and will involve most of the Greek city-states. The conflict provides Philip II with an opportunity to intervene in Greek affairs under the pretext of defending the sanctuary. The war is significant because it demonstrates the fragmentation of the Greek city-state system and creates the conditions for Macedonian dominance.
events.certainty_note: The Third Sacred War is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. The date of 357 BCE is widely accepted by modern scholarship.
cross_references: Leads to -346-001 (end of Third Sacred War)

events.title: Social War begins in Athens
events.description: The Social War (also called the Allies' War) begins when several members of the Athenian maritime confederacy rebel against Athenian control. The rebels include Rhodes, Chios, Cos, and Byzantium. The war reflects the decline of Athenian naval power and the unwillingness of allies to remain under Athenian domination. The conflict weakens Athens at a critical moment when Philip II is rising in the north, reducing Athens' ability to respond to the Macedonian threat.
events.certainty_note: The Social War is well-attested in ancient sources. The date of 357 BCE is widely accepted.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of the beginning of Philip's Thessalian intervention is debated, with some sources placing it in 356 BCE. The significance of the Third Sacred War is sometimes overstated; modern scholarship recognizes that it was one of several conflicts that weakened Greek unity. The Social War's impact on Athenian power is sometimes exaggerated; Athens remained a significant power despite the rebellion.
historiographic_note: Sources for 357 BCE are primarily Greek, with significant bias toward Athenian and Theban perspectives. Demosthenes' Philippics are contemporary primary sources but are rhetorical and politically motivated, not objective historical accounts. Later compilations by Diodorus and Pausanias are valuable but written centuries after the events. Persian and Egyptian sources are almost entirely absent from the record for this year. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military history.

graph_edges.note: Philip's Thessalian intervention continues to expand
Third Sacred War continues for approximately ten years
Social War continues for approximately two years
Both conflicts weaken Greek unity and create opportunities for Macedonian expansion

era_context: Philip II of Macedon consolidates power and begins his military expansion. The Persian Achaemenid Empire continues to face internal instability. Greek city-states remain fragmented and competitive. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing military innovation.

events.title: Philip II defeats the Illyrians and Paeonians
events.description: In his first year as king, Philip II achieves significant military victories against the Illyrians (under Bardylis) and the Paeonians, who had threatened Macedonian territory. These victories secure Macedon's northern frontier and establish Philip's military reputation. The victories are enabled by Philip's military reforms, including the development of the Macedonian phalanx and improved cavalry tactics. These early successes consolidate Philip's internal position and allow him to turn his attention southward toward Greek affairs.
events.certainty_note: The victories are attested in ancient sources, but precise dating within Philip's early reign (359–357 BCE) is uncertain. Assigned to 358 BCE by convention.

events.title: Nectanebo II consolidates Egyptian independence
events.description: Nectanebo II, the last native Egyptian pharaoh of the 30th Dynasty, consolidates his control over Egypt and maintains independence from Persian rule. He undertakes building projects and military preparations to defend against Persian reconquest. Nectanebo II's reign represents a brief resurgence of Egyptian independence and cultural vitality, though it will ultimately be short-lived as Persian forces will reconquer Egypt within a decade.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Egyptian temples and inscriptions

events.certainty_note: Nectanebo II's reign is attested archaeologically and in later historical compilations. The precise chronology of his reign is debated, with some uncertainty of ±2–3 years.
cross_references: Part of broader Egyptian independence narrative

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Philip II's early military campaigns is uncertain, with some sources placing key victories in 357 or 356 BCE rather than 358 BCE. The significance of these victories is sometimes overstated in older historiography; modern scholarship recognizes that Philip's consolidation of power was gradual and faced ongoing challenges. The extent of Nectanebo II's independence is debated; some scholars argue that Persian overlordship remained nominal rather than substantive.
historiographic_note: Sources for 358 BCE are primarily Greek and later Hellenistic compilations, with significant bias toward Macedonian and Greek affairs. Egyptian sources are fragmentary and often preserved only through later Greek writers. Persian sources are almost entirely lost, forcing reliance on Greek accounts of Persian affairs. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military history; economic and social developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Philip's early victories enable continued expansion
Part of Philip's broader campaign to dominate Greece

era_context: The mid-4th century BCE in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East is dominated by the rise of Macedon under Philip II, the decline of the Persian Achaemenid Empire under Artaxerxes III, and the fragmentation of Greek city-states. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing military sophistication. Egypt remains under Persian control but with recurring rebellions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia beyond Persian tributary networks

events.title: Philip II becomes King of Macedon
events.description: Philip II ascends to the throne of Macedon following the death of his brother Perdiccas III. At approximately 23 years old, Philip inherits a kingdom threatened by internal instability and external enemies (Illyrians, Thracians, and rival Greek city-states). He immediately begins military and administrative reforms that will transform Macedon from a peripheral Balkan kingdom into the dominant power of Greece within two decades. His reign marks the beginning of Macedonian hegemony and the eventual conquest of the Persian Empire under his son Alexander.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica (1st century BCE compilation of earlier sources)
Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus (2nd century CE)
Plutarch, Life of Alexander (1st–2nd century CE, contains references to Philip's reign)

events.certainty_note: The accession of Philip II in 359 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources, though no contemporary documents survive. The date is accepted by modern scholarship with high confidence.

events.title: Artaxerxes III campaigns in Egypt and Phoenicia
events.description: Artaxerxes III Ochus, the Achaemenid Persian king, conducts military campaigns to reassert control over rebellious Egyptian territories and Phoenician cities. Egypt had revolted under Nectanebo II, and Phoenician cities were increasingly independent. Artaxerxes III's campaigns are part of a broader effort to stabilize the Persian Empire's western provinces, though these efforts will ultimately prove temporary. The campaigns demonstrate both the military capability and the internal fragility of the late Achaemenid state.

sources.name: Manetho, Aegyptiaca (Egyptian history, preserved in later sources)

events.certainty_note: The campaigns of Artaxerxes III in this period are attested in multiple sources, but precise dating of individual campaigns within the 360s–350s BCE is uncertain. This entry is assigned to 359 BCE by convention, but the campaigns may have spanned several years.
cross_references: Part of broader Persian decline narrative
Contemporary with Philip II's rise in Macedon

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Philip II's accession has been debated by modern scholars. Some sources place it in 360 BCE rather than 359 BCE, with uncertainty of ±1 year. The traditional date of 359 BCE is widely accepted but not universally certain. Additionally, the significance of Artaxerxes III's campaigns is sometimes overstated in older historiography; modern scholarship recognizes that Persian control remained substantial despite rebellions.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 359 BCE is dominated by Greek and later Hellenistic sources, creating a Western-centric bias. Information about Macedon comes primarily from later compilations by Diodorus and Justin, not contemporary documents. Persian history relies heavily on Greek accounts of Persian affairs, which are often hostile or distorted. Egyptian sources are sparse and fragmentary. Chinese sources for this period (Warring States period) are better preserved but rarely cross-referenced with Western chronologies. The survival of sources is heavily skewed toward political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Philip II's accession enables his military reforms and victories in the following year
Philip's reign encompasses the birth of Alexander the Great
Artaxerxes III's campaigns are part of the broader Persian decline that accelerates toward Alexander's conquest

era_context: The Classical Greek world is fragmented following the Battle of Mantinea, with no single power dominating. Philip II of Macedon is consolidating power in the north and will soon emerge as a major player in Greek affairs. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is in its final years, facing succession challenges. The Roman Republic continues to consolidate power in central Italy. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing military competition.

events.title: Philip II consolidates power in Macedon
events.description: Philip II of Macedon, having recently assumed power (c. 359–360 BCE), begins a rapid consolidation of Macedonian authority. Philip reorganizes the Macedonian army, introducing new tactics and discipline that will make Macedon a major military power. He also engages in diplomatic maneuvering with Greek city-states and neighboring kingdoms. By 360 BCE, Philip is establishing himself as a significant player in regional politics, though he is not yet a dominant force in broader Greek affairs. His rise will eventually lead to Macedonian conquest of Greece and the creation of the Hellenistic world.

sources.name: Plutarch, Life of Alexander (contains information about Philip)

events.certainty_note: Philip II's consolidation of power is well-attested in later sources, though the exact chronology of events in 360 BCE is not always clear.

events.title: Satraps' Revolt concludes in Persian Empire
events.description: The Satraps' Revolt against Artaxerxes II reaches its conclusion around 360 BCE (exact dating disputed). Multiple provincial governors who had rebelled against the Great King's authority are defeated or submit. The suppression of the revolt demonstrates the continued military power of the Persian Empire, but also reveals the internal instability that will plague the empire in the coming decades. The exact chronology of the revolt is disputed among modern scholars, with some sources placing the peak of the rebellion earlier (c. 366 BCE) and others later (c. 360 BCE).
events.certainty_note: The Satraps' Revolt is well-attested, but its precise chronology is disputed among modern scholars. Different sources place the rebellion's conclusion between 366 and 360 BCE.

events.title: Artaxerxes II Mnemon in final years of reign
events.description: Artaxerxes II Mnemon, who has ruled the Persian Empire since 405 BCE, is now in his final years (he will die in 358 BCE). The aging Great King faces succession challenges and internal instability. By 360 BCE, the question of succession is becoming increasingly pressing, as Artaxerxes II has multiple sons and the succession is not clearly established. The empire remains stable, but the transition of power will be contested.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Artaxerxes II is well-documented, but the exact timing of succession challenges is uncertain.

events.title: Roman expansion in central Italy
events.description: The Roman Republic continues its gradual expansion and consolidation of power in central Italy. By 360 BCE, Rome has secured dominance over the Latin League and is beginning to extend influence into Campania. The internal conflict between patricians and plebeians continues, with the plebeian class gradually gaining political rights. Rome is not yet a major Mediterranean power but is establishing the military and administrative structures that will enable later expansion.
events.certainty_note: Roman history of this period is known primarily through Livy, whose sources are now lost. Specific events of 360 BCE are difficult to verify independently.

events.title: Warring States period in China continues
events.description: During the Warring States period (475–221 BCE), the Chinese world is fragmented into competing kingdoms. By 360 BCE, states such as Qi, Chu, Zhao, and others are engaged in ongoing military and diplomatic competition. The period is marked by increasing military sophistication, administrative development, and philosophical ferment. Specific events of 360 BCE are not well-attested in surviving sources.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for this period are preserved in later compilations. Specific dating of events within the Warring States period is often uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 360 BCE as a turning point in Greek history is sometimes overstated. While Philip II's consolidation of power is important, his dominance of Greece is not yet apparent in 360 BCE; it will take decades for Macedon to become the dominant Greek power. The Satraps' Revolt's chronology is disputed; some scholars place its conclusion earlier or later than 360 BCE. The rise of Philip II is sometimes portrayed as inevitable in later sources, but in 360 BCE, his future dominance is not yet certain.
historiographic_note: Sources for 360 BCE are primarily Greek and Persian accounts preserved in later compilations. The year 360 BCE is significant primarily as the year when Philip II consolidates power in Macedon, but the full implications of this consolidation are not apparent in contemporary sources. The Satraps' Revolt's chronology is disputed among modern scholars, making the exact status of this rebellion in 360 BCE uncertain. Roman and Chinese sources for this year are less detailed. The year 360 BCE marks a turning point in Greek history, but the broader implications of this turning point are debated among modern scholars.

graph_edges.note: Philip II's consolidation of power leads to Macedonian dominance of Greece at the Battle of Chaeronea
The conclusion of the Satraps' Revolt is followed by succession crisis upon Artaxerxes II's death
Artaxerxes II's final years lead to succession crisis

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the aftermath of the Battle of Mantinea and the death of Epaminondas. No single Greek power dominates; Athens, Sparta, and Thebes compete for influence while remaining weakened by decades of warfare. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II faces succession challenges as the aging king approaches the end of his reign. The Roman Republic continues to consolidate power in central Italy. In China, the Warring States period continues.

events.title: Greek city-states compete for influence after Mantinea
events.description: In the year following the Battle of Mantinea, Greek city-states compete for influence in a fragmented political landscape. Athens, Sparta, and Thebes all seek to maintain or expand their power, but none is strong enough to dominate. This period is characterized by shifting alliances, diplomatic maneuvering, and ongoing military conflicts. The power vacuum created by Theban decline will eventually be filled by Macedon, but in 361 BCE, Macedon is not yet a major player in Greek politics.
events.certainty_note: The general political situation is well-attested, but specific events of 361 BCE are not always clearly distinguished from adjacent years.

events.title: Artaxerxes II Mnemon approaches end of reign
events.description: Artaxerxes II Mnemon, who has ruled the Persian Empire since 405 BCE, is now in his final years. The aging Great King faces succession challenges and internal instability. By 361 BCE, the question of succession is becoming increasingly pressing, as Artaxerxes II has multiple sons and the succession is not clearly established. The empire remains stable, but the transition of power will be contested.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Artaxerxes II is well-documented, but the exact timing of succession challenges is uncertain.

events.title: Philip II of Macedon begins consolidation of power
events.description: Philip II becomes king of Macedon around 359 BCE (exact date disputed) and begins a rapid consolidation of power. By 361 BCE, Philip is establishing himself as a major player in Macedonian politics, though he is not yet a significant force in broader Greek affairs. Philip's rise will eventually lead to Macedonian dominance of Greece and the creation of the Hellenistic world, but in 361 BCE, this future is not yet apparent.

sources.name: Plutarch, Life of Alexander (contains information about Philip)

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Philip II's accession to the throne is disputed, with sources ranging from 360 to 359 BCE. The events of 361 BCE in Macedon are not clearly attested.

events.title: Roman expansion in central Italy continues
events.description: The Roman Republic continues its gradual expansion and consolidation of power in central Italy. By 361 BCE, Rome has secured dominance over the Latin League and is beginning to extend influence into Campania. The internal conflict between patricians and plebeians continues, with the plebeian class gradually gaining political rights.
events.certainty_note: Roman history of this period is known primarily through Livy, whose sources are now lost. Specific events of 361 BCE are difficult to verify.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 361 BCE as a distinct year in Greek history is minimal; it is primarily significant as the year after Mantinea. The rise of Philip II is sometimes portrayed as inevitable in later sources, but in 361 BCE, Philip's future dominance is not yet apparent. The exact date of Philip's accession to the throne is disputed among modern scholars.
historiographic_note: Sources for 361 BCE are primarily Greek and Persian accounts preserved in later compilations. The year 361 BCE is not particularly well-attested in any surviving source tradition; much of what is known about this year is inferred from broader patterns in the surrounding decades. The significance of this year lies primarily in what it leads to (the rise of Macedon) rather than in events that occurred during the year itself.

graph_edges.note: Greek political fragmentation continues until Macedon emerges as a dominant power
Artaxerxes II's approaching death leads to succession crisis
Philip II's consolidation of power leads to Macedonian dominance of Greece

era_context: The Classical Greek world reaches a turning point with the Battle of Mantinea, which effectively ends Theban hegemony and ushers in a period of Greek political fragmentation. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II continues to face internal challenges. The Roman Republic is consolidating power in central Italy. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing military sophistication.

events.title: Battle of Mantinea
events.description: The Battle of Mantinea in 362 BCE is one of the most significant military engagements of the Classical Greek world. Theban forces under Epaminondas face a coalition of Greek city-states (Athens, Sparta, and others) seeking to check Theban hegemony. The battle is tactically decisive for Thebes, but Epaminondas is killed in the fighting. His death marks a turning point: Theban hegemony effectively ends, and no single Greek power emerges to replace it. The battle is often seen as marking the end of the Classical period and the beginning of the Hellenistic age, though this periodization is debated.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Mantinea is one of the best-attested events of the Classical Greek world, with contemporary accounts from Xenophon and later detailed accounts from Plutarch and Diodorus.

events.title: Death of Epaminondas
events.description: Epaminondas, the greatest Theban general and statesman of the Classical period, dies at the Battle of Mantinea. His death marks the end of an era of Theban dominance in Greek politics. Epaminondas had revolutionized Greek military tactics (the oblique phalanx) and had briefly made Thebes the leading power in Greece. With his death, no successor of comparable ability emerges, and Theban power rapidly declines. The death of Epaminondas is often seen as symbolically marking the end of the Classical Greek world.
events.certainty_note: The death of Epaminondas at Mantinea is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Artaxerxes II continues to rule Persian Empire
events.description: Artaxerxes II Mnemon remains the Great King of Persia, having ruled since 405 BCE. By 362 BCE, the Satraps' Revolt has been suppressed (or is in its final stages), and the Persian Empire continues to dominate the Near East. However, the empire faces ongoing challenges from internal instability and the rise of Macedon in the north.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Artaxerxes II is well-documented, but specific events of 362 BCE are not always clearly attested.

events.title: Roman consolidation continues in central Italy
events.description: The Roman Republic continues its gradual expansion and consolidation of power in central Italy. By 362 BCE, Rome has secured dominance over the Latin League and is beginning to extend influence into Campania. The internal conflict between patricians and plebeians continues, with the plebeian class gradually gaining political rights. Rome is not yet a major Mediterranean power but is establishing the military and administrative structures that will enable later expansion.
events.certainty_note: Roman history of this period is known primarily through Livy, whose sources are now lost. Specific events of 362 BCE are difficult to verify independently.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Battle of Mantinea as a 'turning point' in Greek history is sometimes overstated. While it did end Theban hegemony, it did not immediately usher in a new era; Greek politics remained fragmented for decades. The death of Epaminondas is sometimes portrayed as more symbolically significant than it was politically; Theban power had already begun to decline before his death. Some modern scholars argue that the periodization of Classical/Hellenistic is misleading and that the transition was more gradual than the traditional narrative suggests.
historiographic_note: The Battle of Mantinea is one of the best-documented events of the Classical Greek world, with contemporary accounts from Xenophon. However, later sources (Plutarch, Diodorus) add details and interpretations that may not be historically accurate. The Greek sources reflect Athenian and Spartan perspectives and may underestimate Theban achievements. The significance of Epaminondas is sometimes exaggerated in later sources that emphasize individual great men. Roman and Chinese sources for this year are less detailed. The year 362 BCE is a major turning point in Greek history, but the broader implications of this turning point are debated among modern scholars.

graph_edges.note: The power vacuum left by Theban decline is filled by the rise of Macedon under Philip II
Epaminondas' death occurs at the Battle of Mantinea
The Battle of Mantinea reverses the Theban dominance established at Leuctra in 371 BCE

era_context: The Classical Greek world continues under Theban hegemony, with Epaminondas at the height of his power. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II faces internal challenges from satrap rebellions. The Roman Republic is consolidating control in central Italy. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing military competition.

events.title: Epaminondas leads Theban expedition into the Peloponnese
events.description: Epaminondas continues Theban military campaigns in the Peloponnese, seeking to maintain Theban dominance and prevent the re-emergence of Spartan or Athenian power. These campaigns involve complex diplomatic and military maneuvering among Greek city-states. The exact sequence of events in 363 BCE is difficult to establish from surviving sources, but Theban military activity in this region is well-attested for this general period.
events.certainty_note: Theban military activity in the Peloponnese during this period is attested, but the specific events of 363 BCE are not always clearly distinguished from adjacent years.

events.title: Satraps' Revolt against Artaxerxes II reaches critical phase
events.description: The rebellion of Persian satraps against the central authority of Artaxerxes II intensifies during this period. Multiple provincial governors, including the satrap of Egypt and others in Anatolia and Syria, rebel against the Great King's authority. The revolt is eventually suppressed, but the exact chronology is disputed among modern scholars, with some sources placing the peak of the rebellion in 363 BCE and others in adjacent years.
events.certainty_note: The Satraps' Revolt is well-attested, but its precise chronology is disputed. Different sources place the rebellion's peak between 366 and 360 BCE.

events.title: Roman military campaigns in Latium and Campania
events.description: The Roman Republic continues its military consolidation of central Italy. Campaigns against neighboring peoples and city-states are ongoing, though specific events of 363 BCE are not clearly attested in surviving sources. Rome's military organization and tactics are gradually becoming more sophisticated, laying the groundwork for later expansion.
events.certainty_note: Roman history of this period is known primarily through Livy, whose sources are now lost. Specific events of 363 BCE are difficult to verify.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of the Satraps' Revolt is highly disputed. Some modern scholars argue that the rebellion did not reach its peak until 360 BCE or later, making the significance of 363 BCE uncertain. The role of Epaminondas in Greek politics is sometimes exaggerated in later sources that emphasize individual great men over structural political changes.
historiographic_note: Sources for 363 BCE are primarily Greek and Persian accounts preserved in later compilations. The Greek sources often reflect Athenian biases and may overemphasize Theban military activity. Persian sources are known only through Greek intermediaries. Roman history relies entirely on Livy. Chinese sources are similarly late compilations. The year 363 BCE is not particularly well-attested in any surviving source tradition; much of what is known about this year is inferred from broader patterns in the surrounding decades.

graph_edges.note: Theban military campaigns continue until the Battle of Mantinea
Satraps' Revolt continues and eventually leads to succession crisis

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-4th century BCE, dominated by the aftermath of the Corinthian War and the rise of Theban hegemony under Epaminondas. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II remains the dominant Near Eastern power. In Italy, the early Roman Republic is consolidating control over the Latin League. In China, the Warring States period continues with increasing interstate competition.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek/Roman contact zones
Mesoamerica (Maya city-states exist but Greek/Roman sources are absent)

events.title: Theban hegemony consolidates after Leuctra
events.description: Following the decisive Theban victory at Leuctra in 371 BCE, Theban power under Epaminondas continues to dominate Greek interstate relations. By 364 BCE, Thebes has established itself as the leading military power in Greece, having broken Spartan hegemony and challenged Athenian influence. This period sees Theban intervention in the Peloponnese and attempts to create a stable balance of power, though tensions with Athens and other city-states remain high.
events.certainty_note: The broad political situation is well-attested, though specific events of 364 BCE are not always clearly distinguished from adjacent years in surviving sources.

events.title: Artaxerxes II continues Persian rule in the Near East
events.description: Artaxerxes II Mnemon remains the Great King of Persia, having ruled since 405 BCE. By 364 BCE, his reign has seen the suppression of the Satraps' Revolt (c. 366–360 BCE), though this conflict is ongoing or recently concluded depending on chronological sources. The Persian Empire maintains control over vast territories from Egypt to the Indus, though internal instability and satrap rebellions periodically challenge central authority.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Artaxerxes II is well-documented in Greek sources, but precise dating of internal Persian events is often uncertain.

events.title: Roman consolidation in Latium and early Campania
events.description: The early Roman Republic continues its gradual expansion and consolidation of power in central Italy. By 364 BCE, Rome has secured dominance over the Latin League and is beginning to extend influence into Campania. Internal conflicts between patricians and plebeians continue, with the plebeian class gradually gaining political rights. Rome is not yet a major Mediterranean power but is establishing the military and administrative structures that will enable later expansion.
key_figures: Roman consuls of 364 BCE (names uncertain in surviving records)
events.certainty_note: Roman history of this period is known primarily through Livy, writing centuries later. Specific events of 364 BCE are difficult to verify independently.

events.title: Warring States period intensifies in China
events.description: During the Warring States period (475–221 BCE), the Chinese world is fragmented into competing kingdoms. By 364 BCE, states such as Qi, Chu, Zhao, and others are engaged in ongoing military and diplomatic competition. The period is marked by increasing military sophistication, administrative development, and philosophical ferment (the era of Confucius, Laozi, and other thinkers has passed, but their legacies shape statecraft). Specific events of 364 BCE are not well-attested in surviving sources.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for this period are preserved in later compilations. Specific dating of events within the Warring States period is often uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Satraps' Revolt is disputed among scholars; some sources place it earlier (c. 366 BCE) or later (c. 360 BCE), making the status of this rebellion in 364 BCE uncertain. The significance of Theban hegemony is sometimes overstated in Greek sources that favor Athenian or Spartan perspectives; modern scholarship emphasizes that Theban dominance was brief and contested. Roman expansion in this period is often presented as inevitable in later sources, but archaeological evidence suggests a more gradual and contested process.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 364 BCE is heavily weighted toward Greek and Persian sources preserved in later compilations. Greek sources often reflect Athenian or Spartan biases. Roman history of this period relies almost entirely on Livy, whose sources are now lost and whose accuracy for the 4th century BCE is debated. Chinese sources are similarly late compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially absent from surviving written records of this year. The 'great man' narrative (Epaminondas, Artaxerxes II) dominates surviving accounts, potentially obscuring broader social and economic changes.

Persian internal instability contributes to the Satraps' Revolt and eventual succession crisis
Roman consolidation in Italy sets the stage for later expansion into Campania and southern Italy

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-4th century BCE, dominated by the rivalry between Sparta and Thebes following the Theban hegemony established after Leuctra (371 BCE). Philip II of Macedon was consolidating power in the north. The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Artaxerxes II remained the dominant Near Eastern power, though facing internal instability. In China, the Warring States period was intensifying, with major states consolidating territory and military innovation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek/Roman contact zones
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited Greek sources)

events.title: Theban hegemony consolidates; Pelopidas leads Theban expansion
events.description: Following the Theban victory at Leuctra in 371 BCE, Pelopidas and Epaminondas continued to expand Theban influence across Greece. In 365 BCE, Pelopidas led a military campaign into Thessaly to secure Theban dominance in northern Greece and counter Macedonian expansion. This period saw Thebes at its zenith as a Greek power, though internal tensions and the rise of Philip II would soon undermine this hegemony.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon's account is near-contemporary but ends around 362 BCE. Later sources like Plutarch provide narrative detail but are written centuries later. The general trajectory of Theban expansion is secure; specific events in 365 BCE are less precisely dated.
cross_references: -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

events.title: Artaxerxes II faces internal rebellion; Egyptian revolt continues
events.description: The reign of Artaxerxes II (ruled 404–358 BCE) was marked by chronic instability. In 365 BCE, Egypt remained in open revolt under native pharaohs (the 30th Dynasty), having thrown off Persian control around 380 BCE. Simultaneously, Artaxerxes faced rebellions from satraps and dynastic rivals. The empire's military capacity, though still formidable, was increasingly stretched. This period foreshadowed the empire's vulnerability to Macedonian conquest under Alexander.

sources.name: Egyptian temple inscriptions and king lists

events.certainty_note: Egyptian independence is well-attested epigraphically. The dating of specific Persian internal crises in 365 BCE is less precise; Greek sources are our main record and are sometimes anecdotal.

events.title: Philip II of Macedon consolidates northern Greece
events.description: Philip II, who became king of Macedon around 359 BCE, was rapidly transforming Macedon from a peripheral kingdom into a major power. By 365 BCE, he had secured his throne against rivals and was beginning military campaigns to expand Macedonian territory and influence. His military reforms—the sarissa phalanx and professional standing army—were revolutionary. This period marks the beginning of Macedonian ascendancy that would culminate in Alexander's conquests.
events.certainty_note: Philip's reign is well-documented in later Greek and Roman sources, but contemporary accounts are sparse. The chronology of his early campaigns is sometimes uncertain by a few years.

events.title: Warring States period intensifies in China; Qin and Chu expand
events.description: During the Warring States period (475–221 BCE), the major Chinese states were engaged in continuous military competition and territorial consolidation. Around 365 BCE, the state of Qin in the west and Chu in the south were expanding their power. Qin was beginning the military and administrative reforms under Shang Yang (who served the state of Wei before moving to Qin around 361 BCE) that would eventually enable Qin's unification of China. This era saw rapid innovation in military technology, statecraft, and philosophy.
key_figures: Shang Yang (Legalist reformer, active in Wei and Qin)
Various rulers of Qin, Chu, Zhao, and other states

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Archaeological evidence from Warring States sites

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical chronology for the Warring States period is uncertain by several years. The Shiji was compiled centuries later (c. 109 BCE). Archaeological evidence provides material confirmation but not precise dating for individual years.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of Pelopidas' Thessalian campaigns is debated; some scholars place key events in 364 or 366 BCE rather than 365 BCE. The chronology of Philip II's early reign is also uncertain—some sources suggest his consolidation of power took longer than traditional accounts indicate. For China, the Warring States chronology is notoriously uncertain; the Bamboo Annals and Shiji sometimes disagree by several years on the same events. No major events listed above are fundamentally disputed, but their precise dating should be treated as approximate.
historiographic_note: The year 365 BCE is moderately well-documented for the Greek world through later compilations (Plutarch, Diodorus, Xenophon's Hellenica), though contemporary sources are limited. For Persia and Egypt, we rely on Greek accounts and Egyptian temple inscriptions, which may be biased or incomplete. Chinese sources are entirely post-hoc compilations with uncertain chronology. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have no surviving written records for this year. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East.

graph_edges.note: Theban hegemony under Pelopidas and Epaminondas culminated in the Battle of Mantinea (362 BCE), where Epaminondas died and Theban dominance ended.
Philip II's consolidation of Macedonian power in the 360s–350s BCE led to his victory at Chaeronea (338 BCE), which made Macedon the dominant Greek power.
The Egyptian revolt and Persian instability in the 360s BCE eventually led to Artaxerxes III's reconquest of Egypt in 343 BCE.
The intensification of Warring States competition in China was contemporary with Shang Yang's arrival in Qin and the beginning of his reforms.

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-4th century BCE, with Thebes at the height of its power following the victory at Leuctra (371 BCE). Sparta's hegemony had been broken, but no single Greek power had yet achieved lasting dominance. Philip II of Macedon was in the early stages of consolidating his kingdom. The Persian Achaemenid Empire remained vast but internally unstable. In China, the Warring States period was in full swing, with major states competing for supremacy.

events.title: Pelopidas killed in battle at Cynoscephalae; Theban power begins to decline
events.description: Pelopidas, the great Theban general and statesman, was killed in battle against the Thessalian tyrant Alexander of Pherae at Cynoscephalae in 366 BCE (some sources date this to 364 BCE). His death marked a turning point in Theban fortunes. Although Epaminondas remained alive and continued to lead Theban forces, Pelopidas' death symbolized the beginning of the end for Theban hegemony. The loss of this charismatic leader weakened Theban political cohesion.
events.certainty_note: Pelopidas' death is well-attested in multiple sources, but the precise date is disputed. Plutarch and Diodorus place it in 366 BCE, while some modern scholars argue for 364 BCE based on chronological reconstruction. The event itself is confirmed.
cross_references: -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

events.title: Artaxerxes II continues reign amid Persian instability
events.description: Artaxerxes II (ruled 404–358 BCE) was in the middle of his long reign, which was characterized by internal rebellions, satrapal revolts, and the loss of Egypt. In 366 BCE, the empire was stable enough militarily but faced chronic administrative challenges. The reign saw the rise of powerful eunuchs and court factions, which would later destabilize the succession. Despite these internal problems, the Achaemenid Empire remained the largest and most powerful state in the Near East.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Artaxerxes II's reign is well-known from Greek sources, but specific events in 366 BCE are not well-documented. Archaeological evidence confirms the empire's continued operation but does not provide year-specific information.

events.title: Philip II consolidates Macedonian power; early military campaigns
events.description: Philip II, who became king of Macedon around 359 BCE, was in the early years of his reign and was consolidating his power against internal rivals and external threats. By 366 BCE, he had secured his position and was beginning to expand Macedonian territory. His military innovations—the sarissa phalanx and professional standing army—were being developed during this period. Philip was also establishing diplomatic and dynastic ties with neighboring powers.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Philip's early reign is uncertain. Sources disagree on the dates of specific campaigns and consolidation efforts. The general trajectory is clear, but year-specific events are difficult to pin down.

events.title: Warring States period: Qin and Chu continue expansion
events.description: During the Warring States period, the major Chinese states continued their competition for supremacy. Around 366 BCE, Qin was beginning to emerge as a major power in the west, while Chu dominated the south. The state of Zhao in the north was also expanding. This period saw continuous military innovation, including improvements in crossbow technology and chariot tactics. Philosophical schools like Legalism and Confucianism were developing in response to the chaos of the era.
key_figures: Various rulers of Qin, Chu, Zhao, and other states

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Archaeological evidence from Warring States sites

events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology for the Warring States period is uncertain by several years. The Shiji was compiled centuries later. Archaeological evidence provides material confirmation but not precise year-by-year dating.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Pelopidas' death is disputed. While Plutarch and Diodorus place it in 366 BCE, some modern scholars (based on chronological reconstruction) argue for 364 BCE. This is a significant uncertainty. The chronology of Philip II's early reign is also debated; some sources suggest different dates for his consolidation of power. For China, the Warring States chronology is notoriously uncertain, with the Bamboo Annals and Shiji sometimes disagreeing by several years.
historiographic_note: The year 366 BCE is moderately well-documented for the Greek world, primarily through later compilations. Contemporary sources are limited. For Persia, we rely on Greek accounts and archaeological evidence. Chinese sources are entirely post-hoc compilations with uncertain chronology. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have no surviving written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Pelopidas' death weakened Theban leadership, contributing to the decline of Theban hegemony that culminated at Mantinea (362 BCE).
Philip II's consolidation of Macedonian power in the 360s BCE led to his dominance of Greece by 338 BCE.
The Warring States competition in China was contemporary with the rise of Legalist thought and military innovation.

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-4th century BCE, with Thebes at the height of its power following Leuctra (371 BCE). Sparta's hegemony had been broken, but Theban dominance was not yet secure. Philip II of Macedon was consolidating his kingdom in the north. The Persian Achaemenid Empire remained vast but internally unstable. In China, the Warring States period was in full swing.

events.title: Theban hegemony at its peak; Epaminondas leads military campaigns
events.description: In 367 BCE, Thebes under Epaminondas was at the height of its power. Epaminondas was consolidating Theban control over Boeotia and extending influence throughout Greece. The Theban hegemony, established after the victory at Leuctra in 371 BCE, was still secure, though it would face increasing challenges from Philip II of Macedon and internal Greek rivalries. This period represents the brief moment when Thebes was the dominant Greek power.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon's Hellenica provides near-contemporary evidence, though it ends around 362 BCE. Later sources like Plutarch and Diodorus provide narrative detail but are written centuries later. The general trajectory of Theban power is secure; specific events in 367 BCE are less precisely dated.
cross_references: -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

events.title: Plato visits Syracuse; philosophical influence on Dionysius II
events.description: Plato, the Athenian philosopher, made his third and final visit to Syracuse in Sicily around 367 BCE (some sources date this to 361 BCE). He came at the invitation of Dionysius II, the young tyrant of Syracuse, hoping to influence him toward philosophical governance. The visit was ultimately unsuccessful and ended in conflict; Dionysius II eventually had Plato imprisoned or enslaved (accounts vary). This episode illustrates both the appeal of Platonic philosophy to rulers and the practical difficulties of implementing philosophical ideals in politics.
events.certainty_note: Plato's Seventh Letter is autobiographical and provides first-hand evidence, though its authenticity has been debated by scholars. The date of the visit is uncertain; some sources place it in 361 BCE. The general outline of the visit and its failure is well-attested.

events.title: Philip II of Macedon continues consolidation and expansion
events.description: Philip II was in the early-to-middle years of his reign, consolidating Macedonian power and beginning to expand northward and eastward. By 367 BCE, he had secured his throne against internal rivals and was establishing Macedon as a major regional power. His military reforms were being implemented, and he was beginning to pose a threat to the Greek city-states to the south. This period marks the beginning of Macedonian ascendancy.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Philip's early reign is uncertain. Sources disagree on the dates of specific campaigns. The general trajectory is clear, but year-specific events are difficult to pin down.

events.title: Warring States period: military innovation and state consolidation in China
events.description: During the Warring States period, Chinese states were engaged in continuous military competition and technological innovation. Around 367 BCE, crossbow technology was being refined, and chariot tactics were being superseded by infantry formations. The state of Qin in the west was beginning to emerge as a major power. Philosophical schools like Legalism were developing in response to the chaos of the era, emphasizing strict laws and military discipline.

The chronology of Philip II's early reign is also debated. For China, the Warring States chronology is notoriously uncertain.

The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have no surviving written records for this year.

Thebes was at the height of its power, though the rise of Philip II of Macedon in the north was beginning to pose a challenge to Greek independence.

events.title: Artaxerxes II continues reign; Persian Empire remains dominant but unstable
events.description: Artaxerxes II (ruled 404–358 BCE) was in the middle of his long reign. In 368 BCE, the Persian Empire remained the largest and most powerful state in the Near East, but it faced chronic internal instability. Egypt remained in revolt, and various satraps and dynastic rivals posed challenges to central authority. Despite these problems, the empire's military and administrative apparatus remained formidable.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Artaxerxes II's reign is well-known from Greek sources, but specific events in 368 BCE are not well-documented. Archaeological evidence confirms the empire's continued operation but does not provide year-specific information.

events.title: Theban hegemony at its height; Epaminondas dominates Greek politics
events.description: In 369 BCE, Thebes under Epaminondas was at the height of its power and influence. Epaminondas had consolidated Theban control over Boeotia and was the dominant figure in Greek politics. The Theban hegemony, established after Leuctra in 371 BCE, was secure and at its peak. Thebes was the most powerful Greek city-state, though the rise of Philip II of Macedon in the north was beginning to pose a challenge to Greek independence.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon's Hellenica provides near-contemporary evidence. Later sources provide narrative detail but are written centuries later. The general trajectory of Theban power is secure; specific events in 369 BCE are less precisely dated.

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, dominated by the aftermath of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) and the fragile King's Peace. Sparta remained the leading Greek power but faced internal instability and challenges from Thebes. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II was consolidating control after the Anabasis and internal revolts. In the East, the Warring States period in China was intensifying, with the state of Chu expanding. Egypt under the 30th Dynasty was experiencing a brief renaissance of independence from Persian rule.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek/Roman contact zones
Mesoamerica (no contemporary written records in Greek/Roman sources)

events.title: Theban hegemony consolidates after victory at Leuctra
events.description: Following Epaminondas' decisive victory over Sparta at Leuctra in 371 BCE, Theban power continued to expand throughout the 370s. By 370 BCE, Thebes had become the dominant force in Greece, having liberated Messenia from Spartan control and established hegemony over much of central Greece. This shift fundamentally altered the balance of power that had existed since the Peloponnesian War, with Thebes replacing Sparta as the leading Greek city-state. The Theban ascendancy would prove brief but transformative for Greek interstate relations.
events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Theban ascendancy is well-attested in contemporary sources. Specific events within 370 BCE are less precisely dated but fall within the documented period of Theban hegemony (371–362 BCE).
cross_references: -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

events.title: Liberation of Messenia from Spartan control
events.description: In the aftermath of Leuctra, Epaminondas led a Theban army into the Peloponnese, where he orchestrated the liberation of Messenia from Spartan domination. The Messenians, who had been subjugated as helots (serfs) for nearly three centuries, were freed and their territory was reorganized as an independent state. This was a catastrophic blow to Spartan power, as Messenia had been the economic foundation of the Spartan system. The liberation was formalized with the establishment of the city of Messene as the capital, which was fortified and populated by returning exiles.
events.certainty_note: The liberation of Messenia is well-documented in ancient sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence of the city's founding and fortification walls.

events.title: Artaxerxes II consolidates Persian control after internal revolts
events.description: By 370 BCE, Artaxerxes II had successfully suppressed the major internal revolts that had threatened Persian stability in the previous decade, including the revolt of his brother Cyrus the Younger (401 BCE) and subsequent satrapal rebellions. The empire was now entering a period of relative stability, though chronic problems of satrapal autonomy and succession disputes remained. The King's Peace (387 BCE) had established Persian hegemony over the Greek city-states of Asia Minor, and this arrangement was being maintained through diplomatic and military means.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Artaxerxes II's reign is well-documented, but specific events within 370 BCE are less precisely dated. The consolidation of control is inferred from the absence of major recorded revolts in this period.

events.title: Nectanebo I rules Egypt with renewed independence
events.description: Nectanebo I (r. 380–362 BCE), founder of the 30th Dynasty, was consolidating Egyptian independence from Persian rule during this period. He had expelled the Persians and established a native Egyptian dynasty, initiating a period of cultural and economic renaissance. Nectanebo was an active builder and military commander, strengthening Egypt's defenses and restoring traditional religious practices. This period represents one of the last flourishings of pharaonic Egypt before the final Persian conquest in 343 BCE.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from temples and fortifications

events.certainty_note: Nectanebo I's reign dates are well-established through Egyptian king lists and temple inscriptions. His role in expelling the Persians and establishing the 30th Dynasty is confirmed by multiple sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of events within 370 BCE is uncertain; ancient sources often group events by regnal year or by campaign rather than by Julian calendar year. The significance attributed to Theban hegemony has been somewhat revised by modern scholarship, which emphasizes that Theban power was more fragile and contested than earlier historiography suggested. Some scholars argue that Sparta's decline was more gradual and less catastrophic than the traditional narrative implies. The extent of Messenia's immediate economic recovery after liberation is debated—some evidence suggests the region remained economically marginal for decades.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 370 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica, which is contemporary but reflects an Athenian perspective and has gaps in coverage. Persian sources are largely lost, surviving only in later Greek compilations. Egyptian sources are primarily epigraphic and archaeological, lacking the narrative detail of Greek texts. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. The absence of sources from sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Mesoamerica means that significant developments in those regions are entirely absent from this account.

Liberation of Messenia is a consequence of Theban hegemony
Theban hegemony eventually provokes the coalition that leads to Mantinea

era_context: The Classical Greek world at a pivotal moment: the Battle of Leuctra in this year marks the end of Spartan hegemony and the beginning of Theban ascendancy. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II was stable but facing chronic satrapal challenges. In China, the Warring States period was intensifying, with major states consolidating power. Egypt under Nectanebo I was experiencing renewed independence and cultural flourishing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from Persian interior (beyond Greek sources)

events.title: Battle of Leuctra: Theban victory over Sparta
events.description: On a date traditionally placed in summer 371 BCE (exact date uncertain, possibly July), Epaminondas led a Theban and allied Boeotian army to a decisive victory over the Spartan hoplite phalanx near the village of Leuctra in Boeotia. The Theban force, numbering approximately 6,000–7,000 hoplites, defeated a Spartan army of similar size commanded by King Cleombrotus I. The battle was revolutionary in its tactics: Epaminondas deployed a deep phalanx on the left wing (the 'Sacred Band' of elite Theban warriors) while weakening his right, concentrating overwhelming force at the point of contact. This broke the myth of Spartan invincibility and marked the beginning of the end of Spartan hegemony in Greece. Cleombrotus I was killed in the battle.
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date is traditionally given as 371 BCE, though some scholars propose 370 BCE. The tactical details are well-documented, though some specifics (exact troop numbers, precise positioning) vary between sources.
cross_references: -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

events.title: Death of Cleombrotus I of Sparta
events.description: Cleombrotus I, one of the two kings of Sparta, was killed in the Battle of Leuctra. His death marked a symbolic end to Spartan military dominance and contributed to the political instability that would plague Sparta in subsequent decades. The succession passed to his son Agesilaus II, but the loss of prestige and military power was irreversible. Cleombrotus' death is often cited as the moment when Sparta's position as the leading Greek power definitively ended.
events.certainty_note: The death of Cleombrotus I at Leuctra is well-attested in ancient sources.

events.title: Theban Sacred Band emerges as elite military unit
events.description: The Sacred Band of Thebes, an elite unit of 300 hoplites composed of pairs of lovers (according to tradition), played a crucial role in the victory at Leuctra. While the unit may have existed earlier, it gained prominence and fame through this battle. The Sacred Band represented a new model of military organization based on personal bonds and intensive training rather than aristocratic birth. The unit would remain a formidable force in Greek warfare until its destruction at the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The existence and role of the Sacred Band at Leuctra is attested in ancient sources, though the details of its organization and the nature of the bonds between members are debated by modern scholars. Some sources may be romanticizing or projecting later ideals onto the unit.
cross_references: -338-001 (Battle of Chaeronea, destruction of Sacred Band)

events.title: Continued consolidation of Nectanebo I's rule in Egypt
events.description: Nectanebo I continued to consolidate his rule and expel remaining Persian influence from Egypt. He initiated major building projects, including temples and fortifications, as part of a broader program of cultural and economic renewal. The 30th Dynasty under Nectanebo represented a genuine renaissance of pharaonic power and Egyptian cultural identity after decades of Persian domination.
events.certainty_note: Nectanebo I's reign and building activities are well-documented in Egyptian sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence.

events.title: Artaxerxes II maintains Persian hegemony over Greek Asia Minor
events.description: Following the King's Peace of 387 BCE, Artaxerxes II maintained Persian control over the Greek city-states of the Ionian coast and other regions of Asia Minor. This arrangement was enforced through a combination of diplomatic pressure, military garrisons, and the support of local tyrants and oligarchs. The Greek cities remained nominally independent but were effectively under Persian suzerainty, a situation that would persist until the Macedonian conquests of Alexander the Great.
events.certainty_note: The continuation of Persian hegemony over Greek Asia Minor is well-attested in contemporary sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Battle of Leuctra is debated; some scholars place it in 370 BCE rather than 371 BCE. The traditional narrative of Spartan decline as sudden and catastrophic has been revised by modern scholarship, which emphasizes that Sparta remained a significant military power for decades after Leuctra and that the decline was more gradual. The romantic portrayal of the Sacred Band as a unit of lovers may be a later literary invention; contemporary sources are less explicit about this aspect. Some scholars question whether the Sacred Band was as large or as significant as later sources suggest.
historiographic_note: The Battle of Leuctra is one of the best-documented events of the Classical Greek period, attested in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. However, the sources are primarily Greek and reflect Greek perspectives, particularly Athenian and Theban viewpoints. Persian sources are largely lost. The battle has been extensively analyzed by modern historians and archaeologists, but some details remain contested. The significance of the battle in terms of broader historical change (the 'decline of the polis,' the rise of Macedonia, etc.) is a matter of ongoing scholarly debate. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic and social dimensions are underrepresented.

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, in the period immediately before the Battle of Leuctra (371 BCE). Sparta remained the dominant Greek power, though facing challenges from Thebes and other city-states. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II was consolidating control. In China, the Warring States period was intensifying. Egypt under Nectanebo I was consolidating independence from Persian rule.

events.title: Theban-Spartan tensions escalate in Boeotia
events.description: In the years leading up to the Battle of Leuctra, tensions between Thebes and Sparta were escalating. Sparta had long maintained hegemony over Greece through military dominance and a network of alliances. Thebes, under the leadership of Epaminondas and Pelopidas, was challenging Spartan authority and seeking to establish Theban leadership in Boeotia and beyond. The specific events of 372 BCE are not well-documented, but this year falls within the period of rising tensions that would culminate in the Battle of Leuctra in 371 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of escalating tensions is well-documented, but specific events within 372 BCE are not clearly attested. This entry represents the broader context rather than a specific dated event.

events.title: Nectanebo I consolidates Egyptian independence
events.description: Nectanebo I continued to consolidate Egyptian independence from Persian rule and to strengthen the 30th Dynasty. He undertook military campaigns to secure Egypt's borders and initiated building projects to restore Egyptian cultural identity. This period represents the height of the 30th Dynasty's power and prosperity.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from temples and fortifications

events.certainty_note: Nectanebo I's reign and activities are well-documented in Egyptian sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence.

events.title: Artaxerxes II maintains Persian imperial stability
events.description: Artaxerxes II continued to rule the Persian Empire with relative stability. The empire was vast and complex, with numerous satrapies and subject peoples. While chronic problems of satrapal autonomy and succession disputes remained, no major revolts or crises are recorded for this year. The King's Peace arrangement with the Greek city-states was being maintained.
events.certainty_note: The general stability of Artaxerxes II's reign is well-documented, but specific events within 372 BCE are not clearly attested.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, it should be noted that 372 BCE is a relatively poorly documented year in ancient sources. Most surviving accounts focus on major battles and political crises; the routine administration and daily life of this year are largely absent from the historical record.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 372 BCE is sparse compared to years with major documented events. Most information comes from later compilations that group events by broader periods rather than by specific years. The sources are heavily weighted toward Greek political and military history; Persian, Egyptian, and other regional developments are underrepresented. The absence of contemporary written records from most of the world means that significant developments in Asia, Africa, and the Americas are entirely absent from this account.

graph_edges.note: Escalating tensions lead to the Battle of Leuctra

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, in the period before the Battle of Leuctra (371 BCE). Sparta remained the dominant Greek power. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II was stable. In China, the Warring States period was intensifying. Egypt under Nectanebo I was consolidating independence.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of Asia beyond Greek contact zones

events.title: Earthquake destroys Helike in Achaea
events.description: According to ancient sources, a catastrophic earthquake destroyed the city of Helike in Achaea in 373 BCE. The earthquake was followed by a tsunami that inundated the coastal city, killing many inhabitants. The city was never rebuilt and was lost to history until its rediscovery by archaeologists in the 20th century. The disaster was so significant that it was remembered and recorded by ancient historians, though the exact date is debated by modern scholars.
events.certainty_note: The destruction of Helike by earthquake and tsunami is attested in multiple ancient sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Ancient sources place it in the early 4th century BCE, and 373 BCE is the traditional date, but some modern scholars propose alternative dates (e.g., 375 BCE or 365 BCE). Archaeological evidence confirms the destruction but does not precisely date it.

events.title: Nectanebo I continues building program in Egypt
events.description: Nectanebo I continued his extensive building program, constructing temples and fortifications throughout Egypt. These projects served both religious and political purposes, reinforcing Egyptian cultural identity and demonstrating the pharaoh's power and piety. The building program was part of a broader cultural renaissance during the 30th Dynasty.
events.certainty_note: Nectanebo I's building activities are well-documented in Egyptian sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence of surviving temples and structures.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the Helike earthquake is disputed. While 373 BCE is the traditional date found in many ancient sources, modern scholars have proposed alternative dates ranging from 375 BCE to 365 BCE. Some scholars argue that the ancient sources may have confused or conflated multiple earthquakes. The exact relationship between the earthquake and tsunami is also debated—some sources emphasize the earthquake, others the tsunami, and the sequence of events is not entirely clear.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 373 BCE is very sparse. Most surviving accounts focus on major political and military events; natural disasters are recorded only when they had significant political consequences or were remembered in cultural memory. The Helike earthquake is one of the few events from this year that is documented in surviving sources. The sources are heavily weighted toward Greek history; developments in other regions are largely absent. The record is biased toward dramatic events (earthquakes, battles) rather than routine administration, economic activity, or cultural developments.

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, in the period before the Battle of Leuctra (371 BCE). Sparta remained the dominant Greek power, though facing challenges from Thebes and other city-states. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II was stable. In China, the Warring States period was intensifying. Egypt under Nectanebo I was consolidating independence from Persian rule.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of Asia beyond Greek contact zones

events.title: Spartan hegemony over Greece remains unchallenged
events.description: In 374 BCE, Sparta remained the dominant military and political power in Greece. The Spartan system, based on a militarized society and a network of alliances and subject states, had proven effective for maintaining hegemony. However, tensions were building beneath the surface, particularly in Thebes, which was beginning to challenge Spartan authority. The specific events of 374 BCE are not well-documented, but this year falls within the period of Spartan dominance that would end with the Battle of Leuctra in 371 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The general statement that Sparta remained dominant in 374 BCE is reasonable based on the broader historical context, but specific events within this year are not clearly attested in surviving sources.
cross_references: -371-001 (Battle of Leuctra, end of Spartan hegemony)

events.title: Nectanebo I's reign continues in Egypt
events.description: Nectanebo I continued to rule Egypt as the founder of the 30th Dynasty. His reign (380–362 BCE) was characterized by the consolidation of Egyptian independence from Persian rule and a cultural renaissance. The specific events of 374 BCE are not well-documented, but this year falls within the period of Nectanebo's rule and the flourishing of the 30th Dynasty.
events.certainty_note: Nectanebo I's reign dates are well-established through Egyptian sources. The specific events of 374 BCE are not documented, but his rule during this period is confirmed.

events.title: Artaxerxes II rules Persian Empire
events.description: Artaxerxes II (r. 404–358 BCE) continued to rule the Persian Empire. His reign was characterized by relative stability, though the empire faced chronic problems of satrapal autonomy and succession disputes. The King's Peace arrangement with the Greek city-states was being maintained. The specific events of 374 BCE are not well-documented.
events.certainty_note: Artaxerxes II's reign during this period is confirmed by multiple sources, though specific events within 374 BCE are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, it should be noted that 374 BCE is a very poorly documented year in ancient sources. The surviving historical record focuses on major events and crises; routine administration and daily life are largely absent.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 374 BCE is extremely sparse. Most surviving accounts focus on major battles, political crises, and dramatic events; routine years without major incidents are often not recorded in detail. The sources are heavily weighted toward Greek political and military history; developments in other regions are largely absent or known only through later compilations. The absence of contemporary written records from most of the world means that significant developments in Asia, Africa, and the Americas are entirely absent from this account. This year is essentially a 'blank' in the historical record, known only through the broader context of the reigns of major rulers.

graph_edges.note: Spartan dominance in 374 BCE is challenged and ended by the Battle of Leuctra

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, following the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) and the King's Peace. Athens and Sparta remain dominant powers but weakened; Thebes is rising under Epaminondas and the Sacred Band. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II remains a major regional force. This is the era of Plato's mature philosophical work and the decline of the Classical polis system.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (contemporary with Warring States China, but minimal Greek sources)

events.title: Theban hegemony consolidates; Epaminondas emerges as military leader
events.description: Following Theban victory at Leuctra (371 BCE) and the subsequent collapse of Spartan dominance, Thebes under Epaminondas and the Sacred Band consolidates power in Boeotia and begins to challenge Athenian and Spartan influence across Greece. By 375 BCE, Theban military reorganization and the oblique phalanx formation are becoming recognized as innovations. This marks the beginning of Theban hegemony in Greece, which will last until Epaminondas' death at Mantinea (362 BCE).
events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Theban rise is well-attested; specific events of 375 BCE are less precisely dated. Leuctra is securely dated to 371 BCE; the consolidation process spans several years around this period.

events.title: Artaxerxes II of Persia maintains control over Egypt and the Levant
events.description: Artaxerxes II (r. 405–358 BCE) consolidates Persian control over Egypt following the suppression of Egyptian revolts. The Persian Empire remains the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean and Near East. Phoenician cities remain under Persian suzerainty, and the Levantine coast is firmly integrated into the imperial system. This period represents relative stability in the Persian Empire before the rise of Macedon.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Artaxerxes II is well-documented in later Greek sources; specific events of 375 BCE are not precisely dated but fit within the broader pattern of Persian stability in this period.

events.title: Plato's Academy continues as center of philosophical inquiry in Athens
events.description: Plato's Academy, founded c. 387 BCE, is now well-established as the premier philosophical institution in the Greek world. By 375 BCE, Plato is in his early 50s and has completed or is completing works including the Republic and is working on the Phaedrus and Symposium. The Academy attracts students from across the Greek world and represents a new model of sustained intellectual community. This institution will profoundly influence Western philosophy for centuries.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The existence and operation of Plato's Academy in this period is well-attested. The specific works being composed are inferred from internal evidence and later biographical tradition.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of Theban military innovations and the exact chronology of Epaminondas' rise is debated among modern scholars. Some sources place key developments slightly earlier or later than 375 BCE. The significance attributed to Theban hegemony in later Greek historiography may reflect Athenian and Spartan bias in surviving sources. Xenophon's account of this period is known to be sympathetic to Sparta and may understate Theban achievements.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 375 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon and later compilations by Diodorus and Plutarch. These sources are biased toward Greek political and military history and provide limited information about the Persian Empire, Egypt, or regions beyond Greek contact. The Academy's intellectual work is known primarily through Plato's own writings and much later biographical tradition. Archaeological evidence for this specific year is minimal; most material evidence is dated to broader periods rather than individual years.

graph_edges.note: Theban consolidation of power led to the decisive victory at Leuctra
Plato's Academy continues and will receive Aristotle as a student

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, in the period between the Corinthian War and the rise of Theban hegemony. Athens is recovering from the Peloponnesian War and the oligarchic period; Sparta remains militarily dominant but politically weakened. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is stable. This is a transitional period in Greek history before the major power shifts of the 370s BCE.

events.title: Athens rebuilds naval power and democratic institutions
events.description: In the years following the restoration of democracy (403 BCE), Athens continues to rebuild its naval fleet and reassert influence in the Aegean. By 376 BCE, Athenian naval power is recovering, and the city is beginning to form the Second Athenian League (founded formally in 378 BCE), a confederation of Greek city-states allied against Spartan hegemony. This represents Athens' gradual recovery from the devastation of the Peloponnesian War and the oligarchic period.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Athenian recovery is well-attested; specific events of 376 BCE are not precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Spartan military dominance begins to face challenges
events.description: Sparta's position as the dominant Greek military power is beginning to erode. The Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) had already weakened Spartan resources and prestige. By 376 BCE, Sparta faces growing challenges from Athens' naval recovery and Thebes' rising power. The Spartan system, based on the subjugation of the helot population and the maintenance of a warrior elite, is becoming increasingly difficult to sustain. This marks the beginning of the end of Spartan hegemony in Greece.
events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Spartan decline is well-attested; the specific dating of individual events is less precise.

events.title: Dionysius I of Syracuse consolidates power in Sicily
events.description: Dionysius I (r. 405–367 BCE) continues to consolidate his tyrannical rule over Syracuse and much of Sicily. By 376 BCE, he has established a powerful military state and is engaged in ongoing conflicts with Carthage for control of the island. Dionysius is known for his military innovations, including the use of catapults and other siege machinery. His rule represents a new model of Hellenistic tyranny that will influence later political developments.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Dionysius I is well-documented in later sources; specific events of 376 BCE are not precisely dated.
