disconfirming_evidence: The precise chronology of Athenian naval recovery and the founding of the Second Athenian League is debated. Some sources place key events in 378 BCE rather than 376 BCE. The significance of Spartan decline in this specific year is a matter of historiographic interpretation; some scholars argue that Spartan power remained formidable until Leuctra (371 BCE). Xenophon's account is known to be biased toward Sparta.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 376 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon and later compilations. These sources focus on political and military history and provide limited information about economic, social, or cultural developments. The record is heavily biased toward the Greek mainland and Sicily; information about the Persian Empire, Egypt, and other regions is minimal. Archaeological evidence for this specific year is not available; dating is based on literary sources and chronological reconstruction.

graph_edges.note: Athenian naval recovery is part of the broader Second Athenian League project
Spartan decline culminates in defeat at Leuctra

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, in the period of transition between Spartan and Theban hegemony. The Second Athenian League is being consolidated; Thebes is rising as a military power. The Persian Empire remains stable under Artaxerxes II. This is a period of significant political realignment in the Greek world.

events.title: Second Athenian League consolidates membership and structure
events.description: The Second Athenian League, formally established in 378 BCE, continues to expand and consolidate its membership in 377 BCE. The League is designed as a counterweight to Spartan hegemony and includes numerous Aegean island states and coastal cities. Unlike the earlier Delian League, the Second Athenian League is theoretically based on equality among members, though Athens clearly dominates. This represents Athens' reassertion as a major power in the Greek world.
events.certainty_note: The existence and consolidation of the Second Athenian League is well-attested; specific events of 377 BCE are not precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Thebes and Athens form alliance against Sparta
events.description: In response to Spartan aggression and the threat of Spartan hegemony, Thebes and Athens form a military alliance. This alliance is significant because it unites two of the major Greek powers against Sparta. The alliance will prove crucial in the coming conflicts, particularly at the Battle of Leuctra (371 BCE). This represents a major shift in Greek political alignments.
events.certainty_note: The alliance between Thebes and Athens is attested in sources; the precise dating to 377 BCE is approximate and may be off by a year or two.

events.title: Artaxerxes II consolidates Persian control over Egypt
events.description: Artaxerxes II continues to maintain firm control over Egypt, which had been reconquered by the Persians after a period of independence. The Persian administrative system is well-established in Egypt, and the country is integrated into the imperial tax and military system. This represents the height of Persian power in Egypt before the rise of Macedon.
events.certainty_note: The general situation of Persian control over Egypt is well-attested; specific events of 377 BCE are not precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of the Theban-Athenian alliance is uncertain; some sources place it earlier or later than 377 BCE. The significance of this alliance in later historiography may be exaggerated by sources written after Leuctra, which emphasize the importance of the alliance in retrospect. The Second Athenian League's structure and membership are debated among modern scholars, with some questioning the degree of Athenian dominance.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 377 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon and later compilations. These sources focus on political and military history and provide limited information about economic, social, or cultural developments. Epigraphic evidence (inscriptions) provides some contemporary documentation of League decrees. The record is heavily biased toward the Greek mainland; information about the Persian Empire, Egypt, and other regions is minimal.

graph_edges.note: Consolidation of the Second Athenian League
Theban-Athenian alliance leads to victory at Leuctra

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, a pivotal year marking the formal establishment of the Second Athenian League and the beginning of a major realignment of Greek political power. Sparta's hegemony is being challenged; Thebes is rising; Athens is recovering from the Peloponnesian War. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II remains the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean.

events.title: Founding of the Second Athenian League
events.description: Athens formally establishes the Second Athenian League (also called the Second Athenian Confederacy), a military and political alliance of Greek city-states designed to counterbalance Spartan hegemony. The League includes numerous Aegean island states and coastal cities. Unlike the earlier Delian League (which became an Athenian empire), the Second Athenian League is theoretically based on the principle of equality among members, with Athens as the leading power. This represents a major reassertion of Athenian power and influence in the Greek world following the Peloponnesian War and the oligarchic period.

sources.name: Inscriptions recording League charter and decrees (IG II²)

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Second Athenian League in 378 BCE is well-attested in both literary sources and contemporary inscriptions. This is one of the most securely dated events of this period.

events.title: Spartan aggression against Thebes and Athens provokes anti-Spartan coalition
events.description: Sparta, under King Agesilaus II, continues aggressive military campaigns to maintain its hegemony over Greece. Spartan actions against Thebes and Athens provoke a coalition of Greek states to form against Spartan dominance. The founding of the Second Athenian League is a direct response to Spartan aggression. This marks the beginning of the end of Spartan hegemony in Greece, as other powers unite against Spartan military dominance.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Spartan aggression and the anti-Spartan response is well-attested; specific military actions of 378 BCE are not precisely dated.

events.title: Plato's Academy continues as center of philosophical inquiry
events.description: Plato's Academy, founded c. 387 BCE, continues to operate as the premier philosophical institution in the Greek world. By 378 BCE, Plato is in his late 40s and is actively engaged in philosophical inquiry and teaching. The Academy attracts students from across the Greek world and represents a new model of sustained intellectual community. This institution will profoundly influence Western philosophy for centuries.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The existence and operation of Plato's Academy in this period is well-attested.

events.title: Dionysius I of Syracuse continues military campaigns in Sicily
events.description: Dionysius I (r. 405–367 BCE) continues his military campaigns to consolidate control over Sicily and to contest Carthaginian power on the island. By 378 BCE, Dionysius has established a powerful military state with advanced siege machinery and military innovations. His rule represents a new model of Hellenistic tyranny that will influence later political developments.
events.certainty_note: The reign of Dionysius I is well-documented in later sources; specific military campaigns of 378 BCE are not precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise date of the founding of the Second Athenian League is sometimes given as 377 BCE rather than 378 BCE in different sources. Modern scholars debate the exact structure and membership of the League, with some questioning the degree of Athenian dominance and the extent to which the League was truly based on equality. The significance attributed to the League in later historiography may reflect Athenian bias in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 378 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon and later compilations by Diodorus and Plutarch. Epigraphic evidence (inscriptions) provides contemporary documentation of the League's charter and early decrees. These sources focus on political and military history and provide limited information about economic, social, or cultural developments. The record is heavily biased toward the Greek mainland; information about the Persian Empire, Egypt, and other regions is minimal. Archaeological evidence for this specific year is not available.

graph_edges.note: Founding of Second Athenian League leads to consolidation of membership
Spartan aggression provokes the founding of the Second Athenian League
Second Athenian League alliance with Thebes leads to victory at Leuctra

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, in the period following the King's Peace (387 BCE) and preceding the major power shifts of the 370s BCE. Sparta remains the dominant Greek military power, but its hegemony is beginning to be challenged. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is stable and powerful. This is a transitional period before the rise of Thebes and the founding of the Second Athenian League.

events.title: Sparta maintains hegemony over Greece; Agesilaus II continues military campaigns
events.description: Sparta, under King Agesilaus II, remains the dominant military power in Greece. Agesilaus continues military campaigns to maintain Spartan hegemony and to suppress challenges to Spartan authority. The Spartan system, based on the subjugation of the helot population and the maintenance of a warrior elite, remains intact. However, the costs of maintaining this system are becoming increasingly apparent, and Spartan power is beginning to face challenges from rising powers like Thebes and the recovering Athens.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Spartan dominance in this period is well-attested; specific military campaigns of 379 BCE are not precisely dated.

events.title: Athens continues recovery from Peloponnesian War and oligarchic period
events.description: Athens continues to recover from the devastation of the Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE) and the subsequent oligarchic period (404–403 BCE). Democratic institutions are being strengthened, and the city is beginning to rebuild its naval power and economic resources. By 379 BCE, Athens is preparing to reassert itself as a major power in the Greek world, a process that will culminate in the founding of the Second Athenian League in 378 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Athenian recovery is well-attested; specific events of 379 BCE are not precisely dated.

events.title: Thebes begins to emerge as a military power in Boeotia
events.description: Thebes, under the leadership of Epaminondas and the Sacred Band, begins to emerge as a significant military power in Boeotia. The city is consolidating control over the Boeotian League and is beginning to challenge Spartan hegemony. By 379 BCE, Thebes is positioning itself as a major player in Greek politics, a process that will accelerate in the following years and culminate in the decisive victory at Leuctra (371 BCE).
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Theban rise is well-attested; specific events of 379 BCE are not precisely dated.

events.title: Artaxerxes II maintains Persian control over the eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Artaxerxes II (r. 405–358 BCE) continues to maintain firm control over the Persian Empire, including Egypt, the Levant, and Mesopotamia. The Persian Empire remains the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean and Near East. This period represents relative stability in the Persian Empire before the rise of Macedon under Philip II.
events.certainty_note: The general situation of Persian control is well-attested; specific events of 379 BCE are not precisely dated.

events.title: Plato's Academy continues as center of philosophical inquiry
events.description: Plato's Academy, founded c. 387 BCE, continues to operate as the premier philosophical institution in the Greek world. By 379 BCE, Plato is in his late 40s and is actively engaged in philosophical inquiry and teaching. The Academy attracts students from across the Greek world and represents a new model of sustained intellectual community.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The existence and operation of Plato's Academy in this period is well-attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise chronology of Theban rise and the exact dating of Epaminondas' emergence as a leader is debated among modern scholars. Some sources place key developments earlier or later than 379 BCE. The significance attributed to Theban power in this year may be exaggerated by sources written after Leuctra, which emphasize the importance of Theban military innovations in retrospect. Xenophon's account is known to be biased toward Sparta and may understate the significance of Theban and Athenian developments.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 379 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon and later compilations by Diodorus and Plutarch. These sources focus on political and military history and provide limited information about economic, social, or cultural developments. The record is heavily biased toward the Greek mainland; information about the Persian Empire, Egypt, and other regions is minimal. Archaeological evidence for this specific year is not available; dating is based on literary sources and chronological reconstruction.

graph_edges.note: Spartan hegemony provokes anti-Spartan coalition
Athenian recovery leads to founding of Second Athenian League
Theban emergence as military power leads to consolidation of hegemony

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, following the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) and the King's Peace. Athens and Sparta remain major powers but weakened; Thebes is rising under Epaminondas. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II remains a dominant regional force. This is the era of Plato's mature philosophical work and the decline of the Classical polis system.

events.title: Plato founds the Academy in Athens
events.description: Plato establishes his philosophical school, the Academy, in a gymnasium northwest of Athens. This institution becomes one of the most influential centers of learning in the ancient world, operating for nearly 900 years. The Academy emphasizes mathematics, dialectic, and the pursuit of philosophical truth, establishing a model for higher education that influences Western thought profoundly. Plato's school attracts students from across the Greek world and becomes a rival to Isocrates' rhetorical school.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers, Book III

events.certainty_note: The founding date of 387 BCE is more commonly cited in modern scholarship; 380 BCE represents the period of the Academy's consolidation and growth. Exact founding date is uncertain by ±5–10 years.

events.title: Theban hegemony consolidates under Epaminondas
events.description: Epaminondas emerges as the dominant military and political figure in Thebes, beginning a period of Theban ascendancy in Greek affairs. Following the Corinthian War settlement, Thebes begins to challenge Spartan hegemony and reorganize the Boeotian League under its leadership. This period marks the beginning of Thebes' rise to prominence, culminating in the Battle of Leuctra (371 BCE). Epaminondas' reforms and military innovations reshape Greek warfare.
events.certainty_note: Epaminondas' rise to prominence is well-attested, though the precise chronology of his early political consolidation varies by source. The period 380–375 BCE represents his growing influence.

events.title: Artaxerxes II consolidates Persian control in Egypt
events.description: Following the Egyptian revolt under Amyrtaeus (404–399 BCE), Artaxerxes II reasserts Persian control over Egypt, which had been lost for several decades. The reconquest is gradual and involves military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations. Egypt remains a contested and unstable province within the Persian Empire, with periodic revolts. This reassertion of control is part of Artaxerxes II's broader consolidation of the Persian Empire after the Anabasis crisis.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XV

events.certainty_note: The reconquest of Egypt occurred over several years (c. 380–343 BCE). The precise dating of individual campaigns is uncertain; 380 BCE represents the period of renewed Persian military pressure.

disconfirming_evidence: The founding date of Plato's Academy is traditionally given as 387 BCE in many sources, not 380 BCE. Modern scholarship places the founding closer to 387 BCE, with 380 BCE representing a later phase of consolidation. The exact date remains debated among scholars, with some sources suggesting the Academy developed gradually rather than being formally 'founded' in a single year. The significance of Epaminondas' role in 380 BCE specifically is somewhat overstated; his major military innovations and victories came later, particularly after 371 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 380 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon and later compilations by Diodorus and Plutarch. There is a significant bias toward political and military history of the Greek city-states; economic and social history are underrepresented. Egyptian sources exist but are sparse for this period. Persian sources are largely lost, and our knowledge of Persian affairs depends on Greek accounts, which are often hostile or incomplete. The Academy's founding is well-attested in later sources but lacks contemporary documentation of the exact date.

graph_edges.note: Academy's founding is part of broader intellectual ferment in late Classical Athens
Epaminondas' consolidation of power leads to Theban military dominance and the Battle of Leuctra

era_context: Early 4th century BCE Greece, in the aftermath of the Corinthian War and the King's Peace (387 BCE). The Greek city-state system is fragmenting; Sparta's hegemony is weakening, and new powers like Thebes and Syracuse are rising. Philosophically, this is the era of Plato's mature work and the pre-Aristotelian Academy. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II remains stable but faces periodic challenges.

events.title: Dionysius I of Syracuse consolidates Sicilian power
events.description: Dionysius I, tyrant of Syracuse, continues his consolidation of power in Sicily and southern Italy. By 381 BCE, he has established Syracuse as the dominant Greek power in the western Mediterranean, rivaling Athens and Sparta in influence. His reign (405–367 BCE) is marked by military expansion, naval power, and cultural patronage. Dionysius I represents a new model of Hellenistic monarchy that will influence later dynastic systems. He maintains a large mercenary army and develops advanced siege warfare technology.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Books XIII–XIV

events.certainty_note: Dionysius I's reign and consolidation of power are well-attested. The specific events of 381 BCE are less clearly documented than his earlier military campaigns or later conflicts.

events.title: Spartan hegemony faces challenges in Greece
events.description: Sparta's position as hegemon of Greece continues to erode following the Corinthian War. The King's Peace (387 BCE) has failed to establish lasting stability; various Greek city-states resist Spartan dominance. Thebes, Athens, and Corinth form shifting alliances against Spartan power. This period represents the beginning of the end of Spartan hegemony, which will be decisively broken at the Battle of Leuctra in 371 BCE. Spartan military resources are stretched thin by maintaining control over the Peloponnese and resisting challenges from other powers.
events.certainty_note: The general trend of Spartan decline is well-documented; specific events of 381 BCE are less clearly attested.

events.title: Athenian naval recovery and Second Athenian League formation
events.description: Athens begins to recover from its defeat in the Peloponnesian War (404 BCE) and the subsequent oligarchic period. The city rebuilds its naval power and begins to form alliances with other Greek city-states against Spartan hegemony. The Second Athenian League (or Confederacy) is in its early stages of formation, with Athens seeking to establish a maritime alliance without the imperial dominance that characterized the earlier Delian League. This represents Athens' gradual return to prominence in Greek affairs.
events.certainty_note: The Second Athenian League's formation is traditionally dated to 378/377 BCE, with 381 BCE representing the earlier phase of alliance-building and naval recovery.

disconfirming_evidence: The Second Athenian League is more commonly dated to 378/377 BCE rather than 381 BCE. The events of 381 BCE in Athens are less clearly documented than the formal establishment of the League. Spartan hegemony, while declining, was not yet decisively broken in 381 BCE; the major turning point came with the Battle of Leuctra in 371 BCE. The characterization of this period as one of clear Spartan decline may overstate the case for 381 BCE specifically.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 381 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica, which provides a contemporary account of Greek political and military affairs. However, Xenophon's narrative is selective and focuses on major political figures and military campaigns. Economic and social history are underrepresented. Sources for Sicily and the western Mediterranean are primarily later compilations by Diodorus. Persian sources are largely lost. The record is heavily biased toward the Greek mainland and Aegean world; events in other regions are poorly documented or absent.

graph_edges.note: Spartan hegemony's erosion culminates in the Battle of Leuctra
Athenian naval recovery leads to formal establishment of Second Athenian League

era_context: Early 4th century BCE, a period of transition in the Greek world. The Corinthian War has ended (387 BCE), and the King's Peace has established a nominal framework for Greek interstate relations, but this framework is breaking down. Sparta remains the dominant military power but faces increasing challenges. Thebes is beginning its rise to prominence. Philosophically, Plato is active in Athens. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is stable but faces periodic revolts.

events.title: Spartan occupation of Thebes' Cadmea fortress
events.description: Spartan forces, under the command of Phoebidas, occupy the Cadmea, the acropolis of Thebes, under the pretext of supporting the oligarchic faction in the city. This act of aggression violates the King's Peace and marks a turning point in Greek history. The occupation galvanizes Theban resistance and ultimately leads to the liberation of Thebes in 379 BCE and the rise of Epaminondas. The Cadmea occupation is a critical catalyst for Theban independence and the subsequent Theban hegemony. It demonstrates Spartan willingness to use military force to maintain control, but also provokes the very resistance that will undermine Spartan power.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XV

events.certainty_note: This event is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. Xenophon provides a detailed account. The date of 382 BCE is widely accepted in modern scholarship.

events.title: Theban resistance and underground movement begins
events.description: Following the Spartan occupation of the Cadmea, Theban democrats and patriots begin organizing resistance to Spartan rule. Pelopidas and other leaders of the democratic faction go into exile or underground. This period marks the beginning of the movement that will lead to the liberation of Thebes in 379 BCE. The resistance movement is initially small and clandestine but grows in strength and organization. This underground movement becomes the foundation for Theban independence and the subsequent rise of Thebes as a major power.
events.certainty_note: The existence of resistance is attested, though the specific details and chronology of the underground movement are less clearly documented than the occupation itself.

events.title: Athenian response to Spartan aggression
events.description: Athens responds to the Spartan occupation of Thebes with diplomatic protests and begins to consider military support for Theban resistance. The occupation of the Cadmea is widely seen as a violation of the King's Peace and provokes concern among other Greek city-states. Athens, while not yet openly supporting Theban liberation, begins to shift its diplomatic stance away from Sparta. This marks the beginning of Athens' alignment with anti-Spartan forces in Greece.
events.certainty_note: Athens' diplomatic response to the Cadmea occupation is attested, though the specific details and timing of individual diplomatic moves are less clearly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The Spartan occupation of the Cadmea is one of the most clearly documented events of this period, and there is little disconfirming evidence regarding its occurrence or date. However, some sources suggest that Phoebidas' action may have been unauthorized or at least controversial within Sparta itself, and that Spartan leadership was divided on the wisdom of the occupation. The characterization of the occupation as a turning point is widely accepted, but some scholars argue that Spartan decline was already inevitable due to broader structural factors, and that the Cadmea occupation merely accelerated an existing trend.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 382 BCE is relatively rich, dominated by Xenophon's contemporary account in the Hellenica. This is one of the best-documented years of the early 4th century BCE. However, the sources are still selective and focus on political and military history. Economic and social history are underrepresented. The sources are heavily biased toward the Greek mainland and Aegean world; events in other regions are poorly documented or absent. Spartan sources are largely lost, and our knowledge of Spartan decision-making depends on Greek accounts, which are often hostile or incomplete.

graph_edges.note: Spartan occupation of Cadmea provokes Theban liberation movement
Cadmea occupation ultimately leads to Theban hegemony and Battle of Leuctra
Athenian shift away from Sparta contributes to formation of Second Athenian League

era_context: Early 4th century BCE, a period of relative stability following the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) and the King's Peace (387 BCE). Sparta remains the dominant military power in Greece, though its hegemony is increasingly contested. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is stable. Philosophically, Plato is active in Athens. This is a transitional period before the major upheavals of the late 380s and 370s BCE.

events.title: Spartan hegemony in Greece remains stable
events.description: Sparta maintains its position as the dominant military power in Greece following the King's Peace. The Peloponnesian League remains under Spartan control, and Sparta's military superiority is unquestioned. However, underlying tensions and resentments are building among other Greek city-states. The King's Peace framework, while nominally accepted, is increasingly seen as unjust by those who resent Spartan dominance. This period of apparent stability masks growing discontent that will erupt in the late 380s and 370s BCE.
events.certainty_note: The general stability of Spartan hegemony in 383 BCE is attested, though specific events of this year are not clearly documented.

events.title: Athenian recovery continues under democratic leadership
events.description: Athens continues its recovery from the Peloponnesian War and the subsequent oligarchic period. Democratic institutions are fully restored, and the city is rebuilding its naval power and economic strength. Athenian cultural life flourishes, with Plato's Academy and other intellectual institutions attracting students from across the Greek world. Athens is not yet a major military power, but it is regaining influence through cultural prestige and diplomatic activity. The city is beginning to form alliances with other Greek city-states against Spartan hegemony.
events.certainty_note: The general trend of Athenian recovery is well-attested; specific events of 383 BCE are less clearly documented.

events.title: Dionysius I of Syracuse continues expansion in Sicily
events.description: Dionysius I, tyrant of Syracuse, continues his military campaigns to consolidate control over Sicily and southern Italy. His reign (405–367 BCE) is marked by continuous military expansion and the development of advanced siege warfare technology. By 383 BCE, Syracuse is the dominant Greek power in the western Mediterranean, rivaling the major powers of the Greek mainland. Dionysius I's military innovations and mercenary army make Syracuse a formidable power that influences the balance of power in the Mediterranean.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Books XIII–XIV

events.certainty_note: Dionysius I's reign and military campaigns are well-attested. The specific events of 383 BCE are less clearly documented than his earlier or later campaigns.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The characterization of 383 BCE as a period of stability is accurate, though it masks underlying tensions that will erupt later. The specific events of this year are not clearly documented in surviving sources, which focus more on major political and military turning points.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 383 BCE is moderate in quality. Xenophon's Hellenica provides a contemporary account of Greek affairs, but the narrative is selective and focuses on major political and military events. Specific events of 383 BCE are not clearly documented. Economic and social history are underrepresented. The sources are heavily biased toward the Greek mainland and Aegean world; events in other regions are poorly documented or absent. This year falls in a period between major documented events (the Corinthian War ending in 387 BCE and the Cadmea occupation in 382 BCE), so it receives less attention in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Spartan hegemony's underlying tensions lead to aggressive actions like the Cadmea occupation

era_context: Early 4th century BCE, in the aftermath of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) and the King's Peace (387 BCE). Sparta is the dominant military power in Greece, but the King's Peace framework is proving unstable. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is stable and powerful. Philosophically, Plato is active in Athens. This is a period of relative peace in Greece, but underlying tensions are building that will erupt in the late 380s and 370s BCE.

events.title: King's Peace framework continues to govern Greek interstate relations
events.description: The King's Peace (also called the Peace of Antalcidas), established in 387 BCE, continues to provide the nominal framework for Greek interstate relations. The peace treaty, brokered by the Persian Great King Artaxerxes II, guarantees the autonomy of Greek city-states and gives Persia control over the Greek cities of Asia Minor. Sparta is the primary enforcer of the peace, using its military power to suppress violations. However, the peace is increasingly resented by other Greek city-states, particularly Athens and Thebes, which see it as unjust and designed to maintain Spartan hegemony. The framework is beginning to break down as various city-states seek to challenge Spartan dominance.
events.certainty_note: The King's Peace is well-attested in contemporary sources. Its continued operation in 384 BCE is confirmed by Xenophon's account.

events.title: Plato's philosophical work continues in Athens
events.description: Plato continues his philosophical work in Athens, developing his theory of forms and engaging in dialogue with students and other philosophers. While the Academy has not yet been formally established (that occurs around 387 BCE), Plato is already gathering students and conducting philosophical discussions. His works from this period include dialogues that explore ethics, politics, and metaphysics. Plato's philosophical work is beginning to have an influence on Athenian intellectual life and will eventually shape Western philosophy profoundly.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers, Book III

events.certainty_note: Plato's philosophical activity in 384 BCE is attested, though specific works or events of this year are not clearly documented.

events.title: Dionysius I consolidates power in Syracuse
events.description: Dionysius I, tyrant of Syracuse, continues to consolidate his power and expand Syracuse's influence in Sicily and southern Italy. His reign (405–367 BCE) is marked by military expansion, naval power, and cultural patronage. By 384 BCE, Syracuse is the dominant Greek power in the western Mediterranean. Dionysius I's rule represents a new model of Hellenistic monarchy that emphasizes military power, mercenary armies, and advanced siege warfare technology. His court attracts artists, poets, and intellectuals from across the Greek world.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Books XIII–XIV

events.certainty_note: Dionysius I's reign and consolidation of power are well-attested. The specific events of 384 BCE are less clearly documented than his earlier military campaigns or later conflicts.

events.title: Artaxerxes II maintains stability in the Persian Empire
events.description: Artaxerxes II continues to rule the Persian Empire, maintaining stability and consolidating control over the vast territories under Persian rule. His reign (404–358 BCE) is marked by the suppression of revolts, the maintenance of the King's Peace in Greece, and the consolidation of Persian power. The Persian Empire remains the dominant power in the Near East and Eastern Mediterranean. Artaxerxes II's rule is characterized by relative stability, though periodic revolts and challenges to his authority occur throughout his reign.
events.certainty_note: Artaxerxes II's reign and the general stability of the Persian Empire are well-attested. The specific events of 384 BCE are less clearly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The King's Peace is well-attested and its continued operation in 384 BCE is confirmed. Plato's philosophical activity is attested, though the specific works or events of 384 BCE are not clearly documented. Dionysius I's consolidation of power is well-attested. Artaxerxes II's reign is well-attested, though specific events of 384 BCE are not clearly documented.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 384 BCE is moderate in quality. Xenophon's Hellenica provides a contemporary account of Greek affairs, but the narrative is selective and focuses on major political and military events. Specific events of 384 BCE are not clearly documented in surviving sources. Economic and social history are underrepresented. The sources are heavily biased toward the Greek mainland and Aegean world; events in other regions are poorly documented or absent. This year falls in a period of relative stability between major documented events, so it receives less attention in surviving sources. Persian sources are largely lost, and our knowledge of Persian affairs depends on Greek accounts, which are often incomplete or hostile.

graph_edges.note: King's Peace framework's instability leads to Spartan aggression like the Cadmea occupation
Plato's philosophical work leads to formal establishment of the Academy

era_context: Classical Greece in the early 4th century BCE, following the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) and the King's Peace (387 BCE). The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II remains the dominant Near Eastern power. In Italy, Rome is consolidating power in Latium; in China, the Warring States period continues with major kingdoms competing for hegemony.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and early Silk Road trade (sparse contemporary documentation)

events.title: Dionysius I of Syracuse consolidates power in Sicily
events.description: Dionysius I, tyrant of Syracuse, continues his consolidation of power in Sicily following his rise in 405 BCE. By 385 BCE, he has established himself as the dominant military and political figure in the Greek west, building a powerful navy and mercenary army. His reign marks a shift toward Hellenistic-style autocracy and away from traditional polis democracy. His military innovations and aggressive expansion will define Sicilian politics for the next three decades.
events.certainty_note: Dionysius's consolidation is well-attested in later Greek sources, but precise dating of individual actions within the 405–380 BCE period is uncertain. 385 BCE is assigned as a point within his established rule.
cross_references: Leads to -378-001 (Dion's expedition against Dionysius II)

events.title: Artaxerxes II consolidates Persian control after the Corinthian War
events.description: Following the King's Peace of 387 BCE, Artaxerxes II of Persia has successfully reasserted Persian dominance over the Eastern Mediterranean and Asia Minor. Greek city-states, exhausted by the Corinthian War, accept Persian arbitration and Persian garrisons in key cities. This marks the height of Persian influence over Greek affairs in the 4th century BCE, though it will prove unstable as Greek resistance grows.
events.certainty_note: The King's Peace and its aftermath are well-documented in Xenophon's contemporary account. The consolidation of Persian control is historiographic consensus.
cross_references: Leads to -371-001 (Battle of Leuctra, which undermines Persian influence)

events.title: Plato founds the Academy in Athens
events.description: Plato, student of Socrates, establishes the Academy in the gymnasium of Akademos in northwest Athens. This institution becomes one of the most influential philosophical schools of antiquity, operating for nearly 900 years. The Academy's focus on mathematics, dialectic, and metaphysics will shape Western philosophy profoundly. The founding date is traditionally placed around 387–385 BCE, though some sources suggest 388 BCE.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers (Book 3)

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Academy is traditionally dated to 387 or 388 BCE; 385 BCE is within the probable range but not precisely confirmed. Ancient sources give varying dates within a 3–4 year window.
cross_references: Leads to -347-001 (Plato's death and succession of Speusippus)

disconfirming_evidence: The precise founding date of Plato's Academy is disputed: some modern scholars argue for 388 BCE, others for 387 BCE, and some for 385 BCE. No contemporary inscription or primary source gives an exact date. The traditional date of 387 BCE (from Diogenes Laërtius) is often preferred by modern scholarship, making the assignment to 385 BCE somewhat arbitrary. Additionally, the significance of Dionysius I's consolidation in 385 BCE specifically (versus the broader period 405–380 BCE) is not sharply defined in sources; the year is assigned by historiographic convention rather than a documented turning point.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 385 BCE is moderate but unevenly distributed. Greek sources (Xenophon, later compilations by Plutarch and Diodorus) are relatively rich for political and military events in Greece and the Eastern Mediterranean. However, the record is heavily Athens-centric and reflects the interests of Greek elites. Persian sources are sparse in surviving form (though Ctesias and later compilations preserve some material). Events in Italy, Egypt, and the broader Mediterranean are less well-documented. The founding of the Academy is a cultural event of immense later significance but was not necessarily seen as epoch-defining at the time. Bias toward intellectual and political history over economic or social history is evident.

graph_edges.note: Dionysius I's consolidation of power in Sicily sets the stage for Dion's later opposition and invasion.
Persian dominance established by the King's Peace is undermined by the rise of Thebes after Leuctra.
The Academy's founding is the beginning of an intellectual institution that will outlast Plato himself.

era_context: Classical Greece in the early 4th century BCE, in the immediate aftermath of the King's Peace (387 BCE). The Persian Empire is at the height of its influence over Greek affairs. Sparta, though weakened by the Corinthian War, remains a major power. In China, the Warring States period continues; in Italy, Rome is expanding its influence in Latium.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and early Silk Road networks

events.title: Agesilaus II of Sparta campaigns in Asia Minor
events.description: Agesilaus II, king of Sparta, continues his military campaigns in Asia Minor against Persian forces and their Greek allies. Though the King's Peace of 387 BCE nominally ended Greek-Persian conflict, Agesilaus maintains pressure on Persian-controlled territories. His campaigns are part of a broader Spartan strategy to maintain influence in the Eastern Mediterranean, though they will ultimately prove unsustainable given Sparta's declining resources and the rise of rival Greek powers.
events.certainty_note: Agesilaus's campaigns in Asia Minor are well-attested, but the precise chronology of individual campaigns in the 390s–380s BCE is uncertain. 386 BCE is assigned within the broader period of his activity.
cross_references: Leads to -371-001 (Battle of Leuctra, which ends Spartan dominance)

events.title: Evagoras I of Cyprus maintains independence against Persian pressure
events.description: Evagoras I, king of Salamis in Cyprus, continues to maintain a precarious independence from Persian control. Though nominally a Persian vassal, Evagoras cultivates ties with Athens and other Greek powers, attempting to balance Persian pressure with Greek support. His reign represents the complex position of Cypriot rulers in the 4th century BCE, caught between Persian imperial authority and Greek cultural and political influence.
events.certainty_note: Evagoras's reign is attested in Isocrates's contemporary oration, but the specific events of 386 BCE are not sharply defined. The year is assigned within the broader period of his rule (c. 410–374 BCE).
cross_references: Leads to -374-001 (Evagoras's death and Persian reassertion)

events.title: Thebes begins to emerge as a major power in Greece
events.description: In the aftermath of the King's Peace, Thebes begins to consolidate power in Boeotia and assert itself as a major Greek power. The Theban oligarchy, supported by a growing military establishment, starts to challenge Spartan hegemony. This period marks the beginning of Thebes's rise to prominence, which will culminate in the Battle of Leuctra (371 BCE) and the brief Theban hegemony of the 360s BCE.
events.certainty_note: Thebes's emergence as a power is a gradual process; 386 BCE is assigned as a point within this broader trajectory. The specific events of 386 BCE are not sharply documented.
cross_references: Leads to -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

disconfirming_evidence: The specific events of 386 BCE are not well-documented in surviving sources. The year falls within broader periods of activity (Agesilaus's campaigns, Evagoras's reign, Thebes's emergence) but no contemporary source marks 386 BCE as a turning point or significant year. The assignment of events to this year is somewhat arbitrary, based on historiographic convention and the broader context of the period. No disconfirming evidence has been identified for the events listed, but their significance specifically to 386 BCE (rather than the broader 390s–380s BCE period) is not established.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 386 BCE is moderate but sparse in terms of specific dated events. Greek sources (Xenophon, Isocrates, later compilations) provide context for the period, but precise chronology is often uncertain. The year falls in a period of relative stability after the King's Peace, before the major upheavals of the 370s BCE. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military history in Greece and the Eastern Mediterranean; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Persian sources are sparse. Events in other regions (Italy, Egypt, the broader Mediterranean) are poorly documented.

graph_edges.note: Agesilaus's continued campaigns in Asia Minor and the Aegean contribute to Spartan overextension, which is exposed by Thebes's victory at Leuctra.
Evagoras's reign continues until his death in 374 BCE; 386 BCE is a point within this period.
Thebes's consolidation of power in the 380s BCE directly leads to its military victory at Leuctra.

era_context: Classical Greece at a major turning point: the end of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) and the establishment of the King's Peace (also called the Peace of Antalcidas). The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II emerges as the arbiter of Greek affairs. Sparta, though weakened, remains a major power. In China, the Warring States period continues; in Italy, Rome is consolidating power in Latium.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and early Silk Road networks

events.title: King's Peace (Peace of Antalcidas) ends the Corinthian War
events.description: The King's Peace, negotiated by the Persian satrap Antalcidas, ends the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) and establishes a new framework for Greek-Persian relations. The peace terms, dictated by Persia, grant Persian control over Greek cities in Asia Minor and Cyprus, guarantee the autonomy of Greek city-states in Europe, and establish Sparta as the guarantor of the peace. This represents a major shift in the balance of power: Persia becomes the arbiter of Greek affairs, and Sparta, though weakened by the war, is elevated as the enforcer of Persian-backed order. The peace is unstable and will be challenged repeatedly over the next two decades.
events.certainty_note: The King's Peace is one of the best-documented events of the 4th century BCE, attested in contemporary sources (Xenophon) and confirmed by later historiographic consensus. The date of 387 BCE is secure.
cross_references: Leads to -385-002 (Artaxerxes II consolidates Persian control)
Leads to -371-001 (Battle of Leuctra, which undermines the peace)

events.title: Plato travels to Sicily and meets Dionysius I
events.description: Plato, the Athenian philosopher, travels to Syracuse in Sicily to meet Dionysius I, the tyrant. This visit marks the beginning of Plato's involvement with Sicilian politics and his attempt to influence Dionysius toward philosophical governance. The visit is traditionally dated to 387 BCE, though some sources place it slightly later (c. 388 BCE). Plato's engagement with Dionysius will lead to multiple visits and attempts to educate the tyrant's son, Dionysius II, in philosophy—efforts that ultimately fail but become legendary in later accounts.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers (Book 3.18–20)

events.certainty_note: Plato's first visit to Syracuse is traditionally dated to 387 BCE, but the exact date is uncertain. The Seventh Letter, if authentic, provides first-hand testimony, but its authenticity is disputed by modern scholars. The visit is confirmed by later sources, but the precise date may be 388 or 386 BCE.
cross_references: Leads to -367-001 (Plato's second visit to Syracuse)

events.title: Sparta reasserts control over the Peloponnese
events.description: In the aftermath of the Corinthian War, Sparta uses the King's Peace as a framework to reassert its dominance over the Peloponnese. Spartan forces move to suppress or control rival cities and restore Spartan hegemony. This reassertion is enabled by the peace settlement, which grants Sparta the role of guarantor of Greek autonomy—a position Sparta interprets as justifying intervention in the affairs of other Greek states. However, this aggressive interpretation of the peace will generate resentment and resistance, particularly from Thebes.
events.certainty_note: Sparta's reassertion of control is attested in Xenophon's account, but the specific chronology of individual actions in 387 BCE versus the broader period 387–380 BCE is uncertain.
cross_references: Leads to -371-001 (Battle of Leuctra, which ends Spartan hegemony)

events.title: Thebes expelled from the Boeotian League by Sparta
events.description: Following the King's Peace, Sparta moves to break up the Boeotian League, which it views as a threat to Greek autonomy (as defined by the peace). Thebes is expelled from the league, and Spartan forces occupy key Boeotian cities. This action is justified under the King's Peace's guarantee of city autonomy, but it is widely seen as Spartan aggression. The expulsion of Thebes from the league marks a turning point: it alienates Thebes from the Spartan-backed order and sets the stage for Thebes's later resistance and rise to power.
events.certainty_note: The expulsion of Thebes from the Boeotian League is well-attested in Xenophon's contemporary account. The date of 387 BCE is secure.
cross_references: Leads to -371-001 (Battle of Leuctra, Thebes's revenge)

disconfirming_evidence: The King's Peace is traditionally presented as a triumph for Sparta and a stabilizing force in Greek politics. However, modern scholarship increasingly recognizes it as a fundamentally unstable arrangement that sowed the seeds of future conflict. The peace's guarantee of city autonomy, interpreted by Sparta as justifying intervention, was widely resented and led to Spartan overreach. Additionally, the precise date of Plato's first visit to Syracuse is disputed: some sources place it in 388 BCE, others in 387 BCE, and some even later. The Seventh Letter, which provides Plato's own account, is of disputed authenticity, and modern scholars are divided on whether it is a genuine work by Plato or a later forgery. The significance of Thebes's expulsion from the Boeotian League in 387 BCE specifically (versus the broader period of Spartan reassertion in the 380s BCE) is not sharply defined in sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 387 BCE is rich, particularly for the King's Peace, which is one of the best-documented events of the 4th century BCE. Xenophon's Hellenica provides a contemporary account, and later sources (Plutarch, Diodorus) confirm and elaborate on the event. However, the record is heavily weighted toward political and military history in Greece and the Eastern Mediterranean. Persian sources are sparse in surviving form. The record reflects the interests and perspectives of Greek elites, particularly Athenian and Spartan sources. Events in other regions (Italy, Egypt, the broader Mediterranean, Asia, Africa) are poorly documented or absent. The significance attributed to the King's Peace in modern historiography may reflect a bias toward viewing it as a major turning point, when it was perhaps seen as less epochal by contemporaries.

graph_edges.note: The King's Peace establishes Persian dominance over Greek affairs, which Artaxerxes II consolidates in the following years.
The King's Peace provides the framework for Sparta's reassertion of control over the Peloponnese.
The King's Peace justifies Sparta's expulsion of Thebes from the Boeotian League.
Thebes's expulsion from the Boeotian League alienates it from the Spartan-backed order and sets the stage for Thebes's later military victory at Leuctra.
Plato's first visit to Syracuse leads to his later involvement in Sicilian politics and his second visit.

era_context: Classical Greece in the final stages of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE). The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is preparing to impose a settlement on the warring Greek states. Sparta, Athens, Corinth, Thebes, and other powers are exhausted by years of conflict. In China, the Warring States period continues; in Italy, Rome is consolidating power in Latium.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and early Silk Road networks

events.title: Corinthian War continues; negotiations for peace intensify
events.description: The Corinthian War, which began in 395 BCE, continues into its eighth year. The conflict has exhausted the major Greek powers—Sparta, Athens, Corinth, Thebes, and their allies. By 388 BCE, negotiations for peace are intensifying, with Persian mediation becoming increasingly important. The war has demonstrated the inability of Greek city-states to achieve decisive victory against each other and has created a power vacuum that Persia is positioned to fill. The final settlement will come in 387 BCE with the King's Peace.
events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Corinthian War in 388 BCE is well-attested in contemporary sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Plato's first visit to Syracuse (alternative dating)
events.description: Some ancient sources date Plato's first visit to Syracuse to 388 BCE rather than 387 BCE. This visit marks the beginning of Plato's involvement with Sicilian politics and his attempt to influence Dionysius I toward philosophical governance. The exact date is uncertain, with sources varying by one or two years. Plato's engagement with Dionysius will lead to multiple visits and attempts to educate the tyrant's son, Dionysius II, in philosophy.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers (Book 3.18–20)

events.certainty_note: The date of Plato's first visit to Syracuse is uncertain, with sources placing it between 388 and 386 BCE. 388 BCE is one of the proposed dates, but 387 BCE is more commonly cited in modern scholarship.
cross_references: Leads to -367-001 (Plato's second visit to Syracuse)

events.title: Dionysius I of Syracuse continues military expansion in Sicily
events.description: Dionysius I, tyrant of Syracuse, continues his military campaigns to consolidate control over Sicily. By 388 BCE, he has established himself as the dominant military power in the Greek west, building a powerful navy and mercenary army. His campaigns against rival Greek cities and indigenous Sicilian peoples are part of a broader strategy to create a Syracusan empire. His military innovations and aggressive expansion will define Sicilian politics for the next three decades.
events.certainty_note: Dionysius's military campaigns are well-attested in later Greek sources, but precise dating of individual actions within the 405–380 BCE period is uncertain. 388 BCE is assigned as a point within his established rule.
cross_references: Leads to -378-001 (Dion's expedition against Dionysius II)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Plato's first visit to Syracuse is disputed among ancient sources. While some place it in 387 BCE, others place it in 388 BCE, and some even later (386 BCE). The Seventh Letter, which provides Plato's own account, is of disputed authenticity. Modern scholars are divided on the correct date, with 387 BCE being more commonly cited in recent scholarship, but 388 BCE remains a viable alternative. The significance of the Corinthian War in 388 BCE specifically (versus the broader period 395–387 BCE) is not sharply defined; the year is assigned within the broader context of the war's continuation.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 388 BCE is moderate. Greek sources (Xenophon, later compilations by Diodorus and Plutarch) provide context for the Corinthian War and its final stages. However, the record is heavily weighted toward political and military history in Greece and the Eastern Mediterranean. Persian sources are sparse in surviving form. The record reflects the interests and perspectives of Greek elites. Events in other regions (Italy, Egypt, the broader Mediterranean, Asia, Africa) are poorly documented or absent. The year 388 BCE falls within a period of transition, as the Corinthian War moves toward its conclusion; no specific event marks 388 BCE as particularly significant.

graph_edges.note: The continuation of the Corinthian War in 388 BCE leads directly to the King's Peace in 387 BCE.
Plato's first visit to Syracuse (whether in 388 or 387 BCE) leads to his later involvement in Sicilian politics.
Dionysius I's military consolidation in Sicily sets the stage for Dion's later opposition and invasion.

era_context: Classical Greece in the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE), now in its sixth year. The conflict between Sparta and a coalition of Athens, Corinth, Thebes, and other powers continues to exhaust the Greek world. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is beginning to play a more active role in Greek affairs. In China, the Warring States period continues; in Italy, Rome is consolidating power in Latium.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and early Silk Road networks

events.title: Corinthian War continues; Spartan dominance challenged
events.description: The Corinthian War, which began in 395 BCE, continues into its sixth year. The conflict has become a grinding stalemate, with Sparta unable to achieve decisive victory despite its military reputation. The coalition against Sparta—Athens, Corinth, Thebes, and their allies—continues to resist Spartan hegemony. By 389 BCE, the war has exhausted all parties, and the prospect of a negotiated settlement is becoming more likely. The conflict demonstrates the limits of Spartan military power and the resilience of the anti-Spartan coalition.
events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Corinthian War in 389 BCE is well-attested in contemporary sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Conon's naval operations in the Aegean
events.description: Conon, the Athenian general, continues to conduct naval operations in the Aegean Sea with Persian support. Conon, exiled from Athens after the Peloponnesian War, has become a key figure in Persian strategy against Sparta. His naval campaigns aim to weaken Spartan control of the Aegean and support the anti-Spartan coalition. Conon's operations are part of a broader Persian strategy to use Greek conflicts to maintain influence over the Eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Conon's naval operations are attested in contemporary sources, but the precise chronology of individual campaigns in the 390s BCE is uncertain. 389 BCE is assigned within the broader period of his activity.

events.title: Thebes consolidates power in Boeotia
events.description: Thebes, as part of the anti-Spartan coalition in the Corinthian War, consolidates its power in Boeotia. The Theban oligarchy strengthens its control over the Boeotian League and builds up its military forces. This consolidation is part of a broader process that will lead to Thebes's emergence as a major power in the 370s BCE. By 389 BCE, Thebes is becoming a significant player in Greek politics, no longer merely a follower of Spartan or Athenian leadership.
events.certainty_note: Thebes's consolidation of power in Boeotia is a gradual process; 389 BCE is assigned as a point within this broader trajectory. The specific events of 389 BCE are not sharply documented.
cross_references: Leads to -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

events.title: Dionysius I of Syracuse consolidates power in Sicily
events.description: Dionysius I, tyrant of Syracuse, continues to consolidate his power in Sicily. By 389 BCE, he has established himself as the dominant military and political figure in the Greek west. His reign marks a shift toward Hellenistic-style autocracy and away from traditional polis democracy. Dionysius's military innovations and aggressive expansion will define Sicilian politics for the next three decades. His court becomes a center of cultural activity, attracting poets and intellectuals from across the Greek world.
events.certainty_note: Dionysius's consolidation is well-attested in later Greek sources, but precise dating of individual actions within the 405–380 BCE period is uncertain. 389 BCE is assigned as a point within his established rule.
cross_references: Leads to -378-001 (Dion's expedition against Dionysius II)

disconfirming_evidence: The specific events of 389 BCE are not well-documented in surviving sources. The year falls within the broader Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) but no contemporary source marks 389 BCE as a turning point or particularly significant year. The assignment of events to this year is somewhat arbitrary, based on historiographic convention and the broader context of the period. No disconfirming evidence has been identified for the events listed, but their significance specifically to 389 BCE (rather than the broader 390s BCE period) is not established.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 389 BCE is moderate but sparse in terms of specific dated events. Greek sources (Xenophon, later compilations by Diodorus and Plutarch) provide context for the Corinthian War and its progression. However, the record is heavily weighted toward political and military history in Greece and the Eastern Mediterranean. Persian sources are sparse in surviving form. The record reflects the interests and perspectives of Greek elites, particularly Athenian and Spartan sources. Events in other regions (Italy, Egypt, the broader Mediterranean, Asia, Africa) are poorly documented or absent. The year 389 BCE falls within a period of ongoing conflict; no specific event marks it as particularly significant.

graph_edges.note: The continuation of the Corinthian War in 389 BCE leads toward the King's Peace in 387 BCE.
Conon's naval operations contribute to the exhaustion of the combatants and the eventual peace settlement.
Thebes's consolidation of power in Boeotia during the Corinthian War leads to its emergence as a major power and victory at Leuctra.
Dionysius I's consolidation in Sicily sets the stage for Dion's later opposition and invasion.

era_context: The early 4th century BCE in the Mediterranean and Near East. Athens and Sparta remain dominant Greek powers, though both are weakened by the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE). The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II maintains hegemonic control over Asia Minor and the Levant. In Italy, Rome is consolidating power in Latium; the Gallic invasions of central Italy are imminent. In China, the Warring States period is intensifying, with the state of Chu expanding southward.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (Maya and other civilisations; limited synchronisation with Old World chronology)

events.title: Sack of Rome by Gallic forces under Brennus
events.description: Gallic tribes, likely from the region of modern-day France and the Alpine foothills, invaded central Italy and defeated a Roman army at the Allia River (approximately 11 miles north of Rome). The Gauls subsequently sacked and burned much of Rome, though the citadel on the Capitoline Hill held out. This was the first major foreign military incursion into Rome and a profound shock to Roman society. The city was ransomed for gold, and the Gauls eventually withdrew northward. The event prompted significant military and political reforms in Rome and marked a turning point in Roman consciousness regarding external threats.
key_figures: Marcus Manlius Capitolinus (Roman defender of the Capitoline)

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab Urbe Condita), Books 5–6

events.certainty_note: The event is attested in multiple later Greek and Roman sources, but no contemporary Roman written records survive (Rome had limited literacy at this date). Archaeological evidence of burning in the Forum Romanum and surrounding areas is consistent with the tradition, though precise dating to 390 BCE is debated. Some scholars place the sack in 387 or 386 BCE based on chronological recalculation.
cross_references: Follows from broader Gallic migrations into Italy (c. 400–380 BCE)
Led to Roman military reorganisation and the rise of the plebeian class in politics

events.title: Peace of Antalcidas (King's Peace) — aftermath and consolidation
events.region: Eastern Mediterranean and Asia Minor (Persian sphere)
events.description: The Peace of Antalcidas (387 BCE) formally ended the Corinthian War and established Persian hegemony over Greek affairs. By 390 BCE, the terms were being consolidated: Sparta acted as enforcer of Persian interests in Greece, suppressing any coalition that might challenge Persian dominance. This arrangement left Athens weakened and dependent, while Sparta's position as hegemon was secured by Persian backing. The peace effectively froze the balance of power in the Greek world for the next decade.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon was a near-contemporary observer of these events. The peace itself is dated to 387 BCE; by 390 BCE its political consequences were being actively managed.
cross_references: Part of the broader Persian-Greek diplomatic system of the 4th century BCE

events.title: Plato's Academy — early years of philosophical teaching in Athens
events.description: Plato founded his Academy (Akademeia) in the gymnasium of Akademos in northwest Athens around 387 BCE. By 390 BCE, the school was in its early years of operation, attracting students from across the Greek world. The Academy became the centre of philosophical inquiry in the Western tradition, focusing on mathematics, dialectic, and metaphysics. Plato's teaching method and curriculum would influence Greek and later European education for centuries.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of Eminent Philosophers, Book 3

events.certainty_note: The founding date of the Academy is traditionally placed around 387 BCE. Plato's own writings do not explicitly date the Academy's foundation, and later biographical sources vary slightly. The date is accepted by scholarly consensus but carries ±2–3 year uncertainty.
cross_references: Part of the broader intellectual flowering of 4th-century Athens
Precursor to Aristotle's Lyceum (founded c. 335 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Gallic sack of Rome is disputed. While Livy and most Roman sources place it in 390 BCE, some modern scholars (notably based on Polybius's chronological recalculation) argue for 387 or 386 BCE. The discrepancy arises from uncertainty in synchronising Roman and Greek chronologies in this period. Additionally, the scale and destructiveness of the sack as described in later sources may be exaggerated; archaeological evidence suggests significant burning but not total destruction of the city. The narrative of Marcus Manlius Capitolinus defending the Capitoline with the aid of sacred geese is likely legendary embellishment.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 390 BCE is uneven. Greek sources (Xenophon, later compilations by Plutarch and Diodorus) provide relatively detailed accounts of political and military events in the eastern Mediterranean and Greece. Roman sources are problematic: Livy wrote 400 years after the events he describes, drawing on earlier (now lost) annalistic sources and oral tradition. No contemporary Roman written records survive from this period. Archaeological evidence from Rome itself is limited and difficult to date precisely. Asian and African sources are sparse; Chinese sources (Warring States period) are better attested but rarely synchronise with Mediterranean chronology. The historical record is shaped by the survival of Greek and Roman literary traditions; events in regions without written records or whose records did not survive are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: The Gallic sack and the consolidation of the Peace of Antalcidas occurred within a few years of each other, reshaping the Mediterranean balance of power.
Plato's Academy and his philosophical work (including the Republic, composed in the 380s) shaped intellectual discourse for centuries.

era_context: The early 4th century BCE. The Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) is still ongoing or recently concluded. Athens and Sparta remain the dominant Greek powers, though both are constrained by Persian diplomacy. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is at its height. Rome is consolidating control in Latium and facing increasing pressure from Gallic tribes to the north. In China, the Warring States period is intensifying.

events.title: Corinthian War — ongoing military campaigns
events.description: The Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) was a coalition conflict between Sparta and an alliance of Athens, Corinth, Thebes, and Argos, backed by Persian subsidies. By 391 BCE, the war was in its mid-phase, with campaigns focused on control of central Greece and the Peloponnese. The conflict was driven by Spartan hegemonic ambitions and Persian desire to weaken Greek unity. The war would conclude with the Peace of Antalcidas in 387 BCE, which paradoxically strengthened Spartan power while fragmenting the Greek coalition.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon was a near-contemporary observer and participant in some of these events. The Corinthian War is well-attested in Greek sources.
cross_references: Part of the broader pattern of Greek inter-state conflict in the 4th century BCE
Concluded by the Peace of Antalcidas in 387 BCE

events.title: Artaxerxes II's consolidation of Persian hegemony
events.description: By 391 BCE, Artaxerxes II had firmly established Persian dominance over the eastern Mediterranean and Near East. The Persian Empire was at its territorial and economic height, controlling trade routes and extracting tribute from subject peoples. Artaxerxes used Persian wealth and military power to manipulate Greek politics, backing different factions to prevent any single power from challenging Persian hegemony. This period saw the height of Persian cultural influence in the Mediterranean world.
events.certainty_note: Persian hegemony in this period is well-attested in Greek sources, though Persian sources themselves are sparse.
cross_references: Part of the broader Persian imperial system of the 5th–4th centuries BCE

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the Corinthian War's causes and significance are debated among scholars. Some emphasise Persian manipulation; others stress genuine Greek inter-state rivalry. The war's impact on Greek unity is often overstated in older historiography.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 391 BCE is moderate but heavily skewed toward Greek sources. Xenophon provides near-contemporary accounts of military and political events in Greece and the eastern Mediterranean. Persian sources are sparse; what we know of Persian policy comes largely through Greek eyes. Roman sources are minimal; Rome was a minor power at this date and barely noticed by Greek historians. Asian sources (China, India) are not synchronised with Mediterranean chronology in surviving records. The historical record reflects the interests and biases of Greek historians, particularly their focus on inter-state warfare and diplomatic maneuvering.

graph_edges.note: The Corinthian War concluded with the Peace of Antalcidas in 387 BCE.
While the Persian Empire was consolidating hegemony in the eastern Mediterranean, Rome faced the Gallic invasion from the north.

era_context: The early 4th century BCE. The Corinthian War is ongoing. Athens and Sparta are locked in strategic competition, mediated by Persian diplomacy. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is at its zenith. Rome is consolidating power in Latium. In China, the Warring States period is intensifying, with major states competing for hegemony.

events.title: Corinthian War — naval and land campaigns
events.description: The Corinthian War continued with mixed fortunes for both sides. Athenian naval power, rebuilt with Persian support under Conon, challenged Spartan hegemony. Land campaigns in central Greece and the Peloponnese saw shifting alliances and tactical victories without decisive strategic outcomes. The war would drag on until the Peace of Antalcidas in 387 BCE, which paradoxically favoured Sparta despite the coalition's initial advantages.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon provides detailed accounts of the Corinthian War, though his narrative is sometimes fragmentary and his chronology for individual years is not always precise.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)

events.title: Isocrates' rhetorical school — early teaching in Athens
events.description: Isocrates, the renowned Athenian orator and educator, was active in teaching rhetoric and philosophy in Athens during the early 4th century BCE. By 392 BCE, he had established himself as a leading intellectual figure, though his formal school (the Isocratean school) may not have been formally organised until slightly later. Isocrates' emphasis on rhetoric, moral education, and Panhellenism would profoundly influence Greek intellectual life and later Roman education.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of Eminent Philosophers

events.certainty_note: Isocrates' biographical dates are traditionally given as 436–338 BCE. His teaching activity in the early 4th century is attested, but the precise date of his school's formal establishment is uncertain.
cross_references: Contemporary with Plato's Academy (founded c. 387 BCE)
Part of the intellectual ferment of 4th-century Athens

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the precise chronology of individual campaigns in the Corinthian War is debated; Xenophon's account is sometimes vague about year-to-year developments.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 392 BCE is sparse and heavily dependent on Xenophon's Hellenica, which is the primary source for Greek history in this period. Xenophon was a near-contemporary observer but not always precise in dating individual events. Later compilations (Plutarch, Diodorus) draw on earlier sources now lost. Roman sources are minimal. Asian sources are not synchronised with Mediterranean chronology. The historical record is shaped by the survival of Greek literary sources and reflects Greek interests and perspectives.

graph_edges.note: The Corinthian War campaigns of 392 BCE were part of the broader conflict that concluded with the Peace of Antalcidas.

era_context: The early 4th century BCE. The Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) is in its mid-phase. Athens and Sparta are locked in strategic competition. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is at its height. Rome is consolidating power in Latium and facing pressure from Gallic tribes. In China, the Warring States period is intensifying.

events.title: Corinthian War — continued military operations
events.description: The Corinthian War continued with campaigns in central Greece and the Peloponnese. The coalition of Athens, Corinth, Thebes, and Argos, backed by Persian subsidies, maintained pressure on Spartan hegemony. Agesilaus II led Spartan forces in defensive and offensive operations. The war remained inconclusive, with neither side able to achieve decisive victory. Persian diplomacy continued to manipulate the conflict to prevent any single Greek power from dominating.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon provides accounts of the Corinthian War, but his chronology for individual years is sometimes imprecise.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 393 BCE is sparse. Xenophon's Hellenica is the primary source, but it does not provide year-by-year detail for all campaigns. The historical record is heavily skewed toward Greek sources and reflects Greek interests. Roman, Asian, and African sources are minimal or absent.

graph_edges.note: The Corinthian War campaigns of 393 BCE were part of the broader conflict that concluded with the Peace of Antalcidas.

era_context: The early 4th century BCE. The Corinthian War (395–387 BCE) is in its early-to-mid phase. Athens, Corinth, Thebes, and Argos are allied against Spartan hegemony, backed by Persian subsidies. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is at its height. Rome is consolidating power in Latium. In China, the Warring States period is intensifying.

events.title: Battle of Corinth (Corinthian War)
events.description: A major engagement in the Corinthian War, the Battle of Corinth (or battles near Corinth) in 394 BCE saw the coalition forces (Athens, Corinth, Thebes, Argos) clash with Spartan forces under Agesilaus II. The battle was tactically inconclusive but strategically significant: it demonstrated that the coalition could challenge Spartan military dominance. The engagement was part of a broader campaign to contain Spartan power and protect Corinth from Spartan encroachment. The battle's outcome encouraged the coalition to continue the war despite Persian pressure for a negotiated settlement.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon provides an account of battles near Corinth in 394 BCE, though the precise date and details of individual engagements are sometimes unclear. The battle is attested in multiple sources but with varying details.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)
Followed by continued coalition campaigns against Sparta

events.title: Battle of Cnidus — Athenian naval victory
events.description: The Battle of Cnidus (394 BCE) was a major naval engagement in which an Athenian fleet under Conon, backed by Persian subsidies and support, defeated the Spartan navy. This victory was a turning point in the Corinthian War: it restored Athenian naval power after decades of Spartan dominance and demonstrated the effectiveness of Persian-backed coalition warfare. The victory allowed Athens to rebuild its maritime empire and challenged Spartan hegemony in the Aegean. The battle was one of the most significant naval engagements of the early 4th century BCE.
key_figures: Pharnabazus II (Persian satrap, ally of Athens)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Cnidus is well-attested in multiple Greek sources. Xenophon provides a near-contemporary account. The date of 394 BCE is accepted by scholarly consensus.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)
Led to the restoration of Athenian naval power
Contributed to the eventual Peace of Antalcidas (387 BCE)

events.title: Conon's reconstruction of the Long Walls of Athens
events.description: Following the Athenian naval victory at Cnidus in 394 BCE, Conon used Persian subsidies to begin the reconstruction of the Long Walls that connected Athens to its port at Piraeus. These walls had been destroyed by Sparta after the Peloponnesian War (404 BCE). Their reconstruction was a symbol of Athenian resurgence and a strategic necessity for maintaining control of maritime trade and military power. The reconstruction took several years but was largely completed by the early 380s BCE.
key_figures: Artaxerxes II (Persian Great King, financial backer)
