events.certainty_note: The reconstruction of the Long Walls is attested in Greek sources, though the precise chronology of the project is debated. Xenophon indicates that work began after the Battle of Cnidus (394 BCE), but completion took several years.
cross_references: Follows from the Battle of Cnidus (394 BCE)
Part of the broader Athenian resurgence in the early 4th century BCE

events.title: Artaxerxes II's diplomatic maneuvering in Greece
events.description: By 394 BCE, Artaxerxes II was actively using Persian wealth and diplomacy to manipulate Greek politics. While backing the coalition against Sparta (through subsidies to Athens and other allies), Persia also maintained diplomatic channels with Sparta. This strategy of playing Greek powers against each other allowed Persia to prevent any single Greek power from challenging Persian hegemony. The Persian strategy would eventually lead to the Peace of Antalcidas (387 BCE), which formalised Persian dominance over Greek affairs.
events.certainty_note: Persian diplomatic activity in Greece is well-attested in Greek sources, particularly Xenophon.
cross_references: Part of the broader Persian imperial strategy in the 4th century BCE
Led to the Peace of Antalcidas (387 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Cnidus is sometimes dated to 394 BCE and sometimes to 393 BCE in different sources. Xenophon's chronology is not always precise. Some modern scholars debate the exact date, though 394 BCE is more commonly accepted. The significance of the battle in reversing Spartan naval dominance is generally accepted, but the extent to which it changed the strategic balance of the Corinthian War is debated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 394 BCE is moderate, primarily from Xenophon's Hellenica, which provides near-contemporary accounts of Greek military and political events. Later compilations (Plutarch, Diodorus) draw on earlier sources now lost. Persian sources are sparse; what we know of Persian policy comes largely through Greek eyes. Roman sources are minimal; Rome was a minor power at this date. Asian sources are not synchronised with Mediterranean chronology. The historical record reflects the interests and biases of Greek historians, particularly their focus on inter-state warfare and the exploits of famous generals.

graph_edges.note: The Athenian naval victory at Cnidus enabled Conon to secure Persian funding for the reconstruction of the Long Walls.
The Battle of Cnidus and subsequent Athenian resurgence contributed to the diplomatic negotiations that led to the Peace of Antalcidas.
Persian diplomatic maneuvering in the 390s BCE culminated in the Peace of Antalcidas, which formalised Persian hegemony over Greece.

era_context: The beginning of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE), a major conflict in Classical Greece. Sparta, at the height of its military power, faces a coalition of Greek city-states (Athens, Corinth, Thebes, Argos) funded by Persian gold. This war marks a turning point: Spartan hegemony begins to decline, and the balance of power in Greece shifts. In the Near East, Artaxerxes II is successfully using Greek factional conflicts to prevent any single power from threatening Persian interests. The broader Mediterranean continues to see Greek expansion and trade.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior of Persian Empire beyond court records and Greek accounts

events.title: Outbreak of the Corinthian War
events.description: The Corinthian War begins when Corinth and Thebes, supported by Persian gold and encouraged by Athens, attack Spartan allies. The immediate cause is a dispute over Corinthian territory, but the underlying cause is Persian strategy to contain Spartan power by funding anti-Spartan coalitions. The war involves major battles and naval engagements over the next twelve years. Sparta, despite its military superiority, finds itself fighting on multiple fronts and unable to achieve decisive victory. The war marks the beginning of the end of Spartan hegemony in Greece.
key_figures: Lysander (Spartan general, recently deceased but his legacy influential)
events.certainty_note: The outbreak of the Corinthian War in 395 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. Xenophon provides a detailed account of the war's beginning. The date 395 BCE is accepted by modern scholarship with high confidence.
cross_references: Follows from Persian strategy to contain Sparta (-397 to -396)

events.title: Battle of Haliartus and Spartan setback
events.description: Early in the Corinthian War, Spartan forces under Agesilaus II engage Theban and Athenian forces at Haliartus in Boeotia. The battle is tactically indecisive but strategically significant: Spartan forces are unable to achieve a decisive victory, and the coalition forces demonstrate that they can stand against Spartan hoplites. The battle sets the tone for the war: despite Spartan military superiority, the coalition's superior numbers and Persian financial support enable them to sustain the conflict.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Haliartus is attested in Xenophon and other ancient sources. The date 395 BCE is accepted by modern scholarship. However, the exact tactical outcome and significance of the battle are debated by modern scholars.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (-395 to -387)
Demonstrates the coalition's ability to resist Spartan military dominance

events.title: Conon's naval operations and Persian support
events.description: Conon, commanding a Persian-funded naval force, continues operations against Spartan naval dominance. His fleet is being built up with Persian resources to challenge Spartan control of the Aegean. These naval operations are part of the broader Persian strategy to contain Sparta and support the anti-Spartan coalition. Conon's naval activities will culminate in the Battle of Cnidus (394 BCE), where he will achieve a major victory over the Spartan fleet.
events.certainty_note: Conon's naval operations in 395 BCE are attested in Xenophon and later sources. However, the precise dating of specific naval engagements is uncertain. Ancient sources sometimes compress events across multiple years.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (-395 to -387)

disconfirming_evidence: The causes of the Corinthian War are debated by modern scholars. While Persian gold clearly supported anti-Spartan coalitions, the degree to which Persia actively engineered the war versus opportunistically exploited existing Greek tensions is disputed. Some scholars emphasize the role of local Greek grievances and factional conflicts, while others stress Persian agency. Additionally, the extent of Spartan military superiority is debated: while Sparta won many battles, it was unable to achieve decisive victory, suggesting that the coalition's superior numbers and resources were more significant than traditional accounts suggest. The Battle of Haliartus is sometimes described as a Spartan victory, while other sources describe it as indecisive or even a coalition success.
historiographic_note: Sources for 395 BCE are more abundant than for the preceding years, primarily because the Corinthian War is a major event in Classical Greek history. Xenophon's Hellenica is the primary narrative source, and it provides detailed accounts of military campaigns and battles. However, Xenophon was a Spartan sympathizer, and his account may reflect bias in favor of Spartan actions. Persian sources are largely absent from the surviving record, though inscriptions and later Greek accounts of Persian actions exist. The year 395 BCE marks a turning point in Greek history: the beginning of the end of Spartan hegemony and the rise of Persian influence in Greek affairs. The historical record for this year is relatively rich compared to the preceding years, but it is still heavily weighted toward Greek (especially Athenian and Spartan) perspectives.

graph_edges.note: The Corinthian War continues with major naval and land battles
Battle of Haliartus is part of the broader Corinthian War
Conon's naval operations culminate in the Battle of Cnidus
The Corinthian War leads to the Peace of Antalcidas

era_context: Mid-390s BCE. Sparta is at the height of its military power but facing increasing diplomatic isolation as Persian gold flows to rival Greek city-states. Athens is rebuilding its naval and economic strength. Thebes is emerging as a regional power. In the Near East, Artaxerxes II is consolidating his empire and using Greek factional conflicts to prevent any single power from threatening Persian interests. The broader Mediterranean world continues to see Greek expansion and trade.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior of Persian Empire beyond court records

events.title: Agesilaus II's Anatolian campaign reaches its peak; Persian counter-offensive begins
events.description: Agesilaus II, commanding Spartan forces in Asia Minor, achieves his greatest military successes, defeating Persian forces and local satraps in several engagements. However, Artaxerxes II, recognizing the threat, personally takes command of a large Persian army to confront Agesilaus. The two armies approach each other, but a decisive battle does not occur in 396 BCE. Instead, Artaxerxes' presence and the mobilization of Persian resources signal a shift in strategy: rather than defeating Sparta militarily in Asia, Persia will use diplomacy and gold to turn Greek city-states against Sparta.
key_figures: Tissaphernes (Persian satrap, recently executed but his successor continues his policies)
events.certainty_note: Xenophon provides a detailed account of Agesilaus' campaigns, but the precise chronology of events in 396 BCE versus 397 BCE is unclear. The year 396 BCE is sometimes cited as the beginning of Agesilaus' campaign, while other sources place it in 395 BCE. Modern scholars debate the exact dating.
cross_references: Part of Spartan hegemony period (-404 to -371)

events.title: Athenian naval reconstruction and economic recovery
events.description: Athens, recovering from the devastation of the Peloponnesian War and the subsequent oligarchic period (404–403 BCE), is rebuilding its naval power and commercial networks. The city's economy is strengthening through trade, and its population is recovering. This economic and military recovery is partly enabled by Persian gold flowing into Athens as part of Artaxerxes II's strategy to contain Sparta. The Athenian navy, once the dominant force in the Mediterranean, is being reconstructed and will play a crucial role in the coming Corinthian War.
events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of Athenian economic recovery in the 390s BCE is well-attested. However, the precise dating of specific economic developments to 396 BCE is uncertain. Ancient sources do not always provide detailed economic data for this period.
cross_references: Follows from Athenian recovery after the Peloponnesian War (-404)
Leads to Athenian participation in the Corinthian War (-395 to -387)

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of Agesilaus' Anatolian campaign is disputed among modern scholars. Some place the beginning of his campaign in 396 BCE, while others argue it began in 395 BCE or even 397 BCE. Xenophon's account is the primary source, but he does not always provide precise annual dating. Additionally, the extent of Agesilaus' military success is debated: while he won battles, he did not achieve lasting territorial gains, and Persian resources ultimately proved superior. Regarding Athenian recovery, the extent and pace of economic reconstruction in the 390s BCE is debated by modern scholars, with some arguing that recovery was slower and more uneven than traditional accounts suggest.
historiographic_note: Sources for 396 BCE are sparse and primarily Greek in origin. Xenophon's Hellenica remains the main narrative source, but it is not always precise in dating. Persian sources are largely absent from the surviving record. The year 396 BCE is a transitional year with few clearly datable events; it falls between the more dramatic events of 399 BCE (Socrates' trial) and 395 BCE (Corinthian War). The historical record for this year is heavily dependent on inference from broader patterns rather than specific documented events. The dating of Agesilaus' campaign is particularly uncertain, with ancient sources sometimes conflating events across multiple years.

graph_edges.note: Agesilaus' campaigns and Persian counter-strategy lead to the Corinthian War
Athenian economic recovery enables participation in the Corinthian War

era_context: Mid-390s BCE. Sparta remains the dominant military power in Greece, but Persian diplomacy is actively working to undermine Spartan hegemony by supporting rival Greek city-states. Athens is recovering economically and politically but remains wary of Spartan power. In the Near East, Artaxerxes II is consolidating control after the rebellion of Cyrus the Younger. The broader Mediterranean continues to see Greek expansion and trade.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior of Persian Empire beyond court records and Greek accounts

events.title: Agesilaus II continues Anatolian campaigns; Persian counter-strategy
events.description: Agesilaus II continues his military operations in Asia Minor, achieving tactical victories but failing to dislodge Persian control. Artaxerxes II, recognizing that direct military confrontation with Sparta is costly, shifts strategy: he begins secretly funding Greek city-states (particularly Athens, Corinth, and Thebes) to create a coalition against Sparta. This diplomatic maneuver will culminate in the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE). The year 397 BCE marks the transition from Spartan military dominance to a period of Greek factional conflict orchestrated partly by Persian gold.
events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of Agesilaus' campaigns and Persian counter-strategy is well-attested. However, the precise chronology of events in 397 BCE versus 396 BCE is unclear in ancient sources. Xenophon's account is the primary source, but he does not always provide precise annual dating.
cross_references: Part of Spartan hegemony period (-404 to -371)
Directly leads to Corinthian War (-395 to -387)

events.title: Conon's naval operations and Persian support
events.description: Conon, an Athenian general and naval commander, enters Persian service under Artaxerxes II. He is tasked with building a naval force to counter Spartan naval dominance and support Persian interests in the Eastern Mediterranean. Conon's operations in this period are part of the broader Persian strategy to contain Sparta. His naval activities will culminate in the Battle of Cnidus (394 BCE), where he will achieve a major victory over the Spartan fleet.
events.certainty_note: Conon's entry into Persian service and his naval operations are attested in Xenophon and later sources. However, the precise dating of his activities in 397 BCE versus 396 BCE is uncertain. Ancient sources sometimes compress events across multiple years.
cross_references: Part of broader Persian strategy against Sparta

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of Agesilaus' campaigns and Conon's entry into Persian service is debated. Some scholars place key events in 396 BCE rather than 397 BCE. Additionally, the extent of Persian 'orchestration' of the Corinthian War is disputed: while Persian gold clearly supported anti-Spartan coalitions, the degree to which Persia actively engineered the war versus opportunistically exploited existing Greek tensions is debated by modern scholars. Xenophon's account may overstate Persian agency.
historiographic_note: Sources for 397 BCE are sparse and primarily Greek in origin. Xenophon's Hellenica remains the main narrative source, but it is not always precise in dating. Persian sources are largely absent from the surviving record. The year 397 BCE is a transitional year with few clearly datable events; it falls between the more dramatic events of 399 BCE (Socrates' trial) and 395 BCE (Corinthian War). The historical record for this year is heavily dependent on inference from broader patterns rather than specific documented events.

graph_edges.note: Persian strategy of funding anti-Spartan coalitions leads to the Corinthian War
Conon's naval operations culminate in the Battle of Cnidus

era_context: The year following Socrates' execution. Athens is in political and intellectual ferment; the city is recovering from the Peloponnesian War but remains fractious. Sparta is the dominant military power in Greece but faces growing resistance from other city-states and Persian interference. In the Near East, Artaxerxes II is consolidating his empire. The broader Mediterranean world sees continued Greek colonization and trade expansion.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of the Persian Empire beyond court records

events.title: Agesilaus II's Anatolian campaign and Persian response
events.description: Spartan King Agesilaus II, having been sent to Asia Minor to support Greek cities against Persian control, continues military operations in Ionia and Lydia. He achieves initial successes against Persian forces and local satraps. However, Persian King Artaxerxes II, alarmed by Spartan expansion, begins coordinating with Greek city-states (particularly Athens and Corinth) to contain Spartan power. This marks the beginning of a strategic shift that will lead to the Corinthian War.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon provides a detailed account of Agesilaus' campaigns, but the precise chronology of events in 398 BCE versus 397 BCE is somewhat unclear. Ancient sources sometimes compress or expand campaigns across multiple years.
cross_references: Part of broader Spartan hegemony period (-404 to -371)

events.title: Plato's philosophical activity and early dialogues
events.description: In the year following Socrates' death, Plato is actively engaged in philosophical inquiry and likely beginning to compose early dialogues (Apology, Euthyphro, Laches, Charmides). These works preserve Socratic method and teaching while beginning to develop Platonic philosophy. Plato is also traveling—he may visit Sicily and southern Italy during this period, though the exact chronology of his travels remains debated. His intellectual circle in Athens is forming the nucleus of what will become the Academy.
key_figures: Xenophon (contemporary philosopher, also writing memoirs of Socrates)

sources.name: Plato, early dialogues (Apology, Euthyphro, Laches, Charmides)
Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The chronology of Plato's early life and travels is notoriously uncertain. Ancient biographers (Diogenes Laërtius) provide dates, but these are often contradictory or based on inference. The composition dates of early dialogues are debated by modern scholars; they were likely written in the 390s–380s BCE, but precise dating to 398 BCE is speculative.
cross_references: Leads to founding of the Academy (traditionally -387 or -385)

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of Agesilaus' Anatolian campaigns is disputed. Some sources suggest his campaign began in 396 BCE, not 398 BCE. Xenophon's account is the primary source, but he may have compressed or rearranged events for narrative purposes. Additionally, the extent of Agesilaus' military success is debated: while he won battles, he did not achieve lasting territorial gains, and Persian resources ultimately proved superior. Regarding Plato's early life, the tradition of his travels to Sicily and Egypt in the 390s BCE is questioned by some modern scholars, who argue these may be later biographical embellishments.
historiographic_note: Sources for 398 BCE are heavily weighted toward Greek (especially Athenian and Spartan) perspectives. Xenophon's Hellenica is the primary narrative source for military and political events, but Xenophon was a Spartan sympathizer and his account may reflect bias. Persian sources are largely absent from the surviving record, though inscriptions and later Greek accounts of Persian actions exist. The intellectual and cultural life of Athens is documented primarily through Plato's writings and later biographical traditions, which are often unreliable for precise chronology. The year 398 BCE is less well-documented than 399 BCE (Socrates' trial) or 395 BCE (Corinthian War), making it a transitional year with fewer clearly datable events.

graph_edges.note: Agesilaus' campaigns and Persian response escalate into the Corinthian War
Plato's philosophical development in this period leads to the founding of the Academy

era_context: Late Classical Greece during the final decade of the Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE). Athens and Sparta remain locked in exhausting conflict; Persian influence in Aegean affairs is growing. The Athenian democracy is under strain; intellectual ferment continues with sophists, Socrates, and early Platonic circles active in Athens. In the Near East, the Achaemenid Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II is consolidating power after the rebellion of Cyrus the Younger (401 BCE) is still fresh memory.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese sources exist but rarely cross-referenced with Western chronologies for this period)

events.title: Trial and execution of Socrates
events.description: Socrates, the Athenian philosopher, is tried on charges of impiety (asebeia) and corruption of youth. He is convicted and sentenced to death by hemlock poison. The trial reflects post-war Athenian anxiety, factional tension, and suspicion of intellectuals perceived as undermining democratic values. Socrates' death becomes a foundational moment in Western philosophy and martyrdom narratives. His student Plato witnesses the event and later immortalizes it in dialogues including the Apology, Phaedo, and Crito.

sources.name: Aristophanes, Clouds (earlier, 423 BCE, but documents contemporary hostility to Socrates)

events.certainty_note: The trial and execution are attested in multiple near-contemporary sources (Plato and Xenophon wrote within decades). Ancient chronographers (Diogenes Laërtius) place the event in the archonship of Eucleides (399/8 BCE). Modern scholarship confirms the year as 399 BCE with high confidence.
cross_references: Follows from broader Athenian political instability post-Peloponnesian War (-404)
Influences development of Platonic philosophy and Academy founding (-387 or -385)

events.title: Continued Corinthian War tensions and Persian diplomacy
events.description: In the aftermath of Cyrus the Younger's failed rebellion (401 BCE) and the subsequent Ten Thousand's retreat, Persian King Artaxerxes II consolidates control and begins using Greek factional disputes to maintain influence. Sparta, Athens, Corinth, and Thebes maneuver for advantage; Persian gold flows to various Greek states to prevent any single power from dominating. This period marks the beginning of systematic Persian intervention in Greek affairs that will continue through the 390s and 380s BCE.
key_figures: Lysander (Spartan general, recently deceased but legacy influential)
events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of Persian intervention and Greek factional conflict is well-attested. Specific events in 399 BCE are less clearly delineated; the year falls within a period of ongoing diplomatic maneuvering rather than a single decisive event.
cross_references: Follows from Cyrus the Younger's rebellion (-401)

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Socrates' execution has been debated. While 399 BCE is the traditional date (based on Diogenes Laërtius and ancient chronographers), some modern scholars have proposed alternative dates within a few years of this period. However, the weight of modern scholarship supports 399 BCE. Additionally, the precise charges and motivations behind the trial remain contested: some scholars emphasize political factionalism and post-war scapegoating, while others stress genuine religious concerns about Socratic teaching. The trial was not universally popular even in Athens—Plato's account may reflect his own bias in portraying Socrates as a martyr.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 399 BCE is dominated by Athenian sources, particularly Plato and Xenophon. Both were students of Socrates and had personal stakes in how his trial was remembered. Plato's accounts are philosophically sophisticated but may not be historically precise in detail. Xenophon's Hellenica provides a broader Greek perspective but is less detailed on Athenian internal affairs. Non-Greek sources (Persian, Egyptian) are sparse for this year. The trial of Socrates has been heavily mythologized in Western intellectual tradition, potentially obscuring the specific political and social context of late-5th-century Athens.

graph_edges.note: Socrates' death influences Plato's philosophical project and eventual founding of the Academy
Persian intervention and Greek factional tensions escalate into the Corinthian War
